RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[5-methyl-11-(2-piperidin-1-ylethylamino)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]-N-(2-piperidin-1-ylethyl)acetamide iodide is a disubstituted, dicationic [1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with two 2-piperidin-1-ylethylamine (2-carbon spacer) side chains. The shorter ethylamine spacer alters the distance and relative position between the dicationic centers, influencing the binding geometry and affinity to the DNA helix. This compound is used to study the effect of side chain length on the anticancer and antimicrobial activity of potent nucleic acid binding agents.

RNA information: (CUG)₅₀ is a toxic CUG trinucleotide repeat RNA linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it adopts a hairpin structure with multiple 1×1 UU internal loops (its key RNA Motif) and has the sequence 5'-r(CUG)₅₀-3'.Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1, causing RNA mis-splicing; it’s used as a model to screen ligands (e.g., pentamidine, EBAB) via affinity capillary electrophoresis (ACE) for DM1 therapy .
Small_molecule_information: (2R,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(1R,2R,3S,4R,6S)-4,6-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,5R)-4-[(2R,3R,4R,5S,6S)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-4,5-dihydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxy-5-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, also referred to as Neomycin, is an aminoglycoside antibiotic whose molecular structure is rich in nitrogen (containing amino groups and other nitrogen-based functional groups), and as a natural aminoglycoside it is characterized by multiple cationic amino groups arranged around a polycyclic sugar framework, structurally lacking the specific aromatic stacking or spatial precision of synthetic benzo[g]quinoline derivatives which results in broad activity but little discrimination among different repeat sequences. This structural feature enables it to interact strongly with the (CUG) RNA probe associated with the pathology of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), with its binding arising primarily from electrostatic interactions with the phosphate backbone of nucleic acids that makes it a promiscuous and non-selective RNA binder. In affinity capillary electrophoresis (ACE) experiments, its binding constant to (CUG) RNA is significantly higher than that of pentamidine (PTMD) and 1,2-ethane bis-1-amino-4-benzamidine (EBAB), and functionally it can engage RNA targets but does not achieve the selectivity or therapeutic relevance of designed compounds. Moreover, during screening at a high concentration of 1 mM, it can completely eliminate the electrophoretic peak of the (CUG) RNA probe, demonstrating high affinity. However, it should be noted that Neomycin cannot rescue the mis-splicing of affected genes in DM1, so despite its strong RNA-binding activity, it is difficult to serve as a core therapeutic candidate for DM1, and in relevant studies it serves as a control benchmark to highlight advantages of tailored synthetic scaffolds.

RNA information: The (CUG)₁₀ RNA likely forms an A-form double-stranded helical structure with periodic U-U mismatches, similar to longer CUG repeats. These mismatches create bulges and flexible regions, disrupting canonical base pairing. The RNA's propensity to sequester MBNL1 protein contributes to misregulated alternative splicing, a hallmark of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). The structural instability and unique binding pockets make (CUG)₁₀ an attractive target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: (7S,9S)-9-acetyl-7-[(2R,4S,5S,6S)-4-amino-5-hydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxy-6,9,11-trihydroxy-4-methoxy-8,10-dihydro-7H-tetracene-5,12-dione is the chemical name for, or a very close analogue of, the anthracycline antibiotic Daunorubicin (also known as Daunomycin, also referred to as Daunorubicin Hydrochloride), which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound and a cornerstone medication used primarily in the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), acting as a potent DNA intercalator that can also intercalate into double-stranded RNA including expanded CUG repeats, directly bind to CUG RNA, stabilize its double-stranded conformation and compete with MBNL1 for binding, thereby releasing sequestered MBNL1 to restore normal splicing patterns, and it can improve cardiac dysfunction and survival in a DM1 Drosophila model, demonstrating its potential for treating DM1-related cardiac dysfunction.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Chelerythrine, is a methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid featuring a rigid planar aromatic system and a permanent positive charge, and it is also a natural nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that exhibits significant structural and functional properties when interacting with Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA; it can specifically bind to the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant changes in RNA structure which are highly similar to those when binding to other nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds such as proflavine, indicating that the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA has a broad recognition ability for such compounds, and it has a relatively high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, which indicates that its interaction with RNA is very strong, in addition, it can also activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, which further confirms its functional role in gene regulation, and although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, it, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch; in real-world pharmacology, these structures are notorious for their potent DNA intercalation properties, where they insert themselves between base pairs, disrupting DNA structure and function, this mechanism makes them effective cytotoxic agents with powerful anti-tumor and anti-proliferative activity by interfering with DNA replication and Topoisomerase activity, and it has also been reported as an inhibitor of Multidrug Resistance-associated Proteins (MRPs), offering potential value in overcoming cancer resistance mechanisms.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]-3-(1H-indol-3-yl)propanoic acidThis conjugate combines the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core with Arginine and Tryptophan. Tryptophan, with its indole ring, is the most hydrophobic and strongly fluorescent amino acid. Featuring two strong fluorophores, this compound is highly likely to be designed as a high-sensitivity dual-fluorescence probe for live-cell imaging and molecular interaction studies. Its potential activity involves functioning as a cytotoxic drug precursor while allowing for real-time tracking of its distribution and target binding.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-amine, also referred to as a key hit compound featuring a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core with a p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and a 2-(pyrrolidin-1-yl)ethylamine side chain at the 11-position, contains the 2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethylamine side chain, which combines a short ethylamine spacer with the relatively lipophilic pyrrolidine five-membered ring, and the lipophilicity of the pyrrolidine ring enhances cell membrane permeability; this compound is a nucleic acid binding agent that demonstrated excellent binding affinity and selectivity for the NRAS RNA G-quadruplex and potent, broad-spectrum anti-proliferative activity, could effectively repress NRAS translation by stabilizing the RNA G4 and interfering with DHX36 helicase binding thus leading to G0/G1 cell cycle arrest, and has potential in anticancer, antifungal, and antiprotozoal drug research for studying the impact of short-chain, lipophilic substituents on biological activity.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[[(2R)-2-[methyl-[(2R)-2-[methyl-[(2R)-2-[methyl-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]propanoyl]amino]ethyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-3NMe, is a highly complex organic molecule with DNA-targeting characteristics, where 2H-3NMe carries three N-methyl tertiary amides and showed modest potency, while suffering from very poor proteolytic stability (being fully degraded in stability assays) which limited its application despite retaining measurable in vitro activity, exemplifying how chemical scaffold choice strongly affects stability and suitability for cell-based studies; this compounds structural core is an oligopeptide of four N-methylated (2R)-alanine residues linked by an ethylenediamine bridge, with the high-affinity bis-benzimidazole-butanoyl unit attached to both ends, its N-methylation modification of the peptide backbone enhances metabolic stability and cell membrane penetration, and its bis-benzimidazole moiety is a well-known DNA minor groove binder that interacts with the DNA double helix with high affinity, making it a meticulously engineered highly stable, high-affinity DNA-targeting agent valuable in chemical biology for investigating the mechanism of DNA minor groove binding and serving as a lead structure for novel targeted anti-tumor drugs.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Luteolin (a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects that exhibits antioxidant activity and may influence gene expression), is a naturally occurring flavone compound (distinguished from flavonols like 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one by the lack of a hydroxyl group at C3), with the formula C15H10O6. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and a catechol moiety (3,4-dihydroxyphenyl) on the B ring. Luteolin is widely found in celery, parsley, broccoli, cabbage, and various medicinal herbs. In real-world biological activity, Luteolin demonstrates powerful antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, making it an important active component in traditional herbal medicine and modern supplements. It operates through multiple mechanisms, including free radical scavenging, inhibition of various inflammatory mediators, and modulation of immune cell function. Furthermore, it is extensively studied for its neuroprotective, anti-allergic, and potential anticancer properties, such as by inhibiting tumor cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6: This is a six-repeat control RNA with perfect Watson-Crick base pairing. It is used to evaluate the specificity of compound binding and shows minimal structural perturbation upon ligand interaction, confirming that small molecules do not significantly bind to regular duplex RNA.; The (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6 sequence serves as the extended control duplex. Its repetitive yet perfectly paired structure lacks the distinctive features of disease-repeat RNA, allowing researchers to conclusively prove that compound efficacy is due to specific recognition of the pathological mismatch motif and not general RNA binding.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2,4-dimethylphenyl)quinoline-5-carboxylic acid, also referred to as CP14, is a quinoline-5-carboxylic acid derivative substituted at the 2-position with a 2,4-dimethylphenyl group; CP14 is specifically characterized as a quinoline carboxylic acid derivative with a 3,4-dimethylphenyl group, and its carboxylic acid moiety and dimethyl substitution may have hampered its permeability or binding, resulting in relegating it to a moderate candidate. The combination of the quinoline ring and the carboxylic acid is a common motif in Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs) or tyrosine kinase inhibitors. The carboxylic acid group, negatively charged at physiological pH, is crucial for ionic interaction with the targets active site. The 2,4-dimethylphenyl group increases lipophilicity and contributes to hydrophobic interactions. This molecule is a likely candidate for an anti-inflammatory, analgesic, or anti-tumor (kinase inhibition) agent, with its activity driven by the interaction of the planar aromatic ring and the carboxylate group with the enzyme pocket.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(6,8-dimethylimidazo[1,2-a]pyrazin-2-yl)-7-[(3S)-4-ethyl-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]chromen-2-one, also referred to as C2, is a highly fluorescent coumarin derivative featuring an imidazo[1,2-a]pyrazine group at the 3-position and a chiral, cationic piperazin-4-ium at the 7-position, and it is a non-planar coumarin analog with a 4-ethyl-3-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system carrying a 1 charge; the imidazo[1,2-a]pyrazine core is associated with kinase inhibitory activity, while the coumarin scaffold is a classic fluorophore, and this molecule can recognize the single-G bulge of RNA1, with the binding driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics as confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, this combination results in a molecule with strong fluorescence and a potential biological function; in real applications, 3-(6,8-dimethylimidazo[1,2-a]pyrazin-2-yl)-7-[(3S)-4-ethyl-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]chromen-2-one is likely designed as a fluorescent kinase probe for screening or imaging the activity of specific enzymes in living cells, additionally, it may be investigated as a photosensitizer for photodynamic therapy (PDT) due to its strong light absorption and fluorescence properties.

RNA information: TCT8-4 has two conserved regions (5'-AUACCA-3' and 5'-CCUUGG(C/A)AG-3') . It binds theophylline  in a Mg-dependent way, showing 10,900-fold higher affinity for theophylline than caffeine. It aids in diagnosing diseases like asthma/bronchitis, where theophylline is used as a bronchodilator, by distinguishing theophylline from similar compounds.
Small_molecule_information: 7-methyl-3H-purine-2,6-dione, also referred to as 7-Methylxanthine, is one of the core structures of 7-Methylxanthine and an oxopurine alkaloid in which the hydrogen at N-7 of the xanthine ring is replaced by a methyl group, making it an obligatory intermediate in the plant biosynthetic pathway to caffeine; this compound is found as a plant and mammalian metabolite, and its single methyl substitution abolishes the N-7 proton that the TCT8-4 RNA aptamer uses for high-affinity hydrogen bonding, resulting in much weaker binding to the aptamer than theophylline, which demonstrates that an intact N-7 proton is essential for tight recognition by the evolved RNA sensor, and it is also one of the main metabolites of caffeine in humans and is significant in pharmacokinetic and toxicological studies.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-8-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistin (another isoflavone glycoside in this category is Puerarin, which is found in kudzu root), is a glycoside derivative of Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), known as Genistin, which is an isoflavone glycoside with the formula C21H20O9; Puerarin exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects, improves blood circulation and is studied for cardiovascular and metabolic diseases, while Genistins structure features the Genistein backbone with a glucose molecule (or glucoside moiety, the oxan-2-yl part) attached via a C-C bond at the C8 position, with the specific stereochemistry of the sugar being characteristic of its natural form, and it is the most abundant isoflavone compound found in soybeans, where it predominantly exists in this glycosylated form; in biology, most natural flavonoids exist as glycosides, which typically means they have higher water solubility but lower inherent bioactivity, requiring hydrolysis by gut microbes or intracellular -glucosidases to release the more active aglycone form, Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), thus, Genistin is often considered a prodrug form of Genistein, sharing similar health benefits such as phytoestrogenic, antioxidant, and potential anticancer activities, but with a different pharmacokinetic profile.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol, also referred to as Amodiaquine Di HCl, is a quinoline derivative structured around a quinoline core, with a chloro substitution at the 7-position and an amino link at the 4-position to a benzene ring containing a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group (molecular formula C20H22ClN3O), and it is an antimalarial drug with immunomodulatory effects that shares mechanisms with chloroquine but has higher toxicity risks, limiting its use despite efficacy against resistant Plasmodium strains; this compound possesses a planar conjugated system and multiple functional groups (chloro, amine, phenol, quinoline), functionally, the quinoline core is famous as the scaffold for well-known antimalarial drugs (such as chloroquine), and derivatives are commonly investigated for broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antimalarial, and anti-cancer activities, the presence of the phenolic hydroxyl and amine groups suggests potential for metal complexation or binding to enzyme active sites.

RNA information: GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.; GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-2 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.; GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-3 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.; GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-4 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]-2-(cyclohexylmethoxy)phenyl]ethanamine, also referred to as Compound 5d (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a derivative of 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, where the phenol is substituted with a cyclohexylmethoxy group, the compound was synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki couplings and characterized by methods like NMR and SPR, the cyclohexyl group is a saturated alicyclic ring that can significantly increase the molecules lipophilicity but removes the possibility of - stacking, as a tetracationic polyamine, this increase in lipophilicity may enhance its interaction with lipid-rich environments (such as the interior of cell membranes), functionally, Compound 5d binds to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB with low binding affinity and has corresponding binding specificity relative to control hairpins GGh and TARh, shows higher IC in inhibiting the Bh-Rev interaction and no antiviral activity in cell assays, while such derivatives may be explored for targeting membrane-associated proteins or interfering with lipid metabolism pathways, for instance, as novel antiviral or antifungal agents.

Small_molecule_information: 4-amino-1-[(2R,5S)-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-oxathiolan-5-yl]pyrimidin-2-one, also referred to as Lamivudine (3TC) or its enantiomer, is a synthetic cytidine nucleoside analog where the 3 oxygen atom of the ribose ring is replaced by a sulfur atom, forming a 1,3-oxathiolane sugar ring, molecular formula C8H11N3O3S; it is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) that incorporates into viral DNA, terminating its synthesis, and in terms of real-world activity, Lamivudine is an extremely important antiviral drug, classified as a Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitor (NRTI), which is widely used clinically for the treatment of Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) infection and Hepatitis B Virus (HBV) infection, with its mechanism of action involving being phosphorylated intracellularly to its active triphosphate form, which is then incorporated into the viral DNA chain, causing chain termination and effectively inhibiting the activity of the HIV reverse transcriptase and HBV polymerase.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-[5-methyl-7-[2-(1-methylpiperidin-1-ium-1-yl)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]ethane-1,2-diamine diiodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine and quaternary ammonium salt side chains. The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxy-3-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Rutin, is a naturally occurring flavonoid glycoside found in plants, known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and vasoprotective properties; Rutin is widely used as a dietary supplement and medicinal agent due to its potent antioxidant and free-radical scavenging activities, it stabilizes blood vessels, reduces capillary fragility, may protect against oxidative stress-related diseases, clinically it is known for its Vitamin P activity, primarily used to strengthen capillary walls, reduce capillary permeability, and treat conditions like varicose veins, hemorrhoids, and capillary fragility, it also exhibits anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties, and its poor water solubility limits bioavailability, but derivatives are explored for enhanced delivery.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: [5-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN), is a derivative of riboflavin generated through phosphorylation, which is a coenzyme produced by the phosphorylation of riboflavin; its isoalloxazine ring has a planar aromatic structure, while the phosphate group is flexibly linked to the ring system, and its isoalloxazine tricyclic and ribitol-phosphate skeleton together form a reversible electron-transferring redox center; as the natural ligand of bacterial riboswitches, FMN inserts into the aptamer pocket and induces conformational changes in RNA, causing the expression platform to form a terminator/sequestering loop, and it can also induce RNA structure folding into terminator clips or blocking ribosome binding sites by binding to the SA1 riboswitch upstream of riboflavin synthesis genes and the SA2 riboswitch upstream of transport genes, using its own concentration changes as a signal, which consequently shuts down the transcription and translation of the downstream riboflavin synthesis gene ribB as well as the de novo synthesis and uptake pathways of riboflavin, thereby achieving negative feedback regulation of riboflavin biosynthesis; because it is crucial for bacterial survival and does not exist in mammals, FMN and its riboswitch have become targets for antibiotic design, which can be competitionally occupied by structural analogues such as rosettin and ribocil-C, blocking the supply of riboflavin and forcing bacteria to starve to death.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3-N,3-N,7-N,7-N-tetramethyl-4-nitro-3H-phenothiazine-3,7-diamine, also referred to as the compound with a tricyclic phenothiazine core where a sulfur atom bridges two benzene rings, bearing 3,7-tetramethyldiamino groups and a 4-nitro substituent and exhibiting aromatic, electron-deficient, and polar features, is a nitro-substituted phenothiazine derivative, structurally similar to Methylene Blue; functionally, no links to RNA targets (e.g., pre-miR-21, r(GC)) or related biological activities (e.g., RAN translation inhibition) are reported, leaving its specific roles uncharacterized in the given context, and it is used in reality as a redox indicator, biological stain, antimalarial drug, and a potential therapeutic for inhibiting Tau protein aggregation in Alzheimers disease.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-N-[2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl]-2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated analogue of 4c-5 (indoloquinoline core), is an indolo[3,2-b]quinoline quaternary ammonium salt featuring an N-methylpiperazine side chain, and this compound is the N5-methylated analogue of 4c-5 (indoloquinoline core), the piperazine group is included to optimize pharmacokinetic properties such as solubility and target affinity, it is a highly potent candidate for antitumor research due to its cationic and planar nature, this compound showed notable affinity for the NRQ, it showed moderate anti-proliferative activity with notable activity in A375 cells and was ineffective in HeLa cells at the tested concentration.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-[5-[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]phenyl]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole, also referred to as DB60 (furimidazoline, 2,5-bis-[4-(2-imidazolin-2-yl)phenyl]furan), is a symmetric or near-symmetric structure centered around a biphenyl-linked furan ring, with a 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) group attached to each end. The entire molecule forms a conjugated system consisting of two imidazolinium groups, two phenyl groups, and a furan ring, where the imidazolinium groups are connected to the 2- and 5-positions of the furan ring via the phenyl linkers. Imidazolinium groups are typically weakly basic and can bind to biomacromolecules like DNA or RNA, and such polyaromatic heterocyclic systems often possess fluorescent properties. This compound was originally optimized for anti-Pneumocystis carinii activity but has been repurposed as a selective HIV-1 TAR RNA antagonist, binding the pyrimidine bulge of the TAR hairpin, displacing the Tat protein, blocking Tat-mediated trans-activation, stabilizing TAR structure, and suppressing HIV-1 LTR-driven gene expression without cytotoxicity, illustrating a clear RNA-versus-DNA selectivity switch compared with amidine congeners, and identifying it as a lead structure for developing small-molecule inhibitors targeting the TARTat axis of HIV-1 replication.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(2R,3S,4R,5S)-5-[(1R,2R,3S,5R,6S)-3,5-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3-amino-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]oxy-6-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol;sulfuric acid, also referred to as Paromomycin sulphate, is an aminoglycoside antibiotic existing as a sulfate salt, and Paromomycin sulphate is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, causing misreading of mRNA and inhibiting protein synthesis and is used to treat parasitic and bacterial infections, its core structure, (2S,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(2R,3S,4R,5S)-5-[(1R,2R,3S,5R,6S)-3,5-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3-amino-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]oxy-6-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, is similar to (2R,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(1R,2R,3S,4R,6S)-4,6-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,5R)-4-[(2R,3R,4R,5S,6S)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-4,5-dihydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxy-5-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, consisting of multiple aminosugar units linked via glycosidic bonds, featuring numerous amino and hydroxyl groups, the presence of multiple amino groups makes it strongly polycationic, which is the structural basis for its biological activity, allowing it to specifically bind to the bacterial ribosome and disrupt protein synthesis, its formulation as a sulfate salt is typical for this class of drugs, enhancing its water solubility and stability for clinical applications such as injection or solution preparation, this molecule is highly likely to be a clinically relevant antibiotic, such as a derivative of streptomycin, kanamycin, or tobramycin, employed as a broad-spectrum bactericidal agent primarily against aerobic Gram-negative infections, with caution required due to the risk of ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.

RNA information: SA1 RNA is an FMN riboswitch located upstream of the rib operon in methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Its specific sequence folds into a highly conserved aptamer structure, which can specifically bind to flavin mononucleotide (FMN). When the concentration of FMN increases, SA1 undergoes a conformational transition and forms a terminator hairpin, causing RNA polymerase to terminate transcription prematurely. This thereby shuts down the expression of downstream genes responsible for de novo riboflavin synthesis. The synthetic compound ribocil-C and the natural product roseoflavin competitively occupy the same binding site, mimic the role of FMN, induce conformational changes in SA1, and further block endogenous riboflavin synthesis in bacteria, exerting a bacteriostatic effect.
Small_molecule_information: [5-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN), is a derivative of riboflavin generated through phosphorylation, which is a coenzyme produced by the phosphorylation of riboflavin; its isoalloxazine ring has a planar aromatic structure, while the phosphate group is flexibly linked to the ring system, and its isoalloxazine tricyclic and ribitol-phosphate skeleton together form a reversible electron-transferring redox center; as the natural ligand of bacterial riboswitches, FMN inserts into the aptamer pocket and induces conformational changes in RNA, causing the expression platform to form a terminator/sequestering loop, and it can also induce RNA structure folding into terminator clips or blocking ribosome binding sites by binding to the SA1 riboswitch upstream of riboflavin synthesis genes and the SA2 riboswitch upstream of transport genes, using its own concentration changes as a signal, which consequently shuts down the transcription and translation of the downstream riboflavin synthesis gene ribB as well as the de novo synthesis and uptake pathways of riboflavin, thereby achieving negative feedback regulation of riboflavin biosynthesis; because it is crucial for bacterial survival and does not exist in mammals, FMN and its riboswitch have become targets for antibiotic design, which can be competitionally occupied by structural analogues such as rosettin and ribocil-C, blocking the supply of riboflavin and forcing bacteria to starve to death.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-N-(2-phenylethyl)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-amine chloride This complex quaternary ammonium salt features a rigid, highly conjugated pentacyclic core and a lipophilic N-(2-phenylethyl)amine side chain. The molecules large, positively charged structure and its amphiphilic nature (hydrophilic cation head, hydrophobic phenylethyl tail) suggest strong interactions with biological membranes and negatively charged biopolymers like DNA. Real-world applications for compounds with this skeleton often focus on developing broad-spectrum antimicrobial or antifungal agents that disrupt microbial cell integrity, or as DNA intercalators/Topoisomerase inhibitors in anticancer research. The phenylethyl moiety also hints at potential neuro-modulatory activity.

RNA information: RNA5 contains the GG internal loop for compound 2 binding, but the UU loop for compound 1 has been mutated to a UA base pair. This mutation severely diminishes binding by dimeric compound 3, demonstrating that the UU loop is essential for its high-affinity, bivalent interaction.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-azidoethyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2, is an azide-tagged bis-benzimidazole probe featuring a short two-carbon (ethyl) linker, which is a bis-benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module that Informa predicted and binding studies confirmed selectively targets a 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loop adjacent to the Drosha site in pri-miR-96, while it binds its target RNA motif, it also exhibits high, undesired affinity for an AT-rich DNA hairpin and does not effectively inhibit miR-96 biogenesis in cellular assays when used as a monomer; the shorter linker alters the spatial presentation of the azide group for the subsequent Click Chemistry reaction, and its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for DNA binding and labeling studies, used to investigate the impact of a close-to-core tag on the probes biological performance.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]-2-(trifluoromethyl)phenothiazine, also referred to as Trifluoperazine, a phenothiazine antipsychotic drug that primarily acts as a dopamine antagonist, is based on the phenothiazine tricyclic core, features an electron-withdrawing trifluoromethyl group at the 2-position which enhances its lipophilicity, and has a propyl chain connecting an N-methylpiperazine group at the 10-position nitrogen; structurally, this is characteristic of many classic antipsychotic drugs, and this compound is expected to show potent dopamine receptor antagonist activity, particularly towards the central nervous system D2 receptor, making it potentially useful for treating schizophrenia or for its sedative effects.

RNA information: RNA2 is defined by a 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loop that is adjacent to the Drosha site in the pri-miR-96 structure. It is the specific and high-affinity binding target for compound 2, with a Kd of 1,500 nM, but does not bind compound 1.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 1, is a mono-benzimidazole Click Chemistry probe with tert-butyl and azide groups, which is a benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module previously identified by the Informa platform to avidly bind a 1x1 nucleotide UU internal loop within the Drosha processing site of the pri-miR-96 hairpin precursor, inhibits miR-96 biogenesis and induces apoptosis in breast cancer cells with activity that limits its in vivo utility, shows no detectable binding to DNA, and its real-world activity is dedicated to biological orthogonal labeling experiments for target validation and mechanism elucidation.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

RNA information: NA 4 (Drosha and adjacent 515) is an RNA fragment mimicking the Drosha processing site and its flanking region in pri-miR-515. Its core structural feature is the simultaneous inclusion of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-515 and the adjacent Compound 1-binding motif, and the combination of these two motifs provides conditions for dual-site binding.; RNA 4 (Drosha and adjacent 515) is an RNA fragment mimicking the Drosha processing site and its flanking region in pri-miR-515. Its core structural feature is the simultaneous inclusion of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-515 and the adjacent Compound 1-binding motif, and the combination of these two motifs provides conditions for dual-site binding.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 1 (Targaprimir-515/885), is a complex molecular probe based on the bis-benzimidazole (DNA minor groove binder) scaffold and modified with an azide group (-N) and bulky tert-butyl groups, and it is also an azide-containing monomeric small molecule that serves as an RNA-binding module coupling to peptidomimetic scaffolds via Cu-catalyzed azide-alkyne click chemistry, which binds similarly to Drosha processing site motifs of pri-miR-515 and pri-miR-885 plus an adjacent motif of pri-miR-515 to inhibit both miRNAs biogenesis and acts as a lead for selective dimers like Compound 2 to aid RNA-targeting small molecule optimization; in chemical biology, the azide group is the key functional group for Click Chemistry, its real-world activity is designed as a chemically taggable DNA-binding probe, and it is used to study the biological targets of the bis-benzimidazole core by attaching a reporter molecule (e.g., fluorophore or biotin) to the binding site via a highly specific click reaction.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]-2-(cyclohexylmethoxy)phenyl]ethanamine, also referred to as Compound 5d (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a derivative of 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, where the phenol is substituted with a cyclohexylmethoxy group, the compound was synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki couplings and characterized by methods like NMR and SPR, the cyclohexyl group is a saturated alicyclic ring that can significantly increase the molecules lipophilicity but removes the possibility of - stacking, as a tetracationic polyamine, this increase in lipophilicity may enhance its interaction with lipid-rich environments (such as the interior of cell membranes), functionally, Compound 5d binds to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB with low binding affinity and has corresponding binding specificity relative to control hairpins GGh and TARh, shows higher IC in inhibiting the Bh-Rev interaction and no antiviral activity in cell assays, while such derivatives may be explored for targeting membrane-associated proteins or interfering with lipid metabolism pathways, for instance, as novel antiviral or antifungal agents.

RNA information: The 5′CUG/3′GAC motif is a non-pathogenic RNA internal-loop sequence that forms a 1 × 1 nucleotide U·G wobble when embedded in double-stranded RNA. Although structurally similar to the 5′CUG/3′GUC repeat that drives myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), the single-base change from U to G abolishes high-affinity recognition by MBNL1 and prevents the formation of nuclear foci. Consequently, 5′CUG/3′GAC serves as a “bystander” control in vitro and in cell-based assays to verify that small-molecule ligands or antisense oligonucleotides intended to target the pathogenic 5′CUG/3′GUC repeat do not act through generic U/G- or U/U-loop binding, thereby supporting the development of DM1 therapeutics with improved selectivity.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[6-[2-(3-aminophenyl)-3H-benzimidazol-5-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]aniline, also referred to as Compound H1, is a complex bis-benzimidazole compound (a bis-benzimidazole analog derived from Hoechst 33258) with a structure composed of two benzimidazole units and two meta-aminophenyl units linked covalently, molecular formula C26H21N5; the molecule exhibits a polyheterocyclic structure that is highly conjugated and notably planar, featuring multiple amino and imidazole nitrogen atoms capable of extensive hydrogen bonding, and it retains the core DNA/RNA-binding benzimidazole scaffold of Hoechst 33258, which was rationally designed to target the expanded CUG repeat RNA (r(CUG)exp) that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) and optimized through chemical-similarity screening to gain higher affinity and selectivity for the pathogenic 5CUG/3GUC RNA motif; in terms of real-world activity, this bis-benzimidazole structure is highly analogous to the structural characteristics of known DNA minor groove binders (e.g., Hoechst dyes), it is designed for high-affinity, specific binding to the DNA minor groove particularly in AT-rich regions, and it also has the ability to disrupt the toxic r(CUG)expMBNL1 complex in vitro, correct MBNL1-dependent splicing defects in DM1 patient-derived cells, disperse nuclear foci without global off-target splicing effects, restore a significant portion of the aberrant splicing of Clcn1 and Serca1 transcripts in the HSALR DM1 mouse model with good tolerance, minimal cytotoxicity and no overt microbiota disturbance, thus establishing itself as a lead small-molecule RNA binder that selectively rescues DM1 molecular phenotypes with potential for further development toward DM1 therapeutics, while it is primarily explored as a lead candidate for novel antitumor or antiviral drugs, acting by interfering with DNA replication, repair, and transcription, and also holds potential as a nuclear DNA stain in biological imaging.

Small_molecule_information: 2,6-diamino-N-[3-[2-(methylamino)-1,3-thiazol-4-yl]phenyl]hexanamide, also referred to as N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-lysinamide (1b), is a compound with the molecular formula C16H23N5OS, whose structure links the skeleton of the natural amino acid lysine (2,6-diaminohexanoic acid) via an amide bond to an aniline moiety substituted with a thiazole heterocycleTatN-HIV-1 TAR RNA--1bTatTARHIVTARRNAtRNADNAHIVTatHIV

Small_molecule_information: 4-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)benzenecarbothioamide, also referred to as 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole, is a combination of benzimidazole and carbothioamide structures, and 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole is a heterocyclic small molecule with a benzimidazole core linked to a thiocarbamoyl-substituted phenyl group, the benzimidazole core is often associated with antiparasitic, antifungal, antiviral, and antineoplastic activities, the carbothioamide group may enable the molecule to act as a metal chelator or enzyme inhibitor, the structure suggests the molecule may serve as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting on cell division or specific enzyme systems, unlike myricetin, it lacks reported affinity for CAG-repeat RNAs in HD/SCAs but may interact with structured RNAs via benzimidazole intercalation and thiocarbamoyl H-bonding, it shows no validated activity against polyQ aggregation or mitochondrial dysfunction in HD models, though its benzimidazole motif could potentially target nucleic acid mismatches, making it a speculative scaffold for TRED-focused optimization with further biophysical validation needed. -

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-2-[(1S,2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2R,3R,5S,6R)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-5-hydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, is a member of the aminoglycoside antibiotic class, structurally composed of multiple aminosugar units, including an aminocyclohexyl core linked by glycosidic bonds to other aminopyranose units. Like its congeners, the compound is characterized by multiple primary and secondary amine groups that are protonated and polycationic at physiological pH, a feature essential for its biological function. Its mechanism involves binding to the A-site of the bacterial ribosome (which is equivalent to binding to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes) to disrupt protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and premature termination of protein synthesis, effectively killing the bacteria. Based on its specific arrangement of sugar units and stereochemistry, this compound corresponds to a specific aminoglycoside drug, possibly related to amikacin or netilmicin. These drugs are commonly used clinically as second-line or combination therapy for serious conditions like endocarditis, septicemia, and pulmonary infections caused by susceptible organisms, and are also used to treat Gram-negative infections, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-16-[3-(3-methylpyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene is a typical dicationic structure, consisting of the azonium on the pentacyclic scaffold and the azonium on the 3-methylpyridinium ring. This combination of dual positive charges and a highly lipophilic large scaffold makes it a potent cell membrane permeabilizer. Pyridinium rings are common cationic units. In practical applications, such amphiphilic, highly charged compounds are widely explored for disrupting the integrity of biological membranes, thus exhibiting potent, broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against both bacteria and fungi. It may also be studied as a cellular tracer or fluorescent probe for bioimaging.

Small_molecule_information: 2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-piperidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-amine, also referred to as the derivative with a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core that has the p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and a 2-(piperidin-1-yl)ethyl)amino side chain at the 11-position, has the 2-piperidin-1-ylethylamine side chain, combining the ethylamine spacer with the piperidine six-membered ring, and this compound exhibited a good affinity for the NRQ as well as variable but generally potent anti-proliferative activity across cell lines, Piperidine is a ubiquitous structural unit in pharmaceuticals, optimizing lipophilicity and basicity, Piperidine-substituted quinoline derivatives are explored for antimalarial, anti-HIV, and kinase inhibitor activities, and this compounds design aims to regulate its tissue distribution and receptor affinity through the piperidine side chain.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, the xanthene moiety bears 3,6-dihydroxy groups, which facilitate the formation of the emissive quinoid structure, while the 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro substituents on the benzofuranone ring can significantly modify its fluorescence properties, such as increasing quantum yield or causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

RNA information: The miR-96 precursor( pri-miR-96) is a structured RNA hairpin that is specifically recognized by a benzimidazole small molecule (compound 1) identified via the Inforna pipeline. Binding occurs at the single-stranded Drosha-processing site within the precursor, thereby blocking its cleavage and reducing mature miR-96 production. This selective interaction relieves miR-96-mediated repression of the tumor-suppressor FOXO1 mRNA, restoring cellular FMN/riboflavin-like homeostasis by rebalancing oncogenic miRNA levels and apoptotic control in cancer cells.; The pri-miR-96 transcript features a critical hairpin structure containing a Drosha processing site. Its key functional element is a UU internal loop and a nearby GG internal loop within this hairpin, which serve as the binding site for the small molecule Targaprimir-96. Binding of the molecule to this specific structural pocket inhibits Drosha cleavage, thereby blocking the biogenesis of mature oncogenic miR-96. This inhibition leads to the upregulation of the FOXO1 protein and induces apoptosis in cancer cells, highlighting the direct link between its specific secondary structure and its oncogenic function.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Targaprimir-96, is the parent, non-covalent small molecule designed to bind selectively to the Drosha processing site of pri-miR-96, specifically targeting a UU internal loop within its secondary structure, and by binding to this site, it inhibits the biogenesis of oncogenic mature miR-96, which leads to the upregulation of FOXO1 protein expression and subsequently induces apoptosis in triple-negative breast cancer cells, while showing no significant effect in healthy breast epithelial cells, demonstrating its selective, phenotype-modulating efficacy; this compound is a complex benzimidazole, triazole, and amide derivative, whose structure contains multiple benzimidazole rings, piperazine groups, tert-butyl groups, and a lengthy peptidic mimicry chain, resulting in an extremely high molecular weight and high polar surface area, it presents as a large molecule with extreme complexity and multiple potential hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, and its structure is indicative of a potential Anti-cancer Agent or Antiparasitic Agent (Macromolecular Ligand), often designed to interact with DNA or essential biomacromolecular targets (such as protein kinases or enzymes), leveraging its benzimidazole core and polyamine linkers.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[5-methyl-11-(2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]-N-(2-morpholin-4-ylethyl)acetamide iodide. This is a cationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative, featuring an N-methylated polycyclic system with a 2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino substituent at position 11 and a 2-morpholin-4-ylethylacetamide chain at position 7. This nitrogen-rich cationic structure provides high affinity for nucleic acids. Its structure suggests potential application in anti-tumor and broad-spectrum anti-infective drug development. It works by interfering with crucial cellular proliferation mechanisms such as cell cycle regulation and DNA replication/repair processes, and its dual-inhibition capability gives it a practical advantage in overcoming drug resistance.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 1-cyclopropyl-7-(4-ethylpiperazin-1-yl)-6-fluoro-4-oxoquinoline-3-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Enrofloxacin, is a classic Fluoroquinolone antibiotic with N1-cyclopropyl, C6-fluoro, and C7-piperazine substituents (similar to Ciprofloxacin) that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, preventing DNA replication, and is used clinically as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial for infections including those of the respiratory and urinary tracts.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[(4-chlorophenyl)-phenylmethyl]-4-methyl-1,4-diazepane, also referred to as Homochlorcyclizine (1-(4-chlorobenzhydryl)-4-methylhomopiperazine), is a derivative of 1,4-diazepane, bearing a (4-chlorophenyl)phenylmethyl group at the N1 position, a common motif found in established antihistamines (e.g., Cetirizine, Chlorcyclizine) and calcium channel blockers, which is a first-generation antihistamine of the diphenylmethane class originally marketed for allergic rhinitis and motion sickness through blockade of histamine H1 receptors, has been re-profiled as a modest inhibitor of HIV-1 replication as it unexpectedly binds to the internal loop of the HIV-1 Rev response element (RRE) subdomain IIB RNA and displaces the Rev3450 arginine-rich helix, thereby disrupting formation of the RevRRE ribonucleoprotein export complex, showing weak inhibition of full-length RRERev complex assembly in electrophoretic mobility-shift assays and antiviral activity against HIV-1 with no measurable cytotoxicity at relevant concentrations, and although less potent than its congener cyproheptadine, it defines a diphenylmethane antihistamine scaffold that specifically recognizes RNA loop IIB nucleotides and may serve as a starting point for developing RevRRE-targeting antivirals, with its potential activity also likely as a Histamine H1 receptor antagonist (antiallergic) or a calcium channel modulator for potential cardiovascular applications.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6: This RNA contains six consecutive CAG repeats with AA mismatches, forming multiple internal loops. It is a key target for small molecules due to its structural similarity to pathogenic expanded repeats. It shows high affinity and selective binding to compounds such as pyridocoumarin derivatives, leading to conformational changes and thermal stabilization.; The (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6 RNA contains multiple consecutive A-A mismatch loops. This multi-loop structure enhances conformational stability and ligand binding avidity, more accurately mimicking the expanded toxic repeats found in patient-derived RNA and providing a robust platform for biophysical testing.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2-fluorophenyl)-3-methylquinoline, also referred to as CP13, is a fluorine-containing aromatic heterocyclic compound. Its structure uses the quinoline core, with a 2-fluorophenyl substituent at the 2-position and a methyl group at the 3-position. The molecular formula is CHFN. The compound possesses a planar conjugated system, and the fluorine atom alters the molecules electron distribution. Its optimal structure allows for strong -stacking and specific interactions with the C4A5G6 triad, resulting in effective RNA stabilization and powerful neuroprotective effects in cells, establishing it as the most promising lead. In terms of biological function, quinoline compounds generally possess diverse biological activities, including antibacterial, antifungal, antimalarial, anthelmintic, antispasmodic, cardiotonic, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic effects, and the introduction of a fluorine atom is typically used to enhance the compounds metabolic stability or improve its membrane permeability.

RNA information: r(CUG)₈ is an RNA fragment with 8 consecutive CUG repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 8 5′CUG/3′GUC internal loops, U·U mismatches, and optional terminal GC clamps (for reduced "breathing effects"), more stable than shorter CUG repeats. Functionally, it lacks pathogenic long (CUG)ₙ toxicity but binds MBNL1 with higher affinity than r(CUG)₆, has abundant small-molecule binding sites, and serves as an in vitro tool for studying MBNL1-(CUG) interactions and DM1 therapeutic screening.
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing.
