Small_molecule_information: (2S)-5-hydroxy-2-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxyphenyl)-7-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxy-2,3-dihydrochromen-4-one, also referred to as Hesperidin, is a flavonoid glycoside with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects commonly found in citrus fruits, and is an extremely complex natural product flavonoid O-diglycoside with a dihydroflavone (flavanone) core substituted at the 7-position with a diglycoside unit (likely involving glucose and rhamnose); the numerous phenolic hydroxyl groups and the bulky glycoside moiety are the primary drivers of its activity, and in practice, such compounds are key functional components in herbal medicines, demonstrating potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and free radical scavenging capabilities, while due to its glycosidic structure, it typically has high water solubility and specific oral bioavailability (via gut microbial hydrolysis), and it is widely studied for cardiovascular benefits as well as cardioprotective, antiviral, and hepatoprotective effects, making it a focal point in natural product research.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-amino-4-(3-aminopropoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one, also referred to as Phenylquinolone 2a (a quinolone derivative lacking the C-3 carboxylic group, with a phenyl ring attached at the C-2 position and a mono-O-propyl amine side chain (-NH-(CH)-NH) at the para position of the phenyl ring), has a side chain that is a 3-aminopropoxy group, terminating in a primary amine, and Phenylquinolone 2as amine side chain lacks a nitrogen-containing heterocycle which likely prevents optimal protonation state or spatial orientation, resulting in reduced binding efficiency to TAR RNA and limited activity, while the overall molecule has two primary amine groups (on the side chain and the phenyl ring of 2-[3-amino-4-(3-aminopropoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one, and a mono-O-propyl amine side chain with amine groups on Phenylquinolone 2a), giving it dibasic properties, the introduction of primary amines allows it to act as a hydrogen bond donor, enhancing interaction with targets, and features minimal steric hindrance, functionally, this structure is often explored as a novel receptor ligand, particularly for binding certain G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), with potential application in the regulation of anti-inflammatory or neurotransmitter systems, and Phenylquinolone 2a can inhibit the formation of the Tat/TAR complex in vitro with moderate activity that is weaker than other derivatives in the series.

Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]-2-(trifluoromethyl)phenothiazine, also referred to as Trifluoperazine, a phenothiazine antipsychotic drug that primarily acts as a dopamine antagonist, is based on the phenothiazine tricyclic core, features an electron-withdrawing trifluoromethyl group at the 2-position which enhances its lipophilicity, and has a propyl chain connecting an N-methylpiperazine group at the 10-position nitrogen; structurally, this is characteristic of many classic antipsychotic drugs, and this compound is expected to show potent dopamine receptor antagonist activity, particularly towards the central nervous system D2 receptor, making it potentially useful for treating schizophrenia or for its sedative effects.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-piperazin-4-ium-1-yl-3-quinolin-6-ylchromen-2-one, also referred to as C38, is a coumarin cationic derivative with the molecular formula C22H20N3O2, which is non-planar and carries a 1 charge, featuring a piperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system. It contains the positively charged piperazin-4-ium group at position 7 and a quinolin (quinolin-6-yl) ring at position 3, and it recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1, driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand. The quinoline scaffold is the core structure of many traditional drugs (e.g., chloroquine, quinine), lending the molecule potential anti-malarial, anti-inflammatory, and antibacterial activity. The highly conjugated aromatic system of this molecule suggests strong fluorescence emission capabilities. The positive charge and planar structure indicate its potential as a DNA or mitochondrial targeting agent, accumulating in specific organelles via electrostatic attraction and aromatic stacking. This compound could be developed as an anti-cancer drug with antiproliferative activity or as a specific biological tracer.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: 5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazol-9-ol, also referred to as Compound 1 (a potent but non-selective 1a-derivative) and Compound 1e (a bis-benzimidazole derivative that closely resembles 1a in its 9-hydroxy-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazolium core but lacks the cationic piperidinium side chain, carrying instead a neutral 2-hydroxyethyl tail), is a tetracyclic aromatic heterocyclic compound based on the pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole scaffold, which forms the core of the well-known ellipticine alkaloid: the molecule is substituted with methyl groups at positions 5 and 11, and a hydroxyl group at position 9, and its planar conjugated system gives it fluorescence properties, with the molecular formula C19H16N2O; Compound 1 effectively inhibits RAN translation but also modestly affects canonical translation, indicating some off-target effects that limit its therapeutic potential, while Compound 1e has reduced affinity for the pathogenic 5CGG/3GGC internal loops of the r(CGG)exp RNA hairpin and weakened ability to disrupt the toxic r(CGG)12DGCR8 complex, leading to attenuated cellular efficacy (restoring partial wild-type SMN2 and Bcl-x pre-mRNA splicing in FXTAS-model COS-7 cells and achieving a modest reduction in the number of r(CGG)exp nuclear foci at a specific concentration) but retaining low cytotoxicity and good aqueous solubility, making it a useful negative-control probe for dissecting structureactivity relationships and validating the requirement of a permanent positive charge in next-generation ligands targeting CGG-repeat disorders; this compound and its derivatives are potent Topoisomerase II inhibitors and DNA intercalators, exhibiting strong anticancer activity.

Small_molecule_information: N-[4-[6-(furan-2-carbonylamino)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenyl]furan-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Benzimidazole 16 (2015), is a complex aromatic heterocyclic compound centered around a benzimidazole core, a planar heterocycle formed by the fusion of a benzene and an imidazole ring, and Benzimidazole 16 (2015) is a 2-phenyl-benzimidazole-4-carboxamide derivative engineered to selectively bind the toxic r(CUG)109 RNA expansion that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1, the benzimidazole is substituted at position 2 with a 4-aminophenyl-furan-2-carboxamide side chain, and at position 6 with a furan-2-carbonylamino side chain, the entire molecule is composed of multiple planar aromatic rings (benzimidazole, phenyl, and two furan rings) linked by amide bonds, creating an extended, rigid, and highly conjugated system, the amide and benzimidazole moieties provide multiple hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, this scaffold is often designed as a protein kinase inhibitor or receptor ligand, benzimidazole compounds are a privileged scaffold in medicinal chemistry due to their diverse biological activities, frequently exhibiting antiparasitic, antiviral, and anticancer properties, the activity of this specific compound is likely related to its potential as a molecular probe or as a lead compound in the search for new drugs, by mimicking the recognition surface of MBNL1, it engages the periodic UU internal loops of the CUG hairpin with notable potency in TR-FRET displacement, displaces MBNL1 from ribonuclear foci, and restores correct alternative splicing of the cTNT mini-gene in DM1 cell models without altering DMPK mRNA abundance or affecting off-target RNAs such as human A-site rRNA or r(GGCC)4, its drug-like physicochemical profilemoderate molecular weight (352 Da), cLogP 3.9, topological polar surface area 111 , and negligible cytotoxicity up to 100 Mdemonstrates that uncharged, benzimidazole-based small molecules can selectively drug structured, repetitive RNA motifs and rescue disease-relevant splicing defects. Small_molecule_information: 1-ethyl-7-methyl-4-oxo-1,8-naphthyridine-3-carboxylic acid, also referred to as nalidixic acid, is a 1,8-naphthyridone structure characteristic of Quinolone antibiotics, which is a quinolone class of antibacterial drugs characterized by a naphthyridine ring attached to a carboxyl group; this class of drugs is a widely used broad-spectrum antibiotic in clinical practice, treating bacterial infections by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase, and while it has a strong binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, it may not trigger biological function as it does not induce gene expression.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 7-[(1,1-dimethylpiperidin-1-ium-4-yl)methoxy]-5-methyl-N-(2-piperidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative, featuring a 1,1-dimethylpiperidin-1-ium group at position 7 and a 2-piperidin-1-ylethyl chain at position 11. The rigid polycyclic structure and dual positive charges make it a typical DNA/RNA interacting agent. In real-world applications, this class of dicationic compounds is often used as fluorescent probes or cell staining agents due to their intense fluorescence upon binding to nucleic acids. As a potential drug, it is investigated for its action against multidrug-resistant bacteria, possibly by disrupting the bacterial cell membrane or inhibiting key enzymes like FtsZ.

RNA information: RPPH1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) that can form a non-canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by only two G-tetrads, which distinguishes it from canonical rG4s that typically consist of three or more tetrads. This structural configuration contributes to its role in transcriptional regulation. Functionally, RPPH1 has been identified as a regulatory RNA molecule, and its rG4 structure can be stabilized or induced by specific small molecule ligands like **PhenDC3 and PDS. In vitro analyses, including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays, demonstrate that the RPPH1 rG4 structure causes approximately 13% stalling efficiency in potassium-containing buffers, confirming its ability to form a stable secondary structure that can impede enzymatic activity. The integrity of this rG4 structure is crucial for its biological function, particularly in the context of gene expression regulation.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(2-aminoethoxy)-2-N,6-N-bis[4-(2-aminoethoxy)naphthalen-2-yl]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide, also referred to as PhenDC3, is a large, structurally symmetrical bisquinolinium G4 ligand (a pyridodicarboxamide derivative) with the molecular formula C37H37N5O5, centered on a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core; this core is linked via two amide bonds to two identical naphthyl units, which are functionalized with aminoethoxy chains, and its structure further enables chemical derivatization (e.g., PhenDC3-alk for bioorthogonal manipulations). The molecule features multiple aromatic rings (pyridine and naphthyl) providing a hydrophobic, planar surface that allows it to bind rG4s via stacking on G-quartets, and four primary amine groups (-O-CH2CH2NH2) contributing to hydrophilicity and multiple positive charges; this combination of hydrophobicity and multiple positive charges is characteristic of amphiphilic molecules, often designed as DNA minor groove binders or transmembrane transport vectors, and it can interact with DNA bases via - stacking interactions while electrostatically attracting the negatively charged phosphate backbone. Functionally, it acts as a molecular chaperone, inducing folding of canonical (3-quartet, e.g., TERRA) and non-canonical (2-quartet, e.g., XIST) rG4s, stabilizes rG4s and impedes reverse transcriptase by promoting rG4 formation, aiding G4 biology studies, and consequently, this compound has potential as an anti-tumor or antiviral drug by interfering with the replication or transcription of nucleic acids.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(E)-[10-[(E)-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-ylhydrazinylidene)methyl]anthracen-9-yl]methylideneamino]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-amine, also referred to as Bisantrene, is a complex anthracene derivative with known antitumor relevance, where the anthracene ring (three linearly fused benzene rings) is substituted at the 9- and 10-positions with hydrazone/imine-linked 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) groups; anthracene is a well-known fluorescent chromophore, making this compound highly likely to exhibit strong fluorescence, and the molecule contains two imidazolinium groups and a conjugated hydrazone-imine structure, elements that facilitate binding to biomolecules like DNA, consistent with its use as a DNA-intercalating topoisomerase IIrelated agent, and such compounds are typically designed as DNA-binding fluorescent probes or potential anticancer agents

Small_molecule_information: N-(7,8-difluoro-5-methyl-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)-N,N-dimethylethane-1,2-diamine iodide, this compound is an indolo[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

The potential activity is antineoplastic, utilizing the imidazole ring of Histidine for interaction. In reality, the inclusion of Histidine often targets the acidic tumor microenvironment, as the side chain becomes protonated in acidic conditions, which can enhance cellular uptake or specific binding within tumor tissue.

Small_molecule_information: (2S)-5-hydroxy-2-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxyphenyl)-7-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxy-2,3-dihydrochromen-4-one, also referred to as Hesperidin, is a flavonoid glycoside with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects commonly found in citrus fruits, and is an extremely complex natural product flavonoid O-diglycoside with a dihydroflavone (flavanone) core substituted at the 7-position with a diglycoside unit (likely involving glucose and rhamnose); the numerous phenolic hydroxyl groups and the bulky glycoside moiety are the primary drivers of its activity, and in practice, such compounds are key functional components in herbal medicines, demonstrating potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and free radical scavenging capabilities, while due to its glycosidic structure, it typically has high water solubility and specific oral bioavailability (via gut microbial hydrolysis), and it is widely studied for cardiovascular benefits as well as cardioprotective, antiviral, and hepatoprotective effects, making it a focal point in natural product research.

RNA information: No loop (loopless RNA), also known as 5'CAG/3'CUG and 5'CAG/3'GUC, is an RNA with a hairpin structure. Structurally, its stem has fully complementary base pairs (no mismatches/deletions) and no internal loops, showing high conformational stability without inter-strand "breathing effect" as a simplified dsRNA model. Functionally, it serves as a key experimental control to distinguish small molecules’ specific/non-specific binding to RNA, with no direct link to specific diseases.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide;dibromide, also referred to as PyDH4, is a bis-cationic Schiff base dye that contains a central pyridine core with lateral 1-ethylquinolinium-6-yl groups, differing from an analogue by having a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core instead of a pyrimidine; this substitution of the central scaffold slightly alters the molecules electronic distribution and spatial geometry, affecting the overall conjugation and properties, and the compound displayed moderate G4-RNA binding but had no significant effect on EBNA1 expression, while in real-world chemical biology, this subtle change in the core is a strategic approach to tune the dyes excitation/emission wavelength and its selectivity towards different nucleic acid structures, and it would still be classified as a nucleic acid fluorescent probe, but with properties optimized for specific imaging or detection applications.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-[5-methyl-7-[2-(1-methylpiperidin-1-ium-1-yl)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]propane-1,3-diamine diiodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine and quaternary ammonium salt side chains. The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-benzoyl-9-chloro-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 10, is a derivative of the complex chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold, characterized by the 3-amino, 2-benzoyl, and 4-phenyl substituents. It features a chloro atom at position 9 (and overall contains a chlorine atom) and displayed significant binding affinity for CAG RNA, with effectiveness in gel shift and PCR stop assays indicating strong interaction with target RNA; the molecule is a highly rigid, planar polycyclic compound with multiple potential hydrogen bonding sites and stacking regions, and the chloro substitution serves to fine-tune the molecules physicochemical properties and steric hindrance. This fused polycyclic structure is an important template in anti-cancer drug design, with its pharmacological mechanism often hypothesized to involve DNA/RNA interference or inhibition of key enzymes regulating the cell cycle, and the chemical profile of this compound makes it a candidate for research as a cytotoxic agent or anti-proliferative drug.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-chlorophenyl)propyl]-7,8-dimethylbenzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione, also referred to as BRX1675, is a synthetic flavin analogue and a synthetic analog of FMN (flavin mononucleotide) whose structural design is optimized based on BRX1555, with the 7,8-dimethylbenzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione scaffold as its core: the ribitol side chain of riboflavin is replaced by a propyl chain containing a 4-chlorophenyl group, with the introduction of halogen substituents (such as chlorine atoms) to enhance binding ability and selectivity with the FMN riboswitch, significantly increasing the molecules hydrophobicity, with the molecular formula CHClNO; this structural modification not only improves the molecules binding affinity but also enhances its selectivity across different bacterial strains, and BRX1675 exhibits high binding efficiency, which has been proven to effectively induce conformational changes in the riboswitch through SHAPE chemical probing technology and transcription termination assays, further confirming its ability to bind to the riboswitch, and the selectivity of BRX1675 among different bacterial strains indicates that targeted action against specific bacteria can be achieved through chemical modifications, and these research findings provide an important structural basis for the development of more effective antibacterial drugs, and are particularly significant in exploring how chemical modifications can enhance antibacterial activity and selectivity, while such structural modifications often lead to flavin analogues being used to study enzyme inhibitors or as photosensitizers, potentially possessing applications in photodynamic therapy.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-8-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidin-1-ium-4-one, also referred to as SMSM41, is based on the pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidin-4-one core, this compound is a dicationic salt: the pyridopyrimidine ring is quaternized at N1, carrying one positive charge, and the (3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl group at C8 carries the second positive charge, it is substituted with the 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl group at C3, and SMSM41 is a non-planar coumarin analog with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, carrying a 1 charge, which recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, the dicationic nature and high nitrogen content make 3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-8-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidin-1-ium-4-one a potent candidate for DNA/RNA binding agents or broad-spectrum antimicrobial drugs, the dual positive charges may enhance its ability to overcome bacterial or cancerous cell defenses and accumulate within the cell nucleus.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

RNA information: RPPH1 is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) that can form a non-canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by only two G-tetrads, which distinguishes it from canonical rG4s that typically consist of three or more tetrads. This structural configuration contributes to its role in transcriptional regulation. Functionally, RPPH1 has been identified as a regulatory RNA molecule, and its rG4 structure can be stabilized or induced by specific small molecule ligands like **PhenDC3 and PDS. In vitro analyses, including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays, demonstrate that the RPPH1 rG4 structure causes approximately 13% stalling efficiency in potassium-containing buffers, confirming its ability to form a stable secondary structure that can impede enzymatic activity. The integrity of this rG4 structure is crucial for its biological function, particularly in the context of gene expression regulation.
Small_molecule_information: 4,16-difluoro-8,12,20-trimethyl-8-aza-20-azoniapentacyclo[11.7.1.0{2,7}.0{9,21}.0{14,19}]henicosa-1(20),2(7),3,5,9(21),10,12,14(19),15,17-decaene, also referred to as PhenDC3, a bisquinolinium G4 ligand (a pyridodicarboxamide derivative), is a highly complex and rigid pentacyclic fused nitrogen-containing heterocyclic quaternary ammonium salt that binds rG4s via stacking on G-quartets, whose structure enables chemical derivatization (e.g., PhenDC3-alk for bioorthogonal manipulations), functionally acts as a molecular chaperone inducing folding of canonical (3-quartet, e.g., TERRA) and non-canonical (2-quartet, e.g., XIST) rG4s, stabilizes rG4s and impedes reverse transcriptase by promoting rG4 formation to aid G4 biology studies, the chemical name clearly outlines a twenty-one-carbon skeleton composed of multiple bridged and fused rings, the two fluorine atoms, strategically positioned at the 4 and 16 positions, are common bioisosteres used to enhance metabolic stability, lipophilicity, and binding affinity to biological targets, the presence of a quaternary ammonium group imparts a positive charge at physiological pH, enabling strong electrostatic interactions with negatively charged biomolecules such as DNA or acidic residues on proteins, and this rigid polycyclic scaffold and its specific three-dimensional structure suggest its potential application as a novel anti-tumor or antiviral agent, possibly functioning as a topoisomerase inhibitor or DNA intercalator in real-world drug discovery.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3S,5R)-5-(2-amino-6,8-dioxo-1,7-dihydropurin-9-yl)-3-hydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methoxy-hydroxyphosphoryl] phosphono hydrogen phosphate, also referred to as 8-Oxo-2-deoxyguanosine-5-triphosphate (8-oxo-dGTP), is a deoxyribonucleoside diphosphate analog and a non-azaaromatic oxidized nucleotide derivative, featuring a purine base structurally similar to Uric Acid or 8-hydroxyguanine (2-amino-6,8-dioxopurine) and a 2-deoxyribose sugar (deoxyoxolan-2-yl, lacking the 3-OH) linked to a pyrophosphate group at the C5 position (its 2-deoxyguanosine core is modified by a ketone group (O) at the 8-position and three phosphate groups at the 5-end, and it lacks planar azaaromatic features), this structure is analogous to deoxyxanthosine diphosphate (dXDP), a potential erroneous substrate for DNA synthesis, it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, consequently, the compound is researched for its potential to interfere with DNA synthesis, serving as a possible anticancer or antiviral agent by inhibiting key enzymes like ribonucleotide reductase or DNA polymerase, and its core significance lies in being a marker of oxidative DNA damageif incorporated into DNA, it causes mutations, so cells use enzymes to degrade it and prevent genomic instability.

Small_molecule_information: 1-[(4-methylanilino)methyl]naphthalen-2-ol, also referred to as CP3, is a naphthol-containing compound whose structure is based on the naphthol parent skeleton: a benzene ring is fused with another benzene ring to form the naphthalene skeleton, which is substituted with a hydroxyl group at position 2 and a (4-methylanilino)methyl group at position 1, and this amphiphilic structure built around a naphthalene core with a hydroxyl group connected via a methylene bridge to an amino group and a 4-methylphenyl ring allowed moderate RNA binding, but it was not among the most potent compounds in biological assays; in terms of biological function, naphthalenes and their derivatives generally exhibit diverse biological activities such as antifungal, anti-inflammatory, or are used as fluorescent probes.

Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-9-methyl-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 6, is a chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one compound substituted with a methyl group at the 9-position and a 4-methoxybenzoyl group at the 2-position, and with both methyl and methoxy substitutions, this compound demonstrated enhanced binding affinity and selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA and was among the more effective analogs in reducing protein aggregation; the methyl substituent is primarily used to fine-tune the molecules lipophilicity and steric bulk, the complex fused scaffold and multiple aromatic substituents (phenyl and methoxybenzoyl) create a large hydrophobic surface, giving it the potential to bind tightly to protein targets, in practical applications, this type of molecule is often explored for targeted therapy, for instance, as a tyrosine kinase inhibitor to block signaling pathways in cancer cells, and its potential antitumor activity is a main focus of research for these complex heterocyclic compounds.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: 6-(pyrrolidin-1-ylmethyl)-2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine, also referred to as 4s, features a 2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine bicyclic core, which is a fused system of a pyridine and a saturated pyrrole ring, possessing weak basicity, and bears either a pyrrolidin-1-ylmethyl or a secondary aliphatic amine (piperazine) substituent. The pyrrolidinylmethyl or piperazine group is a common basic amine appendage used to optimize pharmacokinetic properties or to engage in critical interactions with drug targets. Heterocyclic amines containing pyridine and amine substituents are known for their broad activities, often researched as histamine receptor antagonists, GPCR ligands, or kinase inhibitors, and this compound also aids in studying structure-activity relationships for pre-miR-21 maturation inhibitors, guiding optimization of more potent analogs, with potential applications in antiallergic or CNS-related diseases.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-N,3-N,7-N,7-N-tetramethyl-4-nitro-3H-phenothiazine-3,7-diamine, also referred to as the compound with a tricyclic phenothiazine core where a sulfur atom bridges two benzene rings, bearing 3,7-tetramethyldiamino groups and a 4-nitro substituent and exhibiting aromatic, electron-deficient, and polar features, is a nitro-substituted phenothiazine derivative, structurally similar to Methylene Blue; functionally, no links to RNA targets (e.g., pre-miR-21, r(GC)) or related biological activities (e.g., RAN translation inhibition) are reported, leaving its specific roles uncharacterized in the given context, and it is used in reality as a redox indicator, biological stain, antimalarial drug, and a potential therapeutic for inhibiting Tau protein aggregation in Alzheimers disease.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: bis(2-acetamidoacetic acid);4-[2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)iminohydrazinyl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Diminazine, is a complex salt molecule consisting of a large cationic formamidine hydrazonebased diamidine species and two 2-acetamidoacetic acid anions, and the ,-diamidine core connected through a biphenyl and hydrazone bridge corresponds to the class of aromatic diamidine compounds used as antiprotozoal agents that exert activity by binding to the minor groove of DNA, with such cationic diamidines commonly displaying antiprotozoal, antifungal, antiviral, anticancer, or broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties.

RNA information: Human A-site rRNA is a conserved structural motif within the small ribosomal subunit that forms the decoding center during mRNA translation. It can specifically recognize and bind to the anticodon stem-loop of aminoacyl-tRNA, ensuring accurate codon–anticodon pairing and maintaining translational fidelity. This RNA element also serves as a binding platform for aminoglycoside antibiotics, whose occupation disrupts decoding fidelity and leads to mis-translation, making the A-site rRNA a key regulator of protein synthesis homeostasis in human cells.

as proteins, enzymes, or DNA, the combination of a chlorine-substituted phenyl group and a benzimidazole is a common motif in drug design, often employed to enhance the molecules biological activity, this compound is investigated for potential anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, or antitumor properties, likely acting by inhibiting specific enzymes or protein-protein interactions, by mimicking the Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

Small_molecule_information: Trisodium;5-oxo-1-(4-sulfonatophenyl)-4-[(4-sulfonatophenyl)diazenyl]-4H-pyrazole-3-carboxylate, also referred to as Tartrazine (and known as the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110)), is the chemical name for the food additive Sunset Yellow FCF (E110), a synthetic azo dye with the formula C16H10N2Na3O7[(2R,3S,4S,5R,6R)-2-[(2R,3S,4S,5S,6S)-2-[(1R,2S)-3-[[(2R,3S,4S)-5-[[(2S,3R)-1-[2-[4-[4-[3-[4-[[2-[[2-[[2-[acetyl(propyl)amino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-prop-2-ynylamino]acetyl]amino]butylamino]propylcarbamoyl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]-1,3-thiazol-2-yl]ethylamino]-3-hydroxy-1-oxobutan-2-yl]amino]-3-hydroxy-4-methyl-5-oxopentan-2-yl]amino]-2-[[6-amino-2-[(1S)-3-amino-1-[[(2S)-2,3-diamino-3-oxopropyl]amino]-3-oxopropyl]-5-methylpyrimidine-4-carbonyl]amino]-1-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)-3-oxopropoxy]-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-3-yl]oxy-3,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-4-yl]carbamate, and Tartrazine is a synthetic yellow azo dye used as a food coloring agent while primarily an industrial colorant, its structure features a pyrazolone core linked by an azo bond (NN) to two benzene rings, both substituted with sodium sulfonate groups, and exists as a trisodium salt, the azo structure imparts a vibrant color, in real-world use, Sunset Yellow is widely employed as an orange-yellow coloring agent in foods, beverages, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics, such as orange juice, sodas, candies, and ice cream, to enhance visual appeal, as a common food colorant, its use is strictly regulated globally to ensure safety within prescribed limits, despite some controversy regarding its potential to affect behavior in sensitive individuals, particularly children.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. Small_molecule_information: [4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-(furan-2-yl)methanone, also referred to as Compound 14 (a DPQ (compound 1) derivative with the piperazines secondary amine modified where Rfuran), is a compound that is synthesized via one-step coupling, binds the influenza A RNA promoter (causing U27 imino proton line broadening) and has improved anti-influenza activity but is toxic to MDCK-HA cells; this compound features a furan-2-carbonyl (furan-2-ylmethanone) side chain, the furan ring, a five-membered aromatic heterocycle, introduces unique steric and electronic properties and an additional hydrogen bond acceptor site, furan derivatives are commonly found in compounds with anti-inflammatory and anticancer activities, while the main activity remains 1-adrenergic receptor antagonism, the furan ring acts as an auxiliary pharmacophore, allowing for more specific interactions within the binding pocket, potentially optimizing its selectivity for a particular 1-receptor subtype.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]-3-(1H-imidazol-5-yl)propanoyl]amino]acetic acidA more complex polypeptide structure involving Arginine, Histidine, and Glycine linked to the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core. The increased complexity suggests it may be designed to mimic natural peptide substrates for enhanced affinity and selectivity towards specific biological targets. Its potential activity is as a peptide inhibitor (e.g., for proteases or GPCRs) which, combined with the cores cytotoxicity, suggests an application in highly targeted cancer therapy.

RNA information: GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as NMR, SPR, and ITC. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.; GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-2 RRE subdomain IIB and contains a G:G pairing structure. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and distinguishes non-specific binding from specific interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, also referred to as Compound 5b (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a structurally simpler tetracationic polyamine (compared to 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2,6-dimethylphenyl]phenol and 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2,6-dimethylphenyl]-2-phenylmethoxyphenyl]ethanamine), containing four primary amine groups and a phenol, and is synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki couplings, characterized by NMR, SPR, and ITC, its rigid scaffold features only methoxy substituents, resulting in less steric hindrance, functionally, due to its high symmetry and lower steric hindrance, it may possess a higher nucleic acid binding affinity or more effective gene transfection ability than more complex derivatives, while it also binds to HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIBs hairpin RNA (Bh) with high-affinity binding detected by ITC, induces chemical shift changes in Bhs loop, has certain specificity ratios for related nucleic acid structures, shows limited DNA selectivity, has high IC in inhibiting Bh-Rev interaction, and no antiviral activity in cell assays, in practical applications, such compounds are often used as probes to study the biological effects of polyamine analogs on cell proliferation and apoptosis, as polyamine metabolism in cellulo is closely linked to cancer cell growth.

RNA information: The r(CAG)₁₀ sequence serves as a comparative motif to test ligand selectivity against repeat RNAs with A–A mismatches instead of U–U mismatches. Structurally, it forms A-form helical RNA with alternating positive and negative electrostatic stripes similar to CUG repeats, leading to comparable overall architectures. Functionally, r(CAG)₁₀ is not associated with myotonic dystrophy but provides a stringent control for assessing molecular recognition. Because many proteins and synthetic ligands show similar affinity toward both CUG and CAG repeats, this sequence demonstrates the challenges of engineering true sequence selectivity in RNA-targeted small-molecule design.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-2,7-bis[[(2S)-6-amino-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]hexanoyl]amino]-N-[(2S)-1-(2-aminoethylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N-[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N,N-dipropyloct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 9 (2012), is a highly complex asymmetric peptidomimetic bearing the benzo[g]quinoline moiety; while benzo[g]quinoline is a necessary aromatic element for relevant interactions, DCC 9 (2012) has an altered peptide framework that disrupts the orientation required for strong and selective recognition, resulting in a weaker interaction profile, limited ability to discriminate among target sequences, and minimal biological effects, which confirms that benzo[g]quinoline is necessary but not sufficient and that correct positioning within the molecular scaffold is equally critical, highlighting its role as a negative control and supporting evidence for the structureactivity relationship developed across the compound series. The molecules core eight-carbon chain contains a trans (E) double bond, and its most significant feature is the unequal length of the polyamine chains attached to the two termini: one is a 3-aminopropylamine (three carbons) and the other is a 2-aminoethylamine (two carbons); this asymmetric polyamine structure is designed to potentially cause differences in binding affinity or mode with biological targets, and the molecule is symmetrically linked to benzo[g]quinoline-lysine derivatives on both sides, integrating DNA intercalation capability with polycationic electrostatic binding, with this design combining asymmetry and dual-targeting groups aiming to investigate how regulating asymmetric conformation can enhance the selectivity and multi-action efficacy against specific biological targets, holding potential application in multi-target anti-cancer therapy.

RNA information: miR-544 precursor is a hairpin-structured non-coding RNA that is specifically processed by the RNase Dicer to generate the mature oncogenic microRNA miR-544. In hypoxic breast-tumor cells it acts as a metabolic sensor: its transcription is driven by HIF-1α, and after Dicer cleavage the resulting miR-544 directly represses mTOR and ATM, thereby redirecting glucose flux and promoting survival under low oxygen. Chemical or genetic blockade of this precursor-to-mature transition derepresses mTOR, sensitizes triple-negative breast-cancer cells to hypoxia-induced apoptosis and 5-fluorouracil, and arrests tumor growth in vivo, demonstrating that the precursor’s controlled processing is essential for tumor adaptation to hypoxic stress.
Small_molecule_information: 1,8-diamino-3,6-dipyrrolidin-1-yl-2,7-naphthyridine-4-carbonitrile, also referred to as Compound 1 (2) (N-(3-(1H-imidazol-1-yl)propyl)-2-(pyrrolidin-1-yl)quinolin-4-amine), is a compound featuring a highly substituted 2,7-naphthyridine scaffold with multiple amino and pyrrolidine substituents, and a cyano group at position 4, with the formula C19H23N7, and Compound 1 (2), identified via Inforna, selectively binds miR-544 precursors Dicer site and r(CUG)exp U loops, naphthyridine cores are often investigated as fluorescent dyes and DNA binders, the high polarity and multiple hydrogen bond sites suggest good water solubility and potential biological activity, it is likely designed as a DNA minor groove binder, aiming to interfere with replication and transcription processes through non-covalent binding, thus having prospects in antimicrobial and anticancer research, it inhibits miR-544 biogenesis, derepresses mTOR, disrupts ATMmTOR axis (induces TNBC apoptosis to a notable extent, with significant efficacy vs antagomirs), displaces MBNL1, improves DM1 splicing/translation, and disperses foci, a single i.p. injection of a low dose abolishes MDA-MB-231 tumor growth (low toxicity), acting as an RNA-directed probe for TNBC and DM1. RNADNA

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-amino-1-[(2R,5S)-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-oxathiolan-5-yl]pyrimidin-2-one, also referred to as Lamivudine (3TC) or its enantiomer, is a synthetic cytidine nucleoside analog where the 3 oxygen atom of the ribose ring is replaced by a sulfur atom, forming a 1,3-oxathiolane sugar ring, molecular formula C8H11N3O3S; it is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) that incorporates into viral DNA, terminating its synthesis, and in terms of real-world activity, Lamivudine is an extremely important antiviral drug, classified as a Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitor (NRTI), which is widely used clinically for the treatment of Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) infection and Hepatitis B Virus (HBV) infection, with its mechanism of action involving being phosphorylated intracellularly to its active triphosphate form, which is then incorporated into the viral DNA chain, causing chain termination and effectively inhibiting the activity of the HIV reverse transcriptase and HBV polymerase.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-4-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide diiodide, also referred to as PhenDH1, is a dicationic aromatic heterocyclic compound based on a phenanthroline backbone, where two 1-methylpyridinium groups are attached via imine bonds at positions 2 and 9 of the 1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide core (1,10-1-)with the formula C28H26I2N8O2, this molecule belongs to a class of dicationic DNA binding small molecules, the structure provides strong nucleic acid affinity and good cell permeability, it also showed strong G4 stabilization and high FID affinity but induced GAr-independent increases in EBNA1, indicating non-specific effects, and it is studied for its potential antimicrobial and anticancer activities, primarily through electrostatic and intercalative binding to DNA, thereby disrupting the integrity of genetic material and leading to cell apoptosis, which is relevant for the development of new antibiotics.

RNA information: TERRA (Telomeric Repeat-containing RNA) is a long non-coding RNA essential for telomere maintenance. It forms a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by three G-tetrads, which is more stable than non-canonical two-tetrad rG4s. In vitro studies using reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays demonstrate that TERRA's rG4 causes significant stalling (~40%) under potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, confirming its stable formation. This structural integrity is crucial for its role in telomere length regulation and heterochromatin formation. Notably, ligands like PhenDC3 and PDS act as effective molecular chaperones, significantly inducing and stabilizing the TERRA rG4 structure even under unfavorable ionic conditions (Li⁺), highlighting its dynamic nature and functional importance in genome stability.; TERRA (telomeric repeat-containing RNA) is a well-characterized RNA that forms stable G-quadruplex structures, with its sequence featuring repeated G-tracts . It binds selectively to RGB-1, showing a 1:1.2 binding stoichiometry and inducing a Tₘ increase of the RNA G-quadruplex upon binding. As a 5'UTR insert in reporter mRNAs, it mediates translation repression by RGB-1 in vitro and HEK293 cells, serving as a model for studying RNA G-quadruplex-related translational regulation.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(2-aminoethoxy)-2-N,6-N-bis[4-(2-aminoethoxy)naphthalen-2-yl]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide, also referred to as PhenDC3, is a large, structurally symmetrical bisquinolinium G4 ligand (a pyridodicarboxamide derivative) with the molecular formula C37H37N5O5, centered on a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core; this core is linked via two amide bonds to two identical naphthyl units, which are functionalized with aminoethoxy chains, and its structure further enables chemical derivatization (e.g., PhenDC3-alk for bioorthogonal manipulations). The molecule features multiple aromatic rings (pyridine and naphthyl) providing a hydrophobic, planar surface that allows it to bind rG4s via stacking on G-quartets, and four primary amine groups (-O-CH2CH2NH2) contributing to hydrophilicity and multiple positive charges; this combination of hydrophobicity and multiple positive charges is characteristic of amphiphilic molecules, often designed as DNA minor groove binders or transmembrane transport vectors, and it can interact with DNA bases via - stacking interactions while electrostatically attracting the negatively charged phosphate backbone. Functionally, it acts as a molecular chaperone, inducing folding of canonical (3-quartet, e.g., TERRA) and non-canonical (2-quartet, e.g., XIST) rG4s, stabilizes rG4s and impedes reverse transcriptase by promoting rG4 formation, aiding G4 biology studies, and consequently, this compound has potential as an anti-tumor or antiviral drug by interfering with the replication or transcription of nucleic acids.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-methylbenzenesulfonate;(2Z)-3-methyl-2-[(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylidene]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as Thiazole Orange, is an organic salt containing quinolinium and benzothiazole heterocyclic rings that intercalates into DNA and RNA, commonly used in flow cytometry and molecular biology for detecting nucleic acids; its structure is based on the quinoline and benzothiazole parent skeletons: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, and a benzene ring is fused with a thiazole ring to form the benzothiazole skeleton, with these two parent nuclei connected by a methine group. In terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically functions as dyes, photosensitizers, or exhibits specific biological enzyme inhibitory activities (Benzothiazoles are important in therapeutic chemistry).

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: N-[4-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-5-(4-chlorophenyl)furan-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Benzimidazole 17 (2015), is another complex aromatic heterocycle centered on a RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.

This strategy of coupling a cytotoxic aromatic heterocycle with natural amino acids or peptide segments is used in medicinal chemistry to improve the compounds bioavailability and targeting. RNA information: xpt-pbuX riboswitch is an guanine-sensing RNA regulatory element located in the 5' untranslated region of Bacillus subtilis mRNA, consisting of a conserved aptamer domain and an expression platform. Its aptamer folds into a three-way connection structure composed of three stems, P1, P2, and P3, and two rings, L2 and L3. The core area precisely recognizes and closely binds guanine through seven hydrogen bonds. After guanine binding, it stabilizes the P1 stem, promotes the formation of a terminator stem in the downstream expression platform, blocks the transcription of the downstream xpt-pbuX gene, and thereby inhibits the expression of purine transporters. In the absence of guanine, the aptamer conformation is loose, and the 3' end of P1 can form an antiterminator structure with the expression platform, allowing transcription to continue. This riboswitch dynamically regulates gene expression by sensing intracellular guanine concentration, maintains purine metabolic balance, and can be activated by artificially designed guanine analogues (such as G7) to achieve antibacterial effects.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(methoxyamino)-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one, also referred to as G2 (a derivative of guanine with an N-methoxy group (-N-OCH) introduced at the C2 position), is a purine derivative related to guanine or hypoxanthine, substituted with methoxyamino at the 2-position; this compound can bind to xpt-pbuX riboswitch aptamer and induce RNA to produce structural changes similar to natural guanine, but only retains binding activity and has no significant antibacterial function, while modified purines are typically investigated as nucleic acid metabolism inhibitors and this compound could potentially act as an antiviral or anticancer agent by inhibiting essential enzymes in DNA/RNA synthesis.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.

The polyethylamino chain offers a higher charge density, enabling tighter binding to the DNA backbone. This compound is designed to study the effect of linker chain length and chemical nature on DNA binding specificity, topoisomerase inhibitory activity, and intracellular drug transport, serving as a potential anticancer drug candidate.

This type of polycyclic cationic structure can interfere with the proliferation of cancer cells through the dual mechanisms of DNA intercalation and inhibition of key enzymes (like topoisomerases), and may help overcome certain forms of cancer cell resistance.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-amino-4-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propoxy]phenyl]-1-methylquinolin-4-one The core scaffold of this compound is a 4-quinolone derivative, linked at the 2-position to a substituted phenyl ring. The phenyl ring carries an amino group and a propoxy chain terminally substituted with N-methylpiperazine. The combination of the quinolone structure and multiple polar substituents suggests strong biological interaction potential. In terms of real activity, quinolone-based compounds are often studied as kinase inhibitors. This molecule may inhibit specific tyrosine kinases, disrupting cell signaling pathways, and thus may possess potential anticancer or anti-inflammatory activity.

Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-8-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistin (another isoflavone glycoside in this category is Puerarin, which is found in kudzu root), is a glycoside derivative of Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), known as Genistin, which is an isoflavone glycoside with the formula C21H20O9; Puerarin exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects, improves blood circulation and is studied for cardiovascular and metabolic diseases, while Genistins structure features the Genistein backbone with a glucose molecule (or glucoside moiety, the oxan-2-yl part) attached via a C-C bond at the C8 position, with the specific stereochemistry of the sugar being characteristic of its natural form, and it is the most abundant isoflavone compound found in soybeans, where it predominantly exists in this glycosylated form; in biology, most natural flavonoids exist as glycosides, which typically means they have higher water solubility but lower inherent bioactivity, requiring hydrolysis by gut microbes or intracellular -glucosidases to release the more active aglycone form, Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), thus, Genistin is often considered a prodrug form of Genistein, sharing similar health benefits such as phytoestrogenic, antioxidant, and potential anticancer activities, but with a different pharmacokinetic profile.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.

tetrazole, nitro, and dione functional groups, and 2,3-Dioxo-8-[2-(5-tetrazolyl)]-2,3,4,7,8,9-hexahydro-1H-6-nitro-cyclopenta[f]quinoxaline is a rationally designed small-molecule ligand that targets the bulge region of the HIV-1 TAR RNA stem-loop, the quinoxaline scaffold itself, formed by fusing a benzene ring and a pyrazine ring, has inherent biological potential and is frequently used in dye and pharmaceutical synthesis, the complexity of this molecule suggests it may be highly specific, the tetrazole ring (2H-tetrazol-5-yl) is a relatively acidic group, often employed as a bioisostere for the carboxylic acid group, capable of forming strong hydrogen or ionic bonds with positively charged protein sites, the dione (-2,3-dione) and nitro (-nitro) groups on the quinoxaline impart potential redox activity and stronger electron-accepting properties, overall, this molecule is likely designed as a highly specific receptor ligand or enzyme inhibitor, in real-world applications, complex scaffolds are beneficial for achieving high selectivity, by inserting its fused cyclopentaquinoxaline core into the widened major groove adjacent to the three-nucleotide bulge, the compound makes specific contacts (protection of C24, G26, G28) that overlap the recognition surface used by the viral Tat protein, this competitive, high-affinity binding in gel-shift assays displaces Tat from moiety provides aqueous solubility and an additional anionic anchor that favors RNA over DNA or tRNA, giving the compound a clear selectivity profile, thus, this quinoxaline derivative validates the RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(1,2,4-triazol-1-ylmethyl)-1H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl]pyridine is a poly-nitrogen heterocyclic compound featuring two 1,2,4-triazole rings and one pyridine ring. One 1,2,4-triazole ring (connected to the pyridine ring) serves as the core, while the other 1,2,4-triazole ring is linked to the 5-position of the core triazole ring via a methylene bridge. The triazole ring is an important pharmacophore, widely present in antifungal drugs and antivirals. This molecule is rich in nitrogen atoms, granting it strong hydrogen bonding and coordination capabilities, suggesting potential biological activities such as antifungal, antiviral, or anticancer effects, or applications as a metal coordinating agent.

RNA information: Contains a G bulge (a single guanine nucleotide that is not base-paired).This structure mimics the bulge present in the Dicer processing sites of both the miR-17 and miR-20a precursors.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)phenyl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as the benzimidazole-based RNA-binding substance, is a probe that is based on a mono-benzimidazole scaffold (instead of bis-benzimidazole) but still includes the tert-butyl and azide groups, and its core structure is benzimidazole which is known to have a binding preference for RNA, it also features a methylphenylpiperazine moiety that is crucial for its RNA-binding capability, and the azide group tag facilitates immobilization onto microarrays via click chemistry; mono-benzimidazoles are often precursors for antiviral, antifungal, and anti-inflammatory drugs, the presence of the azide group dictates its real-world activity as a Click Chemistry probe, it is designed to identify the biological targets of this specific mono-benzimidazole derivative through subsequent labeling and target characterization; in terms of binding properties, this compound does not bind to DNA but exclusively binds to specific RNA bulge structures, its binding to target RNAs has moderate affinity, and it specifically inhibits the processing of miR-17, -18a, and -20a by Dicer thereby suppressing the maturation of these oncogenic miRNAs; regarding its biological function, in cells it inhibits the production of miR-18a which in turn leads to the derepression of the STK4 protein and induces apoptosis in prostate cancer cells, additionally its specific binding to pre-miR-18a in cells has been validated using Chem-CLIP (Chemical Cross-Linking and Isolation by Pull-down) technology.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)benzenecarbothioamide, also referred to as 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole, is a combination of benzimidazole and carbothioamide structures, and 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole is a heterocyclic small molecule with a benzimidazole core linked to a thiocarbamoyl-substituted phenyl group, the benzimidazole core is often associated with antiparasitic, antifungal, antiviral, and antineoplastic activities, the carbothioamide group may enable the molecule to act as a metal chelator or enzyme inhibitor, the structure suggests the molecule may serve as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting on cell division or specific enzyme systems, unlike myricetin, it lacks reported affinity for CAG-repeat RNAs in HD/SCAs but may interact with structured RNAs via benzimidazole intercalation and thiocarbamoyl H-bonding, it shows no validated activity against polyQ aggregation or mitochondrial dysfunction in HD models, though its benzimidazole motif could potentially target nucleic acid mismatches, making it a speculative scaffold for TRED-focused optimization with further biophysical validation needed. -
