Small_molecule_information: acridin-9-amine is a famous DNA intercalator. The molecule exerts its effect by inserting itself between the base pairs of the DNA double helix. Historically, it has been used as a topical antiseptic and antibiotic. In molecular biology, it is an important fluorescent dye and mutagen, used to study DNA structure, replication, and repair, and is also applied in mutagenesis studies; it binds nucleic acids, disrupting replication and transcription, and is explored for anticancer potential. Its derivatives are used in antimalarial and antirheumatic treatment.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 5,12-dihydroquinolino[2,3-b]acridine-7,14-dione, also referred to as Quinacridone, is an organic compound with a highly conjugated structure, with the formula C20H12N2O2, and Quinacridone (C20H12N2O2) is a rigid, planar heteroaromatic pigment that has been repurposed as a non-phosphorylated, redox-neutral analog of flavin mononucleotide (FMN) for targeting the HIV-1 TAR RNA bulge, its core structure is a rigid, five-ring system formed by the fusion of two benzene rings, two pyridine rings, and a central ring, with two ketone groups at positions 7 and 14, this flat, rigid, polycyclic aromatic structure grants it excellent chemical stability and brilliant color, in real-world applications, Quinacridone is predominantly used as a high-performance organic pigment, because of its outstanding lightfastness, heat resistance, and chemical stability, it produces shades ranging from violet-red to red, and is widely used in automotive finishes, industrial coatings, plastics coloring, printing inks, and high-quality artist paints, it is an important functional material rather than a pharmaceutical agent, by replacing the anionic ribityl phosphate of FMN with a fused quinoline-acridine dione system, quinacridone gains exceptional -stacking surface area and photochemical stability while preserving the hydrogen-bond donor/acceptor array required for specific RNA recognition, in electrophoretic mobility-shift assays quinacridone completely disrupts the TatTARcyclin T1 complex at low micromolar concentrations, and in HeLa reporter assays it dose-dependently inhibits Tat-driven LTR trans-activation without affecting a heterologous Rev-RRE control, NMR titrations reveal that quinacridone induces large chemical-shift perturbations exclusively in the 5 bulge (U23, C24, U25, A22, G26) even at sub-stoichiometric ratios, confirming RNA-specific recognition analogous to FMN-riboswitch binding, thus, quinacridone offers a commercially available, non-toxic scaffold for developing next-generation antivirals that target viral RNA instead of host proteins. Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Kaempferol is found in a variety of plant foods, such as tea, broccoli, grapefruit, and herbs. Functionally, as a dietary flavonol, Kaempferol possesses significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and potential anticancer properties, it modulates cell signaling pathways (e.g., PI3K/Akt) and is studied for cardiovascular and neurodegenerative disease prevention, research also suggests it can regulate apoptosis, inhibit angiogenesis, and suppress cell proliferation, thereby potentially inhibiting tumor development, its role in the prevention of chronic diseases and the maintenance of cardiovascular health is particularly studied, although its bioactivity may differ from other flavonols due to the single hydroxyl group on its B ring.

RNA information: The r(CCUG)8 RNA forms an A-form structure with consecutive CU mismatches, sequestering MBNL1 protein, leading to splicing defects and DM2 disease symptoms.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis[3-(2,4,6-triaminopyrimidin-5-yl)propyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide, also referred to as Bisamidinium 6 , is a large, highly symmetrical organic molecule centered on a benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide core, which imparts significant positive charge potential due to its conjugated system and strongly basic imidamide groups, and this ligand exhibits notable inhibition potency against the MBNL1-r(CCUG) complex, the para-substituted core is connected via two three-carbon propylene flexible linkers to two terminal 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine rings, the three amino groups on the pyrimidine rings are also highly basic sites, resulting in a polycationic species under physiological conditions, this structural pattern, featuring multiple cationic heterocycles connected by flexible spacers, is characteristic of high-affinity nucleic acid binders, in pharmaceutical research, such polycationic compounds are often investigated as antiparasitic agents, functioning by electrostatic interaction to intercalate or bind to DNA and RNA, thereby disrupting genetic material replication and metabolism, and may also be potential anti-cancer cell proliferation agents, it effectively dissolves MBNL1-r(CCUG) foci in DM2 model cell cultures, demonstrating low cellular cytotoxicity and good water solubility.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-phenyl-N-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as compound 4, is a fused heterocyclic amide based on the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine scaffold, and it is also a 3-aminothieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide derivative characterized by a phenyl substituent at the 6-position, a structural modification critical for its enhanced potency as a TAR RNA binder. This tricyclic backbone features an amino group at position 3 and a phenyl group at position 6. It is linked via an amide bond to a 3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl substituent. The molecule is highly lipophilic, thanks to the phenyl group and the crucial trifluoromethyl (CF3) group. The trifluoromethyl group is a classic substituent in medicinal chemistry, used to enhance metabolic stability and transmembrane permeability, while also acting as a strong electron-withdrawing group. This planar, highly lipophilic amidic heterocycle is a prime example of a small molecule drug, often investigated as a non-competitive or competitive inhibitor of signal pathway enzymes like tyrosine kinases, with broad applicability in anti-cancer, immunomodulatory, and anti-inflammatory fields; additionally, it specifically interacts with key nucleobases in the HIV-1 TAR RNA elementG21, U23, and G43demonstrating dual binding affinity for both the bulge region (U23) and the lower stem (G43), a unique interaction profile that establishes it as a promising lead molecule for developing antiviral therapeutics that target RNA-protein interactions.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-5-hydroxy-2-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxyphenyl)-7-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxy-2,3-dihydrochromen-4-one, also referred to as Hesperidin, is a flavonoid glycoside with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects commonly found in citrus fruits, and is an extremely complex natural product flavonoid O-diglycoside with a dihydroflavone (flavanone) core substituted at the 7-position with a diglycoside unit (likely involving glucose and rhamnose); the numerous phenolic hydroxyl groups and the bulky glycoside moiety are the primary drivers of its activity, and in practice, such compounds are key functional components in herbal medicines, demonstrating potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and free radical scavenging capabilities, while due to its glycosidic structure, it typically has high water solubility and specific oral bioavailability (via gut microbial hydrolysis), and it is widely studied for cardiovascular benefits as well as cardioprotective, antiviral, and hepatoprotective effects, making it a focal point in natural product research.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]-3-(1H-imidazol-5-yl)propanoyl]amino]acetic acidA more complex polypeptide structure involving Arginine, Histidine, and Glycine linked to the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core. The increased complexity suggests it may be designed to mimic natural peptide substrates for enhanced affinity and selectivity towards specific biological targets. Its potential activity is as a peptide inhibitor (e.g., for proteases or GPCRs) which, combined with the cores cytotoxicity, suggests an application in highly targeted cancer therapy.

RNA information: SL I is a highly structured stem–loop within the HCV 3′ UTR that contains bulge motifs such as GUG and UAG. It is directly engaged by compounds 7, 8, and 10, which selectively bind its asymmetric internal loops. Functional assays confirmed that small molecules targeting SL I reduce viral replication in cell culture. ASO competition and RNase H cleavage validated that ligands interact with the predicted RNA motifs within SL I inside cells, confirming it as a functional viral target.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-4-[4-[[3-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]carbamoyl]phenyl]benzamide, also referred to as Compound 10, is a dicationic compound with an extended linear scaffold; it consists of a para-substituted dibenzamide core, with both ends attached via amide bonds to two identical 3-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl substituents, and the molecule contains two strongly basic imidazolinyl groups, which are protonated at physiological pH, conferring two positive charges, while its extended aromatic backbone and the fixed positive charges make it a classic example of a DNA minor groove binder; unlike traditional compounds where the imidazolinyl group is attached at the para position, this molecule features the imidazolinyl group attached at the meta (3-) position of the phenyl ring, which alters the spatial distribution of the positive charge that can lead to changes in its DNA minor groove binding mode and sequence specificity, and this class of molecules is important in research targeting antiparasitic (e.g., anti-amoebic, antitrypanosomal) and anticancer activities, in addition to which the compound interacts with RNA internal loops present in the HCV 3 UTR, binding to stemloops I and II with mid-nanomolar to low-micromolar affinities, though the exact sequence motifs were not reported in detail, and binding assays consistently showed activity against RNA elements harboring asymmetric bulges, with functional cell-based experiments confirming that it inhibits HCV replication, while similar to Compounds 7 and 8, RNase H cleavage competition validated its engagement with target RNA motifs in the cellular environment, and its activity profile underscores the broader potential of small molecules to exploit recurring viral RNA motifs for therapeutic intervention.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[3-(3-acetamidopropylamino)propylamino]propyl]-9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 1 (Acridine 1), is a complex compound based on the acridine-4-carboxamide scaffold, with the tricyclic aromatic acridine core that in this molecule is rationally employed to selectively bind toxic (CUG)\ RNA repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 through stacking and hydrogen bonding, while its multiple amino and amide groups, long-chain alkylamine linkers, and polyamine-containing side chains enhance solubility, permeability, and nuclear entry, and the appended 4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazine group at position 9 together with the extended N-linked polyamine chain contribute to potent nucleic-acid intercalation and disruption of pathogenic RNAprotein interactions, suggesting potential bioactivity including therapeutic relevance in DM1 as well as possible roles in broader anticancer or antibacterial contexts.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-8-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]-[1,2,4]triazolo[4,3-a]quinoline, also referred to as SMSM60, is a non-planar coumarin analog with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, carrying a 1 charge, and is characterized by a unique tetracyclic aromatic system: the [1,2,4]triazolo[4,3-a]quinoline core, this fused heterocycle is rich in nitrogen atoms, giving it distinct electronic properties, the compound has an 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl group at C4 and a positively charged quaternized (3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium moiety at C8, it recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1, driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, and triazoloquinoline derivatives are prominent in medicinal chemistry, particularly in the central nervous system (CNS) drug space, including agents for anxiety and insomnia, therefore, 4-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-8-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]-[1,2,4]triazolo[4,3-a]quinoline is likely investigated as a potential neurotransmitter receptor modulator, such as a GABA receptor ligand, or for applications in cardiovascular diseases, with its cationic nature influencing its brain distribution or interaction with specific CNS targets.

RNA information: r(AUAU): Functioning as another critical model hairpin, r(AUAU) contains a stem with repeating 5'-AU/3'-UA base pair steps. This structural motif is of particular interest as it directly mimics the periodic AU base pair steps found in the pathogenic r(AUUCU) repeat expansion responsible for Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 10 (SCA10). In binding assays, this RNA consistently showed high affinity for the lead compounds, especially molecule 2 and its dimeric derivative 2AU-2, confirming it as a high-affinity target. Its design was crucial for identifying small molecules that could be developed to target the disease-causing RNA structure selectively.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (DB75, furamidine), is a bis-imidamide compound where the two benzamidine groups are connected by a furan ring, with the formula C18H16N4O, and it is also a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative with a furamidine chromophore and smaller amidine end-groups; the introduction of the furan ring imparts some flexibility to the molecule, allowing for conformational adjustments during DNA binding, and this compound can selectively bind AU base pairs with positive cooperativity, targeting r(AUUCU) repeats and protecting AU sites from RNase cleavage, can bind HIV-1 TAR RNAs GCUCU motif via shape-driven recognition, displace Tat, block trans-activation, stabilize TAR, and has anti-PCP activity/DNA minor-groove binding, with no cytotoxicity below 30 M and serving as a TAR-targeting benchmark; these heterocyclic diamidines are a focus in the development of antiparasitic drugs, leveraging their strong DNA binding ability to effectively inhibit the growth of protozoa like Trypanosoma, and also show potential for antifungal and anticancer therapy.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-benzylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide dibromide, also referred to as PyDH5, is a dicationic compound built on a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide central core, with two Schiff base (methylideneamino) side chains linked to the two amide nitrogen atoms. Each side chain terminates in a N-benzyl quaternized quinoline ring, the 1-benzylquinolin-1-ium structure, attached at the 6-position of the quinoline via the methylideneamino group. The quinolinium salts are permanently positively charged groups, thus the entire molecule carries two positive charges (with Br as the counterion). This structure features an extended conjugated system, highly planar quinoline rings, and two fixed positive charge centers, and is similar to PyDH3 but with 1-benzylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups. Dicationic (or polycationic) aromatic compounds of this type are classic examples of DNA minor groove binders. They are known to enter the minor groove of the DNA double helix through electrostatic attraction and shape complementarity, and may exhibit sequence specificity. Consequently, this compound and its analogues are widely researched as DNA probes, anticancer agents, or antimicrobial agents, acting by interfering with DNA replication, transcription, and repair processes, while PyDH5 itself showed moderate G4 binding but induced GAr-independent increases in EBNA1, indicating non-specific effects or toxicity.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]pentanedioic acidThis is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline-peptide conjugate similar to 2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]butanedioic acid, maintaining the potentially cytotoxic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core. The peptide chain consists of Arginine and Glutamic acid. As an acidic amino acid, Glutamic acid enhances hydrophilicity. The molecule is likely designed as a cell-penetrating or tumor-targeted agent. The Glutamic acid residue might be leveraged for transport via amino acid transporters or cleaved by tumor-specific enzymes to release the active drug, indicating potential applications in targeted cancer therapy.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-[2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]ethane-1,2-diamine, also referred to as the indolo[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with a p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and an N1,N1-dimethylethane-1,2-diamine side chain, is a neutral indolo[3,2-b]quinoline with an ethane-1,2-diamine side chain; this compound exhibits measurable but weak binding to the NRAS RNA G-quadruplex and demonstrates potent anti-proliferative activity in A375 melanoma cells, while the subtle difference in side chain length (two carbons versus three) compared to N,N-dimethyl-N-[2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]propane-1,3-diamine is crucial for modifying the molecules conformation and target affinity, and it is investigated to determine the optimal chain length for potent antiproliferative activity.

RNA information: r(GGCC)4 is a short, double-stranded RNA motif composed of four tandem GGCC repeats that adopts a fully base-paired hairpin structure. It is commonly used in competition assays as a negative control because it lacks internal loops or single-stranded regions and therefore does not bind to RNA-targeted small molecules such as CB253, which selectively recognize the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp RNA .
Small_molecule_information: N-[4-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-5-(4-chlorophenyl)furan-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Benzimidazole 17 (2015), is another complex aromatic heterocycle centered on a benzimidazole core, and Benzimidazole 17 (2015) is a 2-phenyl-benzimidazole-4-carboxamide derivative engineered to selectively bind the toxic r(CUG)109 RNA expansion that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1, the benzimidazole is linked at position 2 via a phenyl ring to an amide bond, which in turn is connected to a furan ring substituted with a 4-chlorophenyl group, compared to N-[4-[6-(furan-2-carbonylamino)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenyl]furan-2-carboxamide, this structure is simpler, but its side chain is more lipophilic, incorporating a chlorine atom (4-chlorophenyl) to modulate its lipophilicity, metabolic stability, and affinity for its target, the molecule exhibits high planarity and an extended conjugated system, enabling strong binding to biomacromolecules such as proteins, enzymes, or DNA, the combination of a chlorine-substituted phenyl group and a benzimidazole is a common motif in drug design, often employed to enhance the molecules biological activity, this compound is investigated for potential anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, or antitumor properties, likely acting by inhibiting specific enzymes or protein-protein interactions, by mimicking the recognition surface of MBNL1, it engages the periodic UU internal loops of the CUG hairpin with notable potency in TR-FRET displacement, displaces MBNL1 from ribonuclear foci, and restores correct alternative splicing of the cTNT mini-gene in DM1 cell models without altering DMPK mRNA abundance or affecting off-target RNAs such as human A-site rRNA or r(GGCC)4, its drug-like physicochemical profilemoderate molecular weight (352 Da), cLogP 3.9, topological polar surface area 111 , and negligible cytotoxicity up to 100 Mdemonstrates that uncharged, benzimidazole-based small molecules can selectively drug structured, repetitive RNA motifs and rescue disease-relevant splicing defects. RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: [(3R,4S,5R,6R)-5-hydroxy-6-[4-hydroxy-3-[[4-hydroxy-3-(3-methylbut-2-enyl)benzoyl]amino]-8-methyl-2-oxochromen-7-yl]oxy-3-methoxy-2,2-dimethyloxan-4-yl]carbamate, also referred to as novobiocin, is a non-azaaromatic aminocoumarin antibiotic with a tricyclic core (coumarin, benzenediol, and sugar moieties) that lacks planar hydrophobic azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, and is a known antibacterial agent that targets the DNA Gyrase B subunit in bacteria, primarily used historically for treating certain Staphylococcus infections, with its key function being inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase to block DNA replication, which is unrelated to azaaromatic sensing.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(E)-2-(1H-indol-2-yl)ethenyl]-1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)quinolin-1-ium, also referred to as Compound 23This is a green-yellow cationic quinolinium derivative and dye featuring a highly conjugated structure with an indole group and a piperazine group, which has a 2-(1H-indol-2-yl)vinyl group at the 2-position and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl group at the 4-position of the quinoline ring. Cationic dyes based on quinoline are known for their ability to bind to nucleic acids (DNA/RNA), and this compound has affinity for A10-RNA-Wt (ADAM10 mRNAs G-quadruplex sequence) as shown by SPR assay. Combining quinoline and indole results in strong fluorescence properties. Its real-world activity is primarily as a fluorescent dye for nuclear staining and quantification in cell biology, and due to its strong DNA binding, it also possesses potential broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antifungal, or anticancer activity; in addition, dual-luciferase reporter assay reveals it enhances ADAM10 translation, and it has certain cytotoxicity towards related cells.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2,6-diaminopurin-9-yl)-5-(hydroxymethyl)oxolane-3,4-diol, also referred to as 2,6-Diaminopurine riboside, is a planar azaaromatic and non-natural purine nucleoside, with its structure characterized by the linkage of 2,6-Diaminopurine (a purine ring carrying amino groups (-NH) at the 2- and 6-positions) to D-ribose at the N9 position, molecular formula C10H14N6O4; it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, and in terms of real-world activity, due to the properties of its DAP base (7H-purine-2,6-diamine), it is considered a non-classical nucleoside that can pair with Thymine (T) via three hydrogen bonds, which is more stable than the natural pairing, giving it special application potential in synthetic biology and nucleic acid detection technology, meanwhile it also serves as an important research tool for molecular biology, and as a purine nucleoside analog, it acts as an antiviral/antitumor agent that can disrupt nucleic acid synthesis by mimicking natural nucleosides, and may inhibit the proliferation of viruses or cancer cells by interfering with purine biosynthesis or competing with natural nucleotides, thus being a potential lead compound for broad-spectrum antiviral drugs.

Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3S,4S)-5-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Flavin mononucleotide (FMN), is a phosphate-containing pteridine derivative and a nucleotide-like coenzyme and flavin compound that binds to yjdF motif RNAs (azaaromatic riboswitches), inducing structural changes in the RNA as shown by in-line probing, whose riboflavin moiety is a planar nitrogen-containing polycyclic compound and unlike some azaaromatics fails to trigger riboswitch-mediated gene expression, thus not considered the natural ligand of these riboswitches, its structure is based on the 7,8-dimethylbenzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione core (an isomer of the flavin or riboflavin core): a tetracyclic fused benzopteridine skeleton, with a phosphated tetrahydroxypentyl side chain attached at the N10-position, the molecular formula is CHNOP, the compound possesses a conjugated skeleton, and the presence of the phosphate group makes it negatively charged and water-soluble under physiological conditions, in terms of biological function, its core derivatives are components of crucial flavin coenzymes (such as FMN, FAD) in living systems, acting as key electron carriers in cellular oxidation-reduction reactions.

Small_molecule_information: Disodium;[(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(2,6-dioxo-3H-purin-9-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methyl phosphate, also referred to as Xanthosine monophosphate (XMP, or xanthylic acid) and Guanosine Monophosphate Disodium (GMP Disodium), is a non-azaaromatic purine nucleotide with the formula C10H12N5Na2O8P that exists as a disodium salt, structured with a xanthine base, a ribose sugar (the oxolan ring), and a single phosphate group linked to the riboses 5-carbon, which is composed of the guanine base (2,6-dioxo-3H-purin) linked at N9 to a D-ribose sugar that is phosphorylated at the 5 position, it lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch), and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, with its core role being as a critical intermediate in purine nucleotide biosynthesis, acting as the precursor for both guanosine monophosphate (GMP) and adenosine monophosphate (AMP) to support nucleic acid synthesis and cellular metabolism, and GMP is also a precursor for Ribonucleic Acid (RNA) and Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA), and participates in energy storage and signal transduction (e.g., in the G-protein coupled receptor system) as Guanosine Triphosphate (GTP) within the cell, in real-world application, GMP Disodium is primarily used as a food additive, specifically as a flavor enhancer, which is often combined with Inosine Monophosphate (IMP) (known as IG) to provide and intensify the savory or umami taste in foods, widely used in soups, instant noodle seasonings, snacks, and processed meats.

RNA information: HIV-1 Ψ SL3 is a key element of the viral Ψ packaging domain, featuring a G9-G10-A11-G12 tetranucleotide loop and a stem structure. Its core function is the specific binding to the Gag polyprotein (especially the nucleocapsid domain), which is a critical step for the packaging of the viral genomic RNA into virions. Additionally, this element is highly conserved and serves as a potential target for antiviral therapy.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[(1-methyl-6-nitroquinolin-4-ylidene)amino]-N-[4-[(1-methyl-4-pyridinylidene)amino]phenyl]benzamide, also referred to as NSC260594 (a quinolinium derivative structurally similar to known DNA-binding compounds), is a complex quinoline-pyridine-benzamide derivative; this compound not only is typically designed to explore anti-tumor activity due to their potential as DNA intercalators and topoisomerase inhibitors but also effectively inhibits HIV-1 replication by specifically binding to the G9-G10-A11-G12 tetraloop region within the viral RNA packaging signal SL3, thereby obstructing the interaction between the Gag protein and the RNA and consequently suppressing viral particle packaging and release, with its mechanism of action confirmed by H NMR spectroscopy involving a specific binding interaction that is distinctly different from the mode of action of the non-specific intercalator ellipticine.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide diiodide, also referred to as PhenDH2, is a dicationic aromatic heterocyclic compound featuring a phenanthroline scaffold and two quaternary quinolinium saltsPhenDH21-C36H30I2N8O2DNA/RNAPhenDH2G4FIDGArEBNA1NCLmRNADNADNA

RNA information: BS-xpt(U48G) is a guanine riboswitch mutant of Bacillus subtilis. Structurally, it alters the aptamer core’s local conformation, enhancing cleavage at positions 47/49 and reducing it at 48. Functionally, G48 introduction (context-independent) markedly impairs ligand (guanine/2AP) binding affinity but retains partial guanine-induced structural reorganization. Its in vivo transcriptional termination regulation efficiency declines compared to the wild-type BS-xpt.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2,6-dimethylphenyl]phenol, also referred to as Compound 6b, is another highly complex and large tetracationic polyamine, containing four primary amine groups and a phenol functional group, which was synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki coupling reactions, binds to HIV-1 RRE Bh with high affinity and can induce loop conformational changes, potently inhibits the relevant protein-RNA interaction and HIV-1 replication, and blocks Rev-mediated RNA transport; the presence of the phenol confers potential for antioxidant or kinase inhibitor activity, and unlike simple linear polyamines, this complex rigid scaffold gives it a unique spatial conformation, while in practical applications, polyamines with such complex, highly charged, large structures may be explored for highly specific nucleic acid binding or as multivalent ligands for binding specific cell surface receptors or metal ions (chelating agents).

RNA information: (CUG)₁₆ is a synthetic RNA oligonucleotide mimicking pathogenic expanded (CUG)ₙ repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it folds into a stable A-form hairpin with repeating 5'CUG/3'GUC units, featuring U-U mismatches flanked by G-C base pairs. Functionally, it high-affinity binds Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1), sequestering it to disrupt alternative splicing (DM1’s core gain-of-function mechanism) and serving in vitro for cleavage assays to screen catalytic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: amino-[4-[amino-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butylazaniumylidene]methyl]phenyl]methylidene]-[4-[[4-amino-6-[2-[bis(2-aminoethyl)amino]ethylamino]-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butyl]azanium, also referred to as a bisamidinium-derived molecule with added amine groups, is a massively complex, highly positively charged compound, likely a dicationic or polycationic dye or polyamine derivative; this compound binds both DNA and RNA CTG/CUG repeats, potently inhibits transcription, cleaves (CUG) selectively (not CCUG), and is highly effective in reversing DM phenotypes with low toxicity, while its core features two Triazine units each linked to a polyamine tail, the central azaniumylidene and methylidene groups point to a highly stabilized guanidinium or amidinium derivative, conferring high basicity and charge, and due to its multiple positive charges and hydrogen bond donors, the molecule is researched as a highly efficient gene transfection reagent (for delivering DNA/RNA into cells) or as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting by disrupting the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 24-methyl-5,7,18,20-tetraoxa-24-azoniahexacyclo[11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21]tetracosa-1(24),2,4(8),9,11,13,15,17(21),22-nonaene, also referred to as Sanguinarine, is a highly complex, polycyclic cationic compound, which can be chemically identified as the N-methyl derivative of a macrocyclic tetraoxaza-heteroatom system, and its molecular structure is dominated by its extensive ring fusion as indicated by its IUPAC name, and Sanguinarine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant structural changes in the RNA, Sanguinarine is also a benzophenanthridine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and pro-apoptotic effects, it targets multiple cellular pathways, including NF-B, and is studied for anticancer applications despite potential toxicity concerns, the compounds formula, C21H16NO4, reveals a structure containing 21 carbon atoms, 4 oxygen atoms, and a single nitrogen atom that carries a permanent positive charge (azonia) due to methylation, the core hexacyclic structure suggests a rigid, cage-like framework featuring two major 12-membered rings bridged by various fused smaller rings (implied by the [11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21] nomenclature), leading to a highly planar and stiff molecular geometry, the presence of four oxygen atoms and nine double bonds (nonaene) indicates extensive unsaturation, and the presence of oxygen atoms at positions 5, 7, 18, and 20 suggests they are likely incorporated into ether or furan-like structures, strongly suggesting this molecule is an analog of macrocyclic dyes or natural products with rigid, fluorescent properties, potentially related to porphyrins or cryptands in shape, although Sanguinarine has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, it fails to activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, possibly due to its toxicity to cells, nevertheless, Sanguinarine, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch, due to its fixed positive charge and large, planar aromatic system, this compound is highly likely to function as an intercalating agent for nucleic acids, making it a potential DNA/RNA binding fluorescent probe for biological imaging, or a promising scaffold for anti-cancer or antimicrobial drug development, where its rigidity could confer high target specificity.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-N-(2-morpholin-4-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with diiodide ions. Its dicationic structure includes the core N-methylation and the 4-methylmorpholin-4-ium group at position 7, along with a 2-morpholin-4-ylethylamino substituted chain at position 11. This structure specifically incorporates two morpholine groups into the molecule. The compound has practical potential in bio-probes and drug delivery vehicles. As a therapeutic agent, its cationic and polycyclic properties allow it to act as a DNA damaging agent or a chemotherapy sensitizer, enhancing the efficacy of existing chemotherapeutic drugs by binding to tumor cell DNA or inhibiting key proteins related to DNA metabolism.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-(3-aminopropoxy)-5-(5,7-dimethoxy-1-methyl-4-oxoquinolin-2-yl)phenyl]-4-(dimethylamino)butanamide, also referred to as Phenylquinolone 3c, is a disubstituted derivative synthesized from its monosubstituted precursor 2c by introducing a second cationic side chain through acylation with 4-(dimethylamino)butyric acid (DMABA), and it features a complex substitution pattern: the primary amine on the phenyl ring is replaced by a 4-(dimethylamino)butanamide, and the side chain is a 3-aminopropoxy (primary amine); the molecule contains amide, primary amine, and tertiary amine functional groups, forming a complex network of basicity and hydrogen bonding, with the amide bond being a key structural feature of many protease inhibitors, while the addition of the second protonable group proved highly detrimental to its activity, as the compound was completely inactive (N/A) in the fluorescence quenching assay, demonstrating no ability to inhibit the Tat/TAR complex formation, a result that strongly supports the critical structure-activity relationship finding that a single, not dual, cationic side chain is essential for effective target binding in this series of 2-phenylquinolone inhibitors; functionally, this multi-functional structure may be designed as a multi-target inhibitor, simultaneously inhibiting multiple biological targets like kinases/topoisomerases and proteases, with potential use in the treatment of multidrug-resistant cancer.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenoxy)pentoxy]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 24 (EBAB, Pentamidine), is an amidine linked by an alkoxy chain, where two amidine groups are connected by a relatively long pentyloxy ether chain, this molecule is an aromatic diamidine with benzamidine pharmacophore and features a 1,2-ethane/1,5-bis(4-amidinophenoxy)pentane scaffold and bisamidinium groups, its bis-amidine ether structure is highly significant in medicinal chemistry, exemplified by the well-known antiprotozoal drug Pentamidine which acts by binding to parasite DNA, it can also bind r(CUG)exps UU loops/5CUG/3GUC hairpins, displace MBNL1, correct DM1 splicing defects, act as an antibiotic adjuvant against multidrug-resistant bacteria, inhibit Class I intron self-splicing, and has low toxicity, the structural features of the molecule make it a candidate for a potential antiprotozoal or antifungal drug particularly against diseases like Pneumocystis pneumonia, and its activity is closely related to the length and flexibility of the linker chain, while also showing promise for DM1 and drug-resistant infections.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, the xanthene moiety bears 3,6-dihydroxy groups, which facilitate the formation of the emissive quinoid structure, while the 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro substituents on the benzofuranone ring can significantly modify its fluorescence properties, such as increasing quantum yield or causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-8-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistin (another isoflavone glycoside in this category is Puerarin, which is found in kudzu root), is a glycoside derivative of Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), known as Genistin, which is an isoflavone glycoside with the formula C21H20O9; Puerarin exhibits antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects, improves blood circulation and is studied for cardiovascular and metabolic diseases, while Genistins structure features the Genistein backbone with a glucose molecule (or glucoside moiety, the oxan-2-yl part) attached via a C-C bond at the C8 position, with the specific stereochemistry of the sugar being characteristic of its natural form, and it is the most abundant isoflavone compound found in soybeans, where it predominantly exists in this glycosylated form; in biology, most natural flavonoids exist as glycosides, which typically means they have higher water solubility but lower inherent bioactivity, requiring hydrolysis by gut microbes or intracellular -glucosidases to release the more active aglycone form, Genistein (7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), thus, Genistin is often considered a prodrug form of Genistein, sharing similar health benefits such as phytoestrogenic, antioxidant, and potential anticancer activities, but with a different pharmacokinetic profile.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 2-amino-9-[(2R,3R,4S,5R)-3,4-dihydroxy-5-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]-1H-purin-6-one, also referred to as Guanosine (G), is the standard, naturally occurring non-azaaromatic nucleoside composed of a guanine base (a purine ring with an amino and carbonyl group) covalently bonded to D-ribose, which lacks planar azaaromatic features; it is one of the fundamental building blocks of RNA and is indispensable in all cellular life, serving as a core building block for RNA and a precursor for guanosine nucleotides (e.g., GTP) that supports nucleic acid synthesis and cellular energy transfer, while it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; while guanosine itself is not typically used as a drug, its derivatives and analogs form the basis of many important medicines, such as antiviral drugs (e.g., ribavirin, acyclovir), and in the body, guanosine is involved in the synthesis of GTP, which is a key molecule in G-protein coupled receptor signaling, energy storage, and protein synthesis. guanosineadenosine

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-diethyl-N-[5-methyl-7-[2-(1-methylpiperidin-1-ium-1-yl)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]propane-1,3-diamine diiodide is a benzofuroquinoline bis-quaternary ammonium salt derivative. Its structure features the benzofuroquinoline scaffold, with quaternization at the 5-position nitrogen and the piperidine ring on the 7-position side chain, and a diethylpropanediamine side chain at the 11-position (molecular formula C33H45N4O22I). This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and two positive charges. It is recognized as a potential anti-cancer drug due to its potent DNA or RNA binding characteristics.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol;dihydrochloride, also referred to as Quinacrine Di HCl, is a quinoline-phenol heterocyclic compound presented as the dihydrochloride salt, which is an antimalarial and antiprotozoal drug with DNA intercalation properties and has been repurposed for cancer and prion diseases due to its ability to inhibit protein aggregation and nucleic acid interactions, its structure featuring a 4-aminoquinoline backbone with a 7-chloro substitution is chemically analogous to classic antimalarial drugs like chloroquine, the presence of both a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group on the phenyl ring provides multiple sites for interaction and protonation, quinolines are a broad class of compounds known for antimalarial, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory activities, due to its salt form the molecule is highly protonated enhancing its water solubility and the functional groups suggest potential for metal ion chelation, in reality this compound is likely explored as a novel antimalarial candidate to combat drug resistance or possibly as an anticancer agent due to its potential to interfere with metal homeostasis or kinase activity.

RNA information: This RNA forms a well-ordered binding pocket through interlocking structural motifs . In the presence of Mg²+, it can bind theophylline with high affinity  and exhibits molecular discrimination specificity towards caffeine.
Small_molecule_information: 1,3-dimethyl-7H-purine-2,6-dione, also referred to as Theophylline, a 1,3-dimethylxanthine structurally related to caffeine (differing from caffeine only by lacking a methyl group at the N7 position), is a naturally occurring alkaloid in green and black tea that acts as a bronchodilator, vasodilator, muscle relaxant, adenosine-receptor antagonist and phosphodiesterase inhibitor; as a ligand, it binds theophylline-binding RNA in a Mg-dependent way, via hydrogen bonds with the RNAs C22 and U24, supported by base triples, and RNA discriminates it from caffeine via such structural interactions. Despite its narrow therapeutic index, the SELEX-derived 38-nt RNA aptamer TCT8-4 binds theophylline with high affinity and high specificity over caffeine, exploiting a hydrogen bond to N-7, favorable contacts with the N-3 methyl and C-2/C-6 carbonyls, and a steric barrier against C-8 substitution, thereby providing a high-resolution RNA sensor for this multifunctional drug and human serum metabolite.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-diethyl-N-[2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]ethane-1,2-diamine, also referred to as the compound with a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline scaffold bearing a p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and an N1,N1-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine side chain at the 11-position, is a neutral benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline featuring a large -conjugated stilbene structure at the 2-position; it has improved binding affinity, exhibits potent anti-proliferative activity against A375 cells, and is a strong fluorophore used as an organic light-emitting material or a biological probe, potentially active in photodynamic therapy (PDT).
