Small_molecule_information: [[(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(6-aminopurin-9-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methoxy-hydroxyphosphoryl] [(2R,3S,4S)-5-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] hydrogen phosphate is a complex diphosphate ester covalently linking a nucleotide and a flavin derivative. The structure consists of two main parts: an Adenosine Monophosphate (AMP) nucleotide moiety and a Flavin Monophosphate (FMN) flavin moiety, connected by a pyrophosphate bridge. The molecular formula is C27H36N9O15P2. The compounds structure is analogous to Flavin Adenine Dinucleotide (FAD), making it a critical coenzyme in biological systems. In terms of biological function, it acts as an electron carrier in redox reactions and a cofactor for various enzymes, playing a vital role in metabolism.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: 1-cyclopropyl-6-fluoro-4-oxo-7-piperazin-1-yl-2,3-dihydroquinoline-3-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Ciprofloxacin (1-cyclopropyl-6-fluoro-1,4-dihydro-4-oxo-7-(1-piperazinyl)-quinoline-3-carboxylic acid), is the active moiety of the drug Ciprofloxacin, a classic example of a second-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic designed to inhibit bacterial DNA replication, with the formula C17H18FN3O3. The core structure is a quinolone moiety with a 4-oxo and a 3-carboxylic acid group, which is a common quinoline-3-carboxylic acid core shared with other fluoroquinolones, substituted with a cyclopropyl group at N1 (a cyclopropyl substituent at N-1), a fluorine atom at C6 (a fluoro group at C-6), and a piperazinyl group at C7 (a piperazinyl group at C-7), modifications that enhance its spectrum and potency, and this complex structure is responsible for its potent, broad-spectrum antibacterial activity. In vitro, ciprofloxacin binds to and stabilizes the DNA gyraseDNA and topoisomerase IVDNA complexes, thereby preventing re-ligation of double-strand breaks and leading to lethal DNA fragmentation, and in real-world clinical use, Ciprofloxacin is an extremely important anti-infective agent that kills bacteria by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase and DNA topoisomerase IV, thereby interfering with bacterial DNA replication, transcription, and repair. It is effective against a wide range of Gram-negative bacteria (including Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Escherichia coli, Neisseria gonorrhoeae) and some Gram-positive bacteria (e.g., Staphylococcus aureus, Enterococcus spp.), commonly used to treat urinary tract infections, respiratory tract infections, gastrointestinal infections, skin infections, and various serious systemic infections, which can be administered both orally and intravenously, representing a key second-generation fluoroquinolone drug that remains a cornerstone agent for empirical and directed antibacterial therapy.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-(2,3,3a,4,6,6a-hexahydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyrrol-2-ium-5-yl)-3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM54, is a non-planar coumarin derivative where the substituent at position 7 is a hexahydropyrrolo[3,4-c]pyrrol-2-ium quaternary ammonium salt, carrying a 1 charge, this is a complex fused ring structure formed by the fusion of two five-membered pyrrolidine rings with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, this fused bicyclic structure is relatively rigid, which helps provide precise spatial positioning during binding to a biological target, the quaternary ammonium positive charge is a crucial factor for improving water solubility and electrostatic binding affinity, the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group is connected at position 3 of the coumarin core, the molecules overall conjugated system may impart unique fluorescent or spectral properties, it recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, and in medicinal chemistry, this type of structure is often designed to target enzymes or receptors with complex binding pockets, such as proteins involved in cell membrane transport or signal transduction.

RNA information: 5′AAC/3′U_G: This AAC/U_G motif represents an asymmetric loop that was enriched in binding assays but not highlighted as a major validated target for compounds with antiviral activity. Its structure provides potential recognition elements but no detailed functional data in the context of HCV was reported.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[3-[[2,5-bis[[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]carbamoyl]phenyl]carbamoylamino]phenyl]carbamoylamino]-1-N,4-N-bis[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 7, is an extremely large, highly branched polycationic compound, and it can selectively bind internal loop motifs such as 5UUA/3A_U and 5UGU/3G_A with submicromolar affinity, with structural studies revealing that its binding is driven by recognition of asymmetric bulges within RNA stems, it can directly interact with HCV 3 UTR stemloops I and II by targeting motifs present in viral RNA, functional assays have demonstrated that it inhibits HCV replication in cell culture without affecting host ribosomal RNAs, and RNase H/ASO mapping has confirmed its intracellular engagement of the predicted binding sites; its intricate scaffold contains multiple phenyl rings, amide bonds, and urea groups, terminated by several 4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl substituents, structural analysis indicates that this molecule bears at least six imidazolinyl groups, making it a potent hexacationic system, this structural design is an extreme example used to construct supramolecular nucleic acid binders, where the molecular length, rigidity, and multiple charges enable high-affinity interactions with multiple sites on the DNA helix, including the minor groove, major groove, or phosphate backbone, and such compounds are often investigated as non-viral gene delivery vectors (by charge-neutralizing DNA), highly effective anticancer agents, or for use as DNA structural probes, aiming for extremely high binding stability and cytotoxicity.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 4-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylideneamino]pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide diiodide, also referred to as PymDH2, is a dicationic compound with a pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide core (containing a central pyrimidine core with 1-methylquinolinium lateral groups) linking two 1-methylquinolin-1-ium cationic groups via methylideneamino (imine) bonds, this structure is a complex, multicharged system with a rigid structure and four potential cationic centers, counterbalanced by two iodide ions, this polycationic nature gives it extremely high affinity for negatively charged biomolecules (such as DNA, ATP, etc.), it showed moderate G4 affinity but no effect on EBNA1 expression, in practical applications, it is studied as a nucleic acid probe and a therapeutic agent, binding to DNA through strong electrostatic interactions and stacking, particularly exhibiting high selectivity for Guanine-rich G-quadruplex structures, making it a potential anticancer G4 ligand.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-8-methyl-7-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM36, is a non-planar coumarin derivative characterized by an additional methyl substituent at position 8 of the coumarin ring, possessing a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and an imidazopyridine-thiazole DE-fused system with a 1 charge, position 3 is substituted with the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group, and position 7 carries the chiral (3S) methylpiperazinium cation, which targets the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatic interactions as validated by FP and SAR analysis, acting as a selective RNA ligand, the methyl group at position 8 is likely to impose significant steric hindrance on the molecules binding to the target, thus modulating its activity and selectivity, it may also influence the electronic density and metabolic stability of the coumarin ring, and the chiral quaternary ammonium salt is crucial for improved water solubility and electrostatic binding, this type of structure is frequently designed to target specific protein kinases, such as FLT3 or BTK, for the treatment of cancer or autoimmune diseases.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(E,2S,7S)-7-amino-1,8-bis[(2S)-2-[[(E)-3-(3-aminopropylamino)-3-oxo-1-phenylprop-1-en-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-1,8-dioxooct-4-en-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 8E, is a large peptidomimetic molecule whose most prominent feature is the incorporation of a dehydro-phenylalanine derivative, an enamine (CC) double bond unit, in its side chain; Compound 8E is a one-armed benzo[g]quinoline derivative with a different truncation pattern compared to 7Z, which shows weak binding to HIV-1 FSS RNA, with relatively low potency than the high-affinity dimers, has a rapid dissociation rate reflecting poor stability of the RNAligand complex, and has no measurable impact on HIV-1 frameshifting or infectivity, further supporting the conclusion that removal of one or both aromatic arms compromises binding capacity and confirming the necessity of the complete heteroaromatic dimeric architecture for strong FSS recognition. The introduction of this double bond serves a dual purpose: it rigidly locks the side chain conformation, which is beneficial for increasing binding specificity to a particular target, and it enhances resistance of the amide bonds to peptidase hydrolysis, potentially extending the molecules half-life in vivo, a key optimization strategy for peptide-based drugs. The compounds backbone contains a trans (E) double bond, resulting in a relatively linear overall conformation. It is capped by a benzo[g]quinoline aromatic heterocycle at one end, providing DNA binding potential, and a free amino group at the other. This combination of high stability and fixed conformation suggests its design as a conformationally constrained, highly stable ligand, potentially acting as a cell membrane receptor ligand or a stable protease inhibitor in oncology or anti-inflammatory fields.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 9H-pyrido[3,4-b]indole, commonly known as -Carboline and also referred to as norharmane, is a naturally occurring fused tricyclic heterocyclic compound formed by the fusion of an indole ring with a pyridine ring at the [3,4-b] positions, possessing a planar hydrophobic nitrogen-containing polycyclic skeleton and alkaloid-like aromatic features, and in addition to being widely found in plants, insects, and mammals with various psychoactive and MAO-inhibitory properties and the ability to bind DNA and benzodiazepine receptors, it can also interact non-specifically with the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) with relatively low affinity and does not trigger riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, making it useful for studying ligand structureactivity relationships and natural ligand specificity.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: N-[[4-[6-[(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-ylhydrazinylidene)methyl]imidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-2-yl]phenyl]methylideneamino]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-amine, also referred to as Compound 2 (H2), is a complex bis-imine and hydrazone derivative. The core structure includes an imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine moiety, a phenyl group, and two 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole rings. Imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine is a fused bicyclic diazaheterocyclic system known for its fluorescent properties and diverse biological activities. The molecule contains two 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) groups, which are connected to the main chain via a hydrazone (hydrazinylidene) and an imine (methylideneamino) linkage, respectively. The imidazolinium structure is often used as a ligand or a biologically active unit. This complex poly-nitrogen aromatic structure recognizes GG internal loops in r(CGG)exp hairpins and binds r(CUG)exp, partially restoring normal reporter signal in DM1 cells, although it is less selective (binding CGG/CCG/A-site RNAs) and shows high-dose cytotoxicity, serving as a tool compound for FXTAS/DM1 therapeutic development, and suggesting potential applications as a fluorescent probe, a ligand for biomacromolecules, or a drug candidate with anticancer or antiviral properties.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

Small_molecule_information: Sodium 1-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-7-hydroxy-2-oxido-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]pyrimidine-2,4-dione, also referred to as Uridine 3,5-cyclic monophosphate (cUMP), is a derivative or analogue of uridylic acid, specifically related to cyclic Uridine Monophosphate (cUMP), existing as a sodium salt, and Uridine 3,5-cyclic monophosphate (cUMP) is a cyclic nucleotide featuring a uracil base linked to a ribose sugar with a 3,5-cyclic phosphate bridge, its core structure is uracil (pyrimidine-2,4-dione) attached to a ribose sugar that contains an unusual cyclic phosphate ester, with the formula C9H10N2NaO9P, structurally, it retains the cyclic nucleotide scaffold but has a pyrimidine core (uracil) instead of the purine found in cAMP/cGMP, lacking amine groups for altered hydrogen bonding, in biology, nucleotides and their cyclic derivatives play crucial roles in cellular signaling, cUMP is an established second messenger, similar to cAMP and cGMP, involved in cell metabolism and regulation, this specific molecule (sodium 1-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-7-hydroxy-2-oxido-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]pyrimidine-2,4-dione), where the phosphate group forms a five-membered ring by linking to two oxygen atoms on the sugar ring, may be designed for specific cell membrane permeability or metabolic stability, functionally, it acts as a rare cyclic nucleotide signaling modulator, binds specific nucleotide receptors/riboswitches, probes pyrimidine-based signaling pathways, and aids studies of cyclic nucleotide diversity, with potential roles in regulating bacterial/viral nucleic acid metabolism and gene expression via ligand-induced RNA conformational shifts, it is primarily studied in biomedicine as a signaling molecule, enzyme inhibitor, or a tool for research into nucleic acid metabolism.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis[4-[[4-amino-6-(prop-2-ynylamino)-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]butyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide, also referred to as Bisamidinium 1b, is a highly symmetrical organic molecule featuring a benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide core linked via butyl chains to complex 1,3,5-triazine rings, with prop-2-ynyl (alkyne) groups enabling Click Chemistry applications. The core diamidine structure is well-known in medicinal chemistry for potent antimicrobial, antiprotozoal, and antiparasitic activities, while triazine derivatives are commonly found in anticancer drugs and functional biological molecules. Functionally, 1b retains the RNA-binding capability of its monomeric precursor 1a, effectively targeting expanded r(CUG) repeats (r(CUG)) implicated in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) , and serves as a precursor in copper-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition to synthesize dimeric ligands with drastically enhanced inhibitory activity against the MBNL1-r(CUG) complex. Additionally, it maintains low cytotoxicity and favorable solubility as a tetrahydrochloride salt, supporting its potential use as a multifunctional biological probe, conjugating agent for targeted delivery or bioimaging, and a candidate for anti-infective or antitumor applications.

RNA information: XIST is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) crucial for X-chromosome inactivation in female mammalian cells. It functions by coating the future inactive X chromosome and recruiting repressive complexes to silence gene expression. Structurally, XIST contains a non-canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) motif composed of only two G-tetrads, making it less stable than canonical three-tetrad rG4s. In vitro studies, such as reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays, confirm its ability to form an rG4 structure, showing approximately 14% stalling in potassium buffer. Notably, specific G4 ligands like PhenDC3 and PDS can act as molecular chaperones, significantly enhancing the formation and stability of the XIST rG4 structure under unfavorable ionic conditions, highlighting its dynamic nature and regulatory potential.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(2-aminoethoxy)-2-N,6-N-bis[4-(2-aminoethoxy)naphthalen-2-yl]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide, also referred to as PhenDC3, is a large, structurally symmetrical bisquinolinium G4 ligand (a pyridodicarboxamide derivative) with the molecular formula C37H37N5O5, centered on a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core; this core is linked via two amide bonds to two identical naphthyl units, which are functionalized with aminoethoxy chains, and its structure further enables chemical derivatization (e.g., PhenDC3-alk for bioorthogonal manipulations). The molecule features multiple aromatic rings (pyridine and naphthyl) providing a hydrophobic, planar surface that allows it to bind rG4s via stacking on G-quartets, and four primary amine groups (-O-CH2CH2NH2) contributing to hydrophilicity and multiple positive charges; this combination of hydrophobicity and multiple positive charges is characteristic of amphiphilic molecules, often designed as DNA minor groove binders or transmembrane transport vectors, and it can interact with DNA bases via - stacking interactions while electrostatically attracting the negatively charged phosphate backbone. Functionally, it acts as a molecular chaperone, inducing folding of canonical (3-quartet, e.g., TERRA) and non-canonical (2-quartet, e.g., XIST) rG4s, stabilizes rG4s and impedes reverse transcriptase by promoting rG4 formation, aiding G4 biology studies, and consequently, this compound has potential as an anti-tumor or antiviral drug by interfering with the replication or transcription of nucleic acids.

RNA information: The (5'CNG/3'CNG) motif refers to repetitive RNA sequences where N is any nucleotide, commonly found in transcript regions like 5' or 3' UTRs. These repeats can form stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures, influencing post-transcriptional gene regulation. They are particularly significant in microsatellite expansion disorders, where expanded CUG or CAG repeats (a subtype of CNG) are pathogenic. For example, in myotonic dystrophy type 1, expanded CUG repeats sequester RNA-binding proteins like MBNL1, disrupting alternative splicing and leading to disease. Their structural integrity is thus crucial for function and dysfunction.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

RNA information: TSL2 is a stem-loop RNA structure overlapping the SMN2 exon 7 5' splice site. Its conformation regulates splicing accessibility—destabilizing mutations or ligand binding (e.g., PK4C9) promotes exon inclusion by exposing the 5' ss.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-(4-carbamimidoylanilino)phenyl]-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide is an indole bis-amidine derivative, containing an indole core, an aniline bridge, and two amidine groups. Indole compounds are widely found in natural products and possess various biological activities. The amidine groups impart a potential for nucleic acid binding, and the molecule is often developed as an antiprotozoal and antiviral agent. Bis-amidine-indole compounds with similar structures have been studied for the treatment of tropical diseases like African trypanosomiasis or as DNA minor groove binding probes.

Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

RNA information: The r(CCUG)₁₀ sequence models the pathogenic RNA associated with myotonic dystrophy type 2. Structurally, it contains four-nucleotide repeats that also produce hairpins with mismatches, although their geometry differs slightly from CUG repeats. These motifs create similar alternating stripes of electrostatic potential, making selectivity between CUG and CCUG challenging for both proteins and small molecules. Functionally, r(CCUG)₁₀ sequesters MBNL1 in a manner comparable to CUG repeats, contributing to splicing defects. Its inclusion in binding studies demonstrates the difficulty of achieving discrimination between closely related repeat RNAs and emphasizes the need for ligands that exploit subtle structural differences.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 5 (2012), is the E-isomer counterpart of compound 4, differing only in the configuration of the olefin bridge, and this geometric change alters the positioning of the benzo[g]quinoline group relative to the peptide scaffold, leading to slightly different recognition properties, while it retains overall high binding capability, though its cooperativity and selectivity are reduced compared to the Z-isomer, and structurally, it highlights how subtle stereochemical differences strongly affect RNA recognition, and biologically, it is still active in cellular models and displaces pathogenic complexes, demonstrating that both isomers contribute valuable insights into structureactivity relationships for RNA-binding molecules; it is a highly complex, multi-cationic peptidomimetic structure, its core is the (E)-oct-4-enediamide scaffold, with side chains derived from proline, 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl, and lysine, the molecule contains multiple primary amino groups, rendering it poly-cationic at physiological pH, the 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline is a bulky, highly hydrophobic aromatic group, this combination of poly-cationic character, multiple chiral centers, and large hydrophobic groups makes it suitable as an anti-tumor agent, a DNA/RNA binding agent, or a novel antibiotic, and the (E)-alkene provides a specific, rigid conformation.

Small_molecule_information: acridine-3,6-diamine, also referred to as Proflavine (commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine), is commonly known as 3,6-Diaminoacridine, a representative acridine dye, structured as a tricyclic acridine core substituted by amino groups at positions 3 and 6, molecular formula C13H11N3, and Proflavine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, the acridine scaffold is characterized by extreme planarity and conjugation, in terms of real-world activity, acridine compounds are indispensable tools and drugs in biology and medicine, its most famous application is as a fluorescent stain for nucleic acids; it intercalates into the DNA and RNA double helix, emitting different colors of fluorescence depending on the nucleic acid type and aggregation state, widely used for cell cycle analysis and nucleic acid quantification, as a pharmaceutical agent, acridine dyes, due to their potent DNA intercalation and disruption effects, are developed as broad-spectrum disinfectants/antiseptics (e.g., acriflavine) and cytotoxic anticancer drugs, Proflavine can induce significant structural changes in the RNA, has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA and can activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, these characteristics indicate that Proflavine is an important tool for studying the functional mechanism of 108yjdF RNA. RNA

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 2-amino-1-N,4-N-bis(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound P1, belongs to the bis-amidines class of derivatives, with a structure containing two amidine groups, one amino group, and two amide bonds linked to a terephthalamide backbone, while it retains the twin benzamidine warheads of pentamidine and is decorated with an extended hydrophobic linker that increases its shape complementarity to the deep major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat, and it is a pentamidine-like small molecule identified through 3-D shape-based similarity screening as a next-generation ligand for the toxic r(CUG)exp RNA that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Bis-amidine compounds typically exhibit broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity, with their mechanism of action often involving binding to microbial DNA, and this compound can dissociate MBNL1 from r(CUG) repeat RNA with higher potency than pentamidine itself and shows high selectivity among tested analogues, preferring RNA containing multiple DM1 motifs over fully paired RNA, the human A-site or MBNL1 protein, while in relevant DM1-cell models it can shift a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon skipped DsRed signal (though higher concentrations are cytotoxic), and it exemplifies how computational shape similarity can rapidly evolve an established antimicrobial template into a selective, low-micromolar chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a starting point for in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics. Due to its highly cationic nature, the molecule is also studied as an anticancer or antiviral drug, acting by interfering with nucleic acid metabolism or specific enzyme functions. Structurally, the molecule is designed as a potential DNA minor groove binder or ion channel inhibitor, but detailed public activity data is lacking, while it has been verified to target DM1-related toxic RNA and regulate MBNL1-related reporter signals in cell models.

RNA information: B4-25 is a 100-nucleotide RNA aptamer isolated from a random-sequence pool by Ellington & Szostak through in vitro selection against Reactive Blue 4; it contains a 28-nt highly conserved core that folds into a stem–loop–stem motif predicted by GCG-FOLD, presenting a pre-organized pocket whose planar, negatively charged surface complements the anthraquinone and sulfonate groups of the dye, achieving ~600 fM affinity and >50 % column retention with virtually no cross-binding to other dyes.
Small_molecule_information: 1-amino-4-[4-[[4-chloro-6-(4-sulfonatoanilino)-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]-3-sulfonatoanilino]-9,10-dioxoanthracene-2-sulfonate, also referred to as Cibacron Blue 3G-A, is extremely similar in structure to 1-amino-4-[3-[[4-chloro-6-(3-sulfonatoanilino)-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl]amino]-4-sulfonatoanilino]-9,10-dioxoanthracene-2-sulfonate, also being a trisulfonate salt containing an anthraquinone dye core, and Cibacron Blue 3G-A is the dye employed in the EllingtonSzostak selection, is an anthraquinone-derived organosulfonate that carries two sulfonate and one reactive chlorotriazine groups, the molecules main activities focus on serving as a dye or fluorescent reagent, or non-specific inhibitor of nucleic acids or enzymes in biology, its planar, amphiphilic scaffold mimics the ADP-ribose fold of NAD(P), allowing the 22-nt conserved domain of the CB-42 RNA aptamer to wrap around the dye through a combination of stacking, hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions that yield extremely high binding affinity. RNA

Small_molecule_information: 2-amino-4-oxo-3,7-dihydropyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidine-5-carbonitrile, also referred to as preQ0 (7-aminomethyl-7-deazaguanine), is a compound containing the pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidine scaffold, which is a structural analog of natural nucleobases (like adenine and guanine), with the formula C7H5N5O, and preQ0 (7-aminomethyl-7-deazaguanine) is a modified purine nucleobase precursor to preQ1 and queuosine, featuring a deazapurine core with an aminomethyl group at the 7-position (replacing the typical purine N7), structurally, it retains the bicyclic purine scaffold but has a unique carbon-linked amine side chain, enabling specific riboswitch recognition, this compound is generally designed as an antimetabolite or nucleotide metabolism inhibitor, its biological activity stems from its ability to mimic natural substrates, potentially interfering with DNA and RNA synthesis by incorporating into nucleic acid chains or inhibiting key enzymes, functionally, preQ0 acts as a key intermediate in queuosine biosynthesis, binds dedicated preQ0 riboswitches to regulate related metabolic genes, and mediates bacterial gene expression via ligand-induced RNA conformational changes, ensuring proper tRNA modification and translational fidelity, it is a focus in the development of broad-spectrum antiviral, anticancer, and immunosuppressive agents. tRNA

RNA information: (5'CUG/3'GUC)x6: This RNA motif contains CUG repeats, which are associated with myotonic dystrophy type 1. It serves as a negative control to test the specificity of compounds designed for CAG repeats. The study shows that the lead compounds have low affinity for this RNA, confirming selectivity for CAG-rich structures.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 3, shares the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one core with 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one but lacks the bromine atom, and Compound 3 emerged as a lead compound with high affinity and selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA, its large aromatic and amino-substituted structure suggests high lipophilicity and the potential to interact strongly with biological targets, it is likely investigated as an antiproliferative agent targeting intracellular enzymes or signaling pathways, Compound 3 significantly reduced polyQ aggregation in cellular models, showed low cytotoxicity, and was cell-permeable, localizing primarily in the cytoplasm. RNA information: No loop (loopless RNA), also known as 5'CAG/3'CUG and 5'CAG/3'GUC, is an RNA with a hairpin structure. Structurally, its stem has fully complementary base pairs (no mismatches/deletions) and no internal loops, showing high conformational stability without inter-strand "breathing effect" as a simplified dsRNA model. Functionally, it serves as a key experimental control to distinguish small molecules’ specific/non-specific binding to RNA, with no direct link to specific diseases.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 20 (DAPI, 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole), is a compound containing an indole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, one imidamide directly attached to the indole ring and the other attached via a phenyl ring at the C2 position of indole, with the formula C16H14N4, and it is also a commercially available guanidino/amidino-containing compound, a routine nuclear stain and chemical progenitor of derivative D6; this indole diamidine combines the lipophilicity of indole with the cationic nature of the diamidine groups, and it can bind influenza A RNA promoter (causes NMR perturbations), AT-rich dsDNA minor groove, and r(CAG)exps 5CAG/3GAC loop, disrupt r(CAG)expMBN1, correct splicing defects in Huntingtons/ataxia type 3 models though it has cytotoxicity that limits direct optimization, this combination also results in an effective DNA minor groove binder with potent antimicrobial and antitumor activity, and it is researched for developing new anticancer agents with high target specificity and enhanced pharmacokinetic profiles.

Small_molecule_information: 3-[[11-[3-(dimethylamino)propylamino]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]propyl-trimethylazanium diiodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine and quaternary ammonium salt side chains. The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

Small_molecule_information: (2R,3R,4R,5R)-4-amino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(1R,2S)-2-amino-6-(aminomethyl)cyclohexyl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-5-methyloxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as Sisomicin Sulphate, is a complex aminoglycoside compound featuring multiple sugar rings and an aminocyclohexitol structure linked by glycosidic bonds. The molecule contains numerous amine (NH2) groups, rendering it polycationic at physiological pH. Aminoglycosides are a critical class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that function by irreversibly binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, disrupting protein synthesis by binding to rRNA, inhibiting protein synthesis. Similar to clinical antibiotics like Streptomycin or Gentamicin, this molecule is primarily used to treat severe Gram-negative bacterial infections, though its therapeutic use must consider inherent ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity side effects.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6: This is a six-repeat control RNA with perfect Watson-Crick base pairing. It is used to evaluate the specificity of compound binding and shows minimal structural perturbation upon ligand interaction, confirming that small molecules do not significantly bind to regular duplex RNA.; The (5'CAG/3'GUC)x6 sequence serves as the extended control duplex. Its repetitive yet perfectly paired structure lacks the distinctive features of disease-repeat RNA, allowing researchers to conclusively prove that compound efficacy is due to specific recognition of the pathological mismatch motif and not general RNA binding.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2-fluorophenyl)-3-methylquinoline, also referred to as CP13, is a fluorine-containing aromatic heterocyclic compound. Its structure uses the quinoline core, with a 2-fluorophenyl substituent at the 2-position and a methyl group at the 3-position. The molecular formula is CHFN. The compound possesses a planar conjugated system, and the fluorine atom alters the molecules electron distribution. Its optimal structure allows for strong -stacking and specific interactions with the C4A5G6 triad, resulting in effective RNA stabilization and powerful neuroprotective effects in cells, establishing it as the most promising lead. In terms of biological function, quinoline compounds generally possess diverse biological activities, including antibacterial, antifungal, antimalarial, anthelmintic, antispasmodic, cardiotonic, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic effects, and the introduction of a fluorine atom is typically used to enhance the compounds metabolic stability or improve its membrane permeability.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-3-[1-[(3S)-4-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-3-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-4-oxobutyl]triazol-4-yl]-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 5, is a complex, linear peptidic analog and a triazole analog with an N-methylation pattern distinct from compound 4, it has binding capacity that is affected by the presence of tRNA, strongly reduced infectivity of pseudotyped HIV and inhibited replication of HIVIIIB, notably, it was essentially nontoxic at high concentrations, making it the safest compound in the series, and its favorable combination of moderate binding, strong antiviral activity, and low toxicity identifies it as a promising lead for therapeutic development; its structure is similar to N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-3-[1-[(3S)-4-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-3-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-4-oxobutyl]triazol-4-yl]-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, featuring two units containing (2S)-phenylalaninyl-3-aminopropylamine and (2S)-proline fragments linked by a 1,4-disubstituted 1,2,3-triazole ring, with both termini acylated by a 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl group, the connectivity of the two units to the triazole ring is reversed compared to N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-3-[1-[(3S)-4-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-3-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-4-oxobutyl]triazol-4-yl]-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, but the substitution pattern of the two units themselves (one containing methylcarbamoyl, the other containing carbamoyl) is the same, it incorporates the benzo[g]quinoline aromatic heterocycle and contains multiple chiral centers and amide bonds, this compound adopts a non-planar, flexible conformation, the benzo[g]quinoline core confers stronger aromatic stacking and hydrophobic interaction capabilities, and the 3-aminopropylamine group enhances hydrophilicity and cationic properties, in terms of biological function, peptides and their analogs generally have the potential to act as receptor ligands, enzyme inhibitors, or signaling molecules, and benzo[g]quinoline compounds typically possess diverse biological activities, with this compound specifically exhibiting potent anti-HIV activity and low toxicity.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 6-amino-4-oxo-1-[3-(pyridin-2-ylamino)propyl]-7-(4-pyridin-2-ylpiperazin-1-yl)quinoline-3-carboxylicacid This is a quinoline-3-carboxylic acid derivative featuring a 4-pyridin-2-ylpiperazine substituent at position 7, which lends the molecule considerable basicity and steric bulk. Its substituent at the N1 position is a 3-(pyridin-2-ylamino)propyl chain. Compared to the double-piperazine analogues, this structure employs a simpler secondary amine-linked pyridine ring at N1. This structural simplification is intended to optimize the molecules binding geometry within the target binding pocket and potentially alter its metabolic rate. Based on its quinolone core, this compound may be researched for antimicrobial or anti-cancer applications, with the side-chain design tailored to explore its selectivity for specific receptors or enzymes.

RNA information: (CUG)₅₀ is a toxic CUG trinucleotide repeat RNA linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it adopts a hairpin structure with multiple 1×1 UU internal loops (its key RNA Motif) and has the sequence 5'-r(CUG)₅₀-3'.Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1, causing RNA mis-splicing; it’s used as a model to screen ligands (e.g., pentamidine, EBAB) via affinity capillary electrophoresis (ACE) for DM1 therapy .
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-acetamidophenyl)-2-[(4-chlorobenzoyl)amino]-1,3-thiazole-4-carboxamide, also referred to as entamidine (PTMD), is a complex thiazole derivative belonging to the amide class of compounds, in which the 1,3-thiazole-4-carboxamide core bears a (4-chlorobenzoyl)amino group at the 2-position and a 3-acetamidophenyl group attached to the nitrogen atom of the carboxamide at the 4-position, and while such a combination of a thiazole ring, chlorinated benzoyl moiety, and acetamidophenyl substituent suggests potential anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, or kinase-inhibition activities, this compound additionally exhibits bisamidinium-like polar interaction properties characteristic of entamidine, functioning as an antiprotozoal/antifungal agent that can reduce DM1 nuclear foci in HeLa cells but displays high toxicity, making it useful as a positive control in DM1 drug-screening assays.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: acridin-9-amine is a famous DNA intercalator. The molecule exerts its effect by inserting itself between the base pairs of the DNA double helix. Historically, it has been used as a topical antiseptic and antibiotic. In molecular biology, it is an important fluorescent dye and mutagen, used to study DNA structure, replication, and repair, and is also applied in mutagenesis studies; it binds nucleic acids, disrupting replication and transcription, and is explored for anticancer potential. Its derivatives are used in antimalarial and antirheumatic treatment.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-methylbenzenesulfonate;(2Z)-3-methyl-2-[(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylidene]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as Thiazole Orange, is an organic salt containing quinolinium and benzothiazole heterocyclic rings that intercalates into DNA and RNA, commonly used in flow cytometry and molecular biology for detecting nucleic acids; its structure is based on the quinoline and benzothiazole parent skeletons: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, and a benzene ring is fused with a thiazole ring to form the benzothiazole skeleton, with these two parent nuclei connected by a methine group. In terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically functions as dyes, photosensitizers, or exhibits specific biological enzyme inhibitory activities (Benzothiazoles are important in therapeutic chemistry).

RNA information: XIST is a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) crucial for X-chromosome inactivation in female mammalian cells. It functions by coating the future inactive X chromosome and recruiting repressive complexes to silence gene expression. Structurally, XIST contains a non-canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) motif composed of only two G-tetrads, making it less stable than canonical three-tetrad rG4s. In vitro studies, such as reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays, confirm its ability to form an rG4 structure, showing approximately 14% stalling in potassium buffer. Notably, specific G4 ligands like PhenDC3 and PDS can act as molecular chaperones, significantly enhancing the formation and stability of the XIST rG4 structure under unfavorable ionic conditions, highlighting its dynamic nature and regulatory potential.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,6-N-bis(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-3-yl)pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide, also referred to as 360A, is a pyridodicarboxamide (PDC) derivative and an early prototype of G4 ligands initially identified by Sanofi-Aventis, its chemistry is well-developed, with conjugates like PDC-biotin (for SELEX), PDC-TO (fluorophore), BrdU-linked (visualization), and PDC-XL (cross-linking), and functionally, tritiated forms of this compound aid in demonstrating telomeric G4 existence and impact telomere integrity, while it shows some renaturing activity on rG4s but has limited molecular chaperone ability on unusual rG4s compared to PhenDC3 and PDS; this is a dicationic compound with a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core, linking two 1-methylquinolin-1-ium cationic groups via two amide bonds, this rigid, highly conjugated molecule is itself a well-known G-quadruplex (G4) stabilizer ligand, its two positive charges and planar structure allow it to interact efficiently with nucleic acids, the real-world application of this compound is mainly focused on anticancer drug development and biomolecular fluorescent sensing, as a G4 stabilizer, it selectively binds to and stabilizes G4 structures found in the promoter regions of oncogenes (like cMYC) or telomeric DNA, thereby inhibiting oncogene expression or interfering with telomerase activity, achieving an anti-tumor effect.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-(7-aza-4-azoniaspiro[2.5]octan-7-yl)-2-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidin-4-one, also referred to as SMSM11, is another derivative based on the pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidin-4-one scaffold, characterized by the unique spiro-substituent at position 7: the 7-aza-4-azoniaspiro[2.5]octan-7-yl group, while SMSM11 is a non-planar coumarin derivative with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system bearing a 1 charge that binds the single-G bulge of RNA1 via minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, validated by FP and SAR comparison, offering modest selectivity for bulged-G RNA, this spirocyclic structure of the derivative, formed by the fusion of a three-membered ring and a six-membered ring sharing a single atom, introduces significantly greater geometric rigidity and specificity compared to simple cyclic substituents, the spiro-quaternary ammonium center is positively charged, intended to improve the compounds pharmacokinetic profile and reinforce electrostatic binding to the target, the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group, an active pharmacophore, is connected at position 2 of the pyrido-pyrimidine core, and the incorporation of a spirocyclic moiety in drug design is often a strategy to enhance binding selectivity and affinity or to overcome drug resistance, highlighting its potential utility in novel anti-tumor drug discovery.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Kaempferol is found in a variety of plant foods, such as tea, broccoli, grapefruit, and herbs. Functionally, as a dietary flavonol, Kaempferol possesses significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and potential anticancer properties, it modulates cell signaling pathways (e.g., PI3K/Akt) and is studied for cardiovascular and neurodegenerative disease prevention, research also suggests it can regulate apoptosis, inhibit angiogenesis, and suppress cell proliferation, thereby potentially inhibiting tumor development, its role in the prevention of chronic diseases and the maintenance of cardiovascular health is particularly studied, although its bioactivity may differ from other flavonols due to the single hydroxyl group on its B ring.

It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). Small_molecule_information: 3-methyl-2-pyridin-4-ylquinoline, also referred to as CP4, is a nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocyclic compound consisting of a naphthyridine fused heterocycle with methyl and pyridine substitutions; its structure uses the quinoline core, with a pyridin-4-yl substituent at the 2-position and a methyl group at the 3-position, the molecular formula is CHN, this compound contains two nitrogen heterocyclic rings and has an extensive conjugated system, in terms of biological function, quinoline compounds generally possess diverse biological activities, and the introduction of the pyridine ring is often used to enhance coordination capability and modify biological activity.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 4-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-4-yl)methylideneamino]pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide diiodide, also referred to as PymDH1, is structurally similar to 4-N,6-N-bis[(E)-(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylideneamino]pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide diiodide, featuring a pyrimidine-4,6-dicarboxamide core but linked to two 1-methylpyridin-1-ium cationic groups, and PymDH1 is a bis(acylhydrazone) with a central pyrimidine core and 1-methylpyridinium lateral groups, it adopted a linear conformation, compared to the quinolinium, the pyridinium group is smaller but retains strong cationic properties, the polycationic and conjugated structure of this compound makes it an efficient fluorescent chemical sensor, in real-world applications, this class of compounds is extensively researched for selective recognition and fluorescent detection of biologically relevant molecules in aqueous solution, such as nucleoside triphosphates (ATP/GTP) or specific anions, its fluorescence signal changes significantly upon binding to the target molecule, giving it significant value in bioanalytical chemistry, PymDH1 showed very poor G4 binding and no biological activity.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₆ oligonucleotide is a key in vitro model used to study the pathogenic CUG repeat expansion. Its repetitive sequence forms a stable stem-loop structure characterized by canonical GC base pairs and non-canonical, dynamic UU mismatches. This unique structural motif is the primary binding site for both the Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein and the designed small molecule ligands. The RNA was synthetically labeled with a TAMRA fluorophore at its 5' end for binding assays. In these assays, ligand binding to the UU mismatches induces a structural change that quenches the TAMRA fluorescence, allowing the direct measurement of binding affinity (K_D), thus establishing r(CUG)₆ as a critical tool for quantifying ligand-RNA interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-2-carbonyl]amino]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethoxy]ethyl]acridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 12, is an extremely large, bifunctional molecule whose core structure consists of two acridine rings and is a dimeric ligand with an oligoether linker, its binding affinity was close to that of the monomer, it showed a small bivalent effect and no melting temperature data could be determined for this compound; Acridine is a tricyclic planar aromatic system, the parent structure of famous DNA intercalators, the two acridine units are linked by a flexible tetraethylene glycol (PEG-like) spacer, which enhances the molecules water solubility and biological flexibility, each acridine ring also carries a triazine-butane amine side chain, introducing additional basicity and nucleic acid binding capacity, the structure of this compound represents a classic dimerized drug design, aimed at enhancing its affinity for DNA and cytotoxicity through double intercalation or multivalency effects, in practice, this compound is researched as a potent anti-cancer agent, especially against multi-drug resistant (MDR) cell lines, or as a highly specific fluorescent probe.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: (7S,9S)-7-[(2R,4S,5R,6S)-4-amino-5-hydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxy-6,9,11-trihydroxy-9-(2-hydroxyacetyl)-4-methoxy-8,10-dihydro-7H-tetracene-5,12-dione, also referred to as Epirubicin, is the chemical name for, or a very close analogue of, the anthracycline antibiotic Epirubicin, which is an anthracycline chemotherapeutic agent that intercalates into DNA, inhibiting topoisomerase II and causing DNA strand breaks; Epirubicin is a widely used chemotherapy drug in clinical practice for treating various cancers such as breast cancer and ovarian cancer, and its mechanism involves DNA intercalation and Topoisomerase II inhibition.

Small_molecule_information: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroacridin-9-amine, also referred to as 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroacridine-9-amine, is a hydrogenated acridine derivative with the acridine core, where the benzene ring is fused with a dihydropyridine ring, and the 1,2,3,4 positions are hydrogenated, with an amino group at position 9, molecular formula C13H16N2; the compounds core is a partially saturated tricyclic acridine structure with some lipophilicity, and the amino group imparts basicity. In terms of real-world activity, such acridine derivatives, due to their unique planar tricyclic structure and amino side chain, are often studied as DNA intercalators, showing potential in anticancer and antibacterial fields; specifically, this compound shares structural similarities with the well-known Alzheimers drug tacrine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor), which makes it a derivative of tacrine with potential acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity, suggesting its potential for Central Nervous System activity as a candidate for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimers disease treatment, and it may also possess topoisomerase inhibitory activity, making it valuable in antitumor drug development.

RNA information: (CCUG)₈ is an RNA oligonucleotide modeling the pathogenic expanded repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 2 (DM2). It forms a stable hairpin structure with internal loops and serves as a target for evaluating ligand selectivity. Some small molecules can bind and cleave (CCUG)₈, while others  show no activity, highlighting its role in distinguishing DM2-specific targeting.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[2-aminoethyl-[2-[[2-(1H-imidazol-5-yl)acetyl]amino]ethyl]amino]ethyl]-9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as the imidazole-added acridine-triazine conjugate, is a compound that integrates Acridine (DNA intercalation), Triazine (hydrogen bonding recognition), and an Imidazole functional group, and this imidazole-added acridine-triazine design binds and cleaves both (CUG) and (CCUG) repeats effectively, inhibits transcription moderately, and shows strong bioactivity in cellular and fly models, the imidazole ring, key to molecules like Histamine and Histidine, often serves as a site for proton shuttle and acid-base catalysis in biological systems, this polyfunctional design suggests the compound aims to be a molecule with multiple targeting capabilities, for example, acridine targets nucleic acids, while the imidazole ring may be designed to target specific metalloenzymes (like zinc finger proteins) or G-quadruplex structures, its activity is thus focused on novel anticancer agents or molecular tools for investigating complex enzyme reactions and nucleic acid structures. --

Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-methylbenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 2, which is structurally identical to the aforementioned compound but lacks the chlorine atom at the 9-position, exhibited selective binding to r(CAG)exp RNA with improved affinity compared to some analogs and was synthesized with a methyl substitution on the benzoyl ring that contributed to its RNA-binding properties without significant cytotoxicity; its core is the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold, substituted with a 3-amino group, a 4-phenyl group, and a 2-4-methylbenzoyl group, and this planar, multi-ring system is investigated for its potential as a kinase inhibitor or PDE inhibitor, often in anti-tumor or anti-inflammatory research, serving as a fundamental analog to determine the necessity and role of substituents (such as the 9-chloro group in the related compound) on the biological activity profile.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,9-N-bis(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-3-yl)-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide, also referred to as PhenDC3, is also a dicationic compound, built on a 1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide core, linking two 1-methylquinolin-1-ium cationic groups via amide bonds, and it is a well-known G4 ligand used as a positive control that showed very strong G4 stabilization and high FID affinity, enhanced EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation in a GAr-dependent manner but exhibited significant cytotoxicity at certain concentrations, the phenanthroline core provides a larger, more rigid planar structure, the structural features of this compound make it an efficient DNA intercalator and metal ion coordinating agent, in practical applications, its strong fluorescence properties and nucleic acid affinity make it a molecular fluorescent probe, suitable for live-cell imaging and nucleic acid quantification, furthermore, it holds potential in chemotherapy, inducing cancer cell death by intercalating into DNA and possibly generating oxidative stress upon coordination with metal ions (like copper or iron).

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carbonyl]amino]ethylamino]ethylamino]ethylamino]ethyl]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Acridine 10, is an acridine-triazine dimer with acridine substituted at position 4, and the linker is a longer polyethylamino chain containing four ethylamino units (an oligoamino linker), molecular formula C60H67N21O2, and this potent dimeric ligand demonstrated strong binding and inhibition with a notable Tm, a much lower KD than the monomer, and a significant bivalent effect. In terms of real-world activity, its core function is as an ultra-potent DNA intercalator/cross-linker and topoisomerase inhibitor. Compared to 9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]-N-[2-[2-[2-[[9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carboxamide, the increase in linker chain length means the molecule carries more positive charges, which generally further enhances its non-specific electrostatic affinity for DNA and may alter the span across the DNA double helix, affecting DNA cross-linking efficiency and topoisomerase toxicity. The synthesis and study of such compounds aim to systematically explore the influence of linker structure on the activity and pharmacokinetics of multi-target drugs, to develop more effective anticancer therapies.

RNA information: RNA1 contains the 1x1 nucleotide UU internal loop motif that is present within the Drosha processing site of pri-miR-96. It serves as the specific binding target for compound 1, with a Kd of 1,300 nM, but does not bind compound 2.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-azidoethyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2, is an azide-tagged bis-benzimidazole probe featuring a short two-carbon (ethyl) linker, which is a bis-benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module that Informa predicted and binding studies confirmed selectively targets a 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loop adjacent to the Drosha site in pri-miR-96, while it binds its target RNA motif, it also exhibits high, undesired affinity for an AT-rich DNA hairpin and does not effectively inhibit miR-96 biogenesis in cellular assays when used as a monomer; the shorter linker alters the spatial presentation of the azide group for the subsequent Click Chemistry reaction, and its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for DNA binding and labeling studies, used to investigate the impact of a close-to-core tag on the probes biological performance.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purine-6-carbonitrile, also referred to as 6-cyanopurine, is a simple purine derivative where the C6 position is substituted with a cyano group, with the formula C6H3N5, and 6-cyanopurine is a purine analog featuring a purine bicyclic core (fused pyrimidine and imidazole rings) with a cyano (-CN) group at the 6-position, replacing the typical carbonyl or amino moiety of natural purines, the cyano group (CN) is often used in medicinal chemistry as a prodrug or a potential covalent enzyme inhibitor, structurally, it retains the planar aromatic heterocyclic scaffold of purines while introducing a polar cyano functional group that enhances hydrogen bonding potential, this compound is an important intermediate for synthesizing other biologically active purine nucleoside analogs, functionally, it acts as a potential disrupter of purine metabolism, thereby influencing normal cellular processes and exhibiting possible antiviral or antiproliferative activity, it also acts as a nucleotide metabolism modulator, competes with endogenous purines for binding to purine-associated enzymes or riboswitches, and serves as a tool for studying purine-dependent pathways, with potential applications in antiviral or anticancer drug development due to its ability to disrupt nucleic acid synthesis and gene regulation. 6-

Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-(5-methyl-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)propane-1,3-diamine iodide, this compound is an indolo[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 3-N,3-N,7-N,7-N-tetramethyl-4-nitro-3H-phenothiazine-3,7-diamine, also referred to as the compound with a tricyclic phenothiazine core where a sulfur atom bridges two benzene rings, bearing 3,7-tetramethyldiamino groups and a 4-nitro substituent and exhibiting aromatic, electron-deficient, and polar features, is a nitro-substituted phenothiazine derivative, structurally similar to Methylene Blue; functionally, no links to RNA targets (e.g., pre-miR-21, r(GC)) or related biological activities (e.g., RAN translation inhibition) are reported, leaving its specific roles uncharacterized in the given context, and it is used in reality as a redox indicator, biological stain, antimalarial drug, and a potential therapeutic for inhibiting Tau protein aggregation in Alzheimers disease.
