Small_molecule_information: 24-methyl-5,7,18,20-tetraoxa-24-azoniahexacyclo[11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21]tetracosa-1(24),2,4(8),9,11,13,15,17(21),22-nonaene, also referred to as Sanguinarine, is a highly complex, polycyclic cationic compound, which can be chemically identified as the N-methyl derivative of a macrocyclic tetraoxaza-heteroatom system, and its molecular structure is dominated by its extensive ring fusion as indicated by its IUPAC name, and Sanguinarine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant structural changes in the RNA, Sanguinarine is also a benzophenanthridine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and pro-apoptotic effects, it targets multiple cellular pathways, including NF-B, and is studied for anticancer applications despite potential toxicity concerns, the compounds formula, C21H16NO4, reveals a structure containing 21 carbon atoms, 4 oxygen atoms, and a single nitrogen atom that carries a permanent positive charge (azonia) due to methylation, the core hexacyclic structure suggests a rigid, cage-like framework featuring two major 12-membered rings bridged by various fused smaller rings (implied by the [11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21] nomenclature), leading to a highly planar and stiff molecular geometry, the presence of four oxygen atoms and nine double bonds (nonaene) indicates extensive unsaturation, and the presence of oxygen atoms at positions 5, 7, 18, and 20 suggests they are likely incorporated into ether or furan-like structures, strongly suggesting this molecule is an analog of macrocyclic dyes or natural products with rigid, fluorescent properties, potentially related to porphyrins or cryptands in shape, although Sanguinarine has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, it fails to activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, possibly due to its toxicity to cells, nevertheless, Sanguinarine, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch, due to its fixed positive charge and large, planar aromatic system, this compound is highly likely to function as an intercalating agent for nucleic acids, making it a potential DNA/RNA binding fluorescent probe for biological imaging, or a promising scaffold for anti-cancer or antimicrobial drug development, where its rigidity could confer high target specificity.

RNA information: GGh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Small_molecule_information: 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2,6-dimethylphenyl]phenol, also referred to as Compound 6b, is another highly complex and large tetracationic polyamine, containing four primary amine groups and a phenol functional group, which was synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki coupling reactions, binds to HIV-1 RRE Bh with high affinity and can induce loop conformational changes, potently inhibits the relevant protein-RNA interaction and HIV-1 replication, and blocks Rev-mediated RNA transport; the presence of the phenol confers potential for antioxidant or kinase inhibitor activity, and unlike simple linear polyamines, this complex rigid scaffold gives it a unique spatial conformation, while in practical applications, polyamines with such complex, highly charged, large structures may be explored for highly specific nucleic acid binding or as multivalent ligands for binding specific cell surface receptors or metal ions (chelating agents).

Small_molecule_information: 9-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-2,7-dihydroxy-2-sulfanylidene-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]-2-amino-1H-purin-6-one;N,N-diethylethanamine, also referred to as Guanosine-3,5-cyclic monophosphorothioate (sp-isomer), is a guanosine thiophosphate nucleoside derivative, comprising a guanine base and a thiophosphate sugar ring, with N,N-diethylethanamine acting as a counterion or co-crystal component (molecular formula C10H12N5O5PSC6H15N), and Guanosine-3,5-cyclic monophosphorothioate (sp-isomer) is a cyclic nucleotide analog with a sulfur substitution in its phosphate backbone (sp-configured phosphorothioate linkage), this compound contains chiral centers and is a biologically significant nucleoside or signaling molecule analog, structurally, it retains guanosines purine core and 3,5-cyclic scaffold but has modified phosphate chemistry for enhanced stability, such compounds are often used as research tools for enzyme (e.g., kinase) inhibition, as their structural similarity to natural nucleosides allows them to potentially interfere with intracellular signaling pathways or act as non-hydrolyzable analogs of high-energy phosphate bonds, functionally, it acts as a selective cGMP signaling modulator, targeting cyclic nucleotide-dependent pathways, resisting phosphodiesterase degradation, and serving as a tool to study cGMP-mediated cellular processes like signal transduction and gene regulation.

Its structure is based on the benzofuroquinoline scaffold, with the 5-position nitrogen quaternized, and the propane-amine group on the 7-position side chain also quaternized (trimethylammonium), with diiodide as the counterion (molecular formula C31H43N4O22I). This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and two positive charges. The structural difference from N-[7-[(1,1-dimethylpiperidin-1-ium-4-yl)methoxy]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]-N,N-diethylpropane-1,3-diamine diiodide may influence its spatial orientation and DNA binding mode, making it a powerful potential DNA-targeting anti-cancer agent.

Small_molecule_information: 2-amino-3H-pteridin-4-one, also referred to as Pterine, Pterin or Isoxanthopterin, is a planar azaaromatic heterocyclic compound consisting of a fused pyrimidine and pyrazine ring system with two nitrogen atoms in each ring and a basic heterocyclic structure, it is the core structural unit and core scaffold of folates, pterins and related molecules like folic acid (for one-carbon metabolism) and the essential biological cofactor Tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4), it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis while its core role is as a building block that forms the basis of biologically active molecules related to pigmentation and enzyme function in organisms, in addition, BH4 is a crucial cofactor for aromatic amino acid hydroxylases (like phenylalanine hydroxylase) and nitric oxide synthases, consequently, the compound is primarily studied in the context of enzyme cofactor replacements or modifiers, potentially for the treatment of metabolic diseases associated with BH4 deficiency, such as phenylketonuria.

This is a tripeptide-heterocycle conjugate containing a histidine residue, linked to the cationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium scaffold. The imidazole ring of the histidine residue imparts a unique pH-responsiveness, which is significant in biological systems. The practical application potential of this compound lies in utilizing the histidine property to develop pH-sensitive drug delivery systems or diagnostic probes. Due to the lower pH of many tumor environments and lysosomes, the compound may be designed to undergo structural change or release the active moiety under acidic conditions, thus achieving targeting of the tumor microenvironment, representing an advanced concept in chemotherapeutic drug design.

Small_molecule_information: (4S,4aS,5aS,6S,12aR)-4-(dimethylamino)-1,6,10,11,12a-pentahydroxy-6-methyl-3,12-dioxo-4,4a,5,5a-tetrahydrotetracene-2-carboxamide, also referred to as nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, is the core structure or a derivative of a tetracycline antibiotic, which is a broad-spectrum antibiotic that binds to the 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking tRNA attachment and inhibiting protein synthesis by interacting with bacterial rRNA but not directly with mRNA or other RNA types; tetracycline has a rigid hydrophobic skeleton with four rings connected together, the side chains of C-10 phenol dione and C-1 to C-3 triketone formamide provide negative charge and hydrogen bond donors, this planar aromatic region can be stacked at the single-stranded/double-stranded junction of RNA, meanwhile, magnesium ions mediate the electrostatic bridging between its dimethylamino group and the phosphate skeleton, inducing conformational distortion at the active site and demonstrating a non-competitive inhibitory function for Class I intron splicing; tetracyclines are clinically important broad-spectrum antibiotics used in reality to treat various bacterial infections like pneumonia, acne, and Lyme disease, by inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]-2-(trifluoromethyl)phenothiazine, also referred to as Trifluoperazine, a phenothiazine antipsychotic drug that primarily acts as a dopamine antagonist, is based on the phenothiazine tricyclic core, features an electron-withdrawing trifluoromethyl group at the 2-position which enhances its lipophilicity, and has a propyl chain connecting an N-methylpiperazine group at the 10-position nitrogen; structurally, this is characteristic of many classic antipsychotic drugs, and this compound is expected to show potent dopamine receptor antagonist activity, particularly towards the central nervous system D2 receptor, making it potentially useful for treating schizophrenia or for its sedative effects.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-N-[(4-chlorophenyl)methyl]-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 11, is a thienopyridine carboxamide derivative with a 4-chlorobenzyl side chain modified from initial active analogs, which features the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine core, with the carboxamide at the 2-position linked to a 4-chlorobenzyl group; this 4-chlorobenzyl group is a common lipophilic substituent used to improve cell permeability and metabolic stability, and this N-benzyl substituted amide structure is frequently observed in kinase inhibitors, consequently, this molecule is a strong candidate for a kinase inhibitor intended for anti-tumor or anti-inflammatory research, and it also binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key elements for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs with moderate affinity while lacking further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity, and its structure represents a typical lead compound in medicinal chemistry used to probe the activity of the thienopyridine scaffold against specific drug targets.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethyl)aniline, also referred to as the compound that shows moderate binding affinity with unspecified functional activity in Dicer inhibition assays, consists of the 2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine core linked via a methylene bridge to an aniline group; aniline is an aromatic amine where the nitrogen is directly attached to the benzene ring, which lowers the amines basicity, and aniline structures and their derivatives are crucial pharmacophores in many kinase inhibitors and anti-inflammatory drugs, making this molecule a likely candidate for a kinase inhibitor or a signaling pathway modulator, as many successful anticancer drugs utilize aniline-substituted aromatic systems to interact with the ATP-binding site of kinases

RNA information: This RNA forms a well-ordered binding pocket through interlocking structural motifs . In the presence of Mg²+, it can bind theophylline with high affinity  and exhibits molecular discrimination specificity towards caffeine.
Small_molecule_information: 1,3-dimethyl-7,9-dihydro-1H-purine-2,6,8(3H)-trione also referred to as 1,3-Dimethyluric acid is an oxopurine metabolite bearing carbonyls at C-2, C-6 and C-8 and methyl groups at N-1 and N-3, it is a theophylline derivative that represents the terminal oxidation product of theophylline in humans, differing from theophylline by an additional 8-position keto group and an N9 proton; introduction of the C-8 carbonyl forces the ring into the 7,9-dihydro-1H-purine-2,6,8(3H)-trione tautomer, enlarges the -system and creates a steric/electrostatic clash with the TCT8-4 RNA binding pocket, no specific biological function is mentioned; its key role is in studying RNA-ligand binding, it binds theophylline-binding RNA with much lower affinity than theophylline, its N9 proton disrupts the N9-U24 H-bond, and the 8-keto group causes steric interference which together with the steric/electrostatic clash with the TCT8-4 RNA binding pocket fails to stabilize RNAs core base triples, demonstrating that an unsubstituted C-8 position is essential for high-affinity recognition by the evolved RNA sensor, thus explaining the reduced binding.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2-chlorophenyl)quinolin-3-ol, also referred to as CP7, is a quinoline derivative (and a hydroxyquinoline derivative with a 2-chlorophenyl substitution) that has a classic intercalator motif which provided RNA binding capability, though its cellular efficacy was not sufficiently highlighted for further progression; the quinoline scaffold of this compound is an important nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocyclic system, very common in natural products and drug molecules, possessing broad biological activity, and its structural features are the hydroxyl group (OH) at the 3-position and the 2-chlorophenyl substituent at the 2-position, with 3-hydroxyquinoline as the core structure of many fluorescent dyes or metal chelating agents because the hydroxyl group and the quinoline nitrogen can form strong intramolecular hydrogen bonds and are capable of forming stable complexes with metal ions (e.g., aluminum, zinc, magnesium), while the 2-(2-chlorophenyl) substituent increases the molecules steric hindrance and lipophilicity, and in real-world applications, such compounds are frequently studied as fluorescent probes, particularly for detecting and imaging metal ions within cells, additionally, its structure bears resemblance to some potential anticancer or anti-inflammatory drugs.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-N-(3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropyl)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-amine chloride This compound is characterized by a pyrrolidine ring at the side chain terminus, linked by a propyl spacer to the rigid cationic core. Pyrrolidine, a five-membered saturated heterocycle with a basic nitrogen, is commonly used to build drugs with central nervous system (CNS) activity. In real-world drug design, pyrrolidine derivatives are frequently part of Dopamine, Serotonin, or Norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, indicating its potential for research as an antidepressant or anti-anxiety agent. The molecules high amphiphilicity and spatial complexity may also confer high selectivity for specific receptor subtypes, such as a potential lead for a -opioid receptor antagonist.

RNA information: The bacterial riboflavin (FMN) riboswitch is located in the 5' untranslated region (5' UTR) of mRNA and consists of an aptamer domain and an expression platform. It can specifically bind to FMN and regulate the expression of the downstream key riboflavin synthesis gene ribB through conformational changes. When FMN is sufficient, the riboswitch forms a terminator/sequestering loop to inhibit gene expression; when FMN is deficient, it switches to an antiterminator/anti-sequestering loop, allowing gene transcription and translation to achieve negative feedback regulation. This riboswitch exists exclusively in bacteria and has no homologous structure in mammals, making it a highly selective target for new antibacterial drugs (such as ribocil).
Small_molecule_information: 2-[1-[[2-(methylamino)pyrimidin-5-yl]methyl]piperidin-3-yl]-4-thiophen-2-yl-1H-pyrimidin-6-one, also referred to as Ribocil, is a synthetic small molecule with an overall U-shaped rigid skeleton, this complex molecule has a substituted pyrimidin-6-one core, linked at the 2-position to a substituted piperidine ring, which is further connected to a 2-(methylamino)pyrimidinyl-methyl group, the 4-position is substituted with a thiophenyl group, its left 6-thienyl-pyrimidinone ring can intercalate into the stacking plane of the RNA double helix, while the right methylamino-piperidinyl group forms weak hydrogen bonds with key guanines, such multi-heterocyclic and complex structures are characteristic of many small-molecule drugs, this compound can highly specifically bind to the aptamer pocket of the bacterial FMN riboswitch, mimicking the conformation of the natural ligand FMN and blocking the binding of FMN to RNA, thereby relieving the negative feedback inhibition of the riboswitch on the riboflavin synthesis gene ribB, leading to impaired bacterial growth due to riboflavin depletion, and importantly, its activity can be completely reversed by exogenous riboflavin, meanwhile this compound is often designed to target specific protein kinases, by inhibiting key kinases, it may find applications in the treatment of cancer, inflammation, or autoimmune diseases.

RNA information: G-quadruplex TERRA is a well-characterized, stable RNA G-quadruplex structure formed by the human telomeric repeat sequence. It serves as a canonical model for studying RNA G-quadruplex interactions. In this study, it was used as a control to demonstrate that although known G-quadruplex ligands bind TERRA with high affinity (low nM Kd), they are poor inhibitors of C9orf72 RAN translation. This key finding confirms that the toxic pathology in c9ALS/FTD is driven primarily by the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp rather than the G-quadruplex form, highlighting the therapeutic importance of targeting the specific hairpin structure.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(2-aminoethoxy)-2-N,6-N-bis[4-(2-aminoethoxy)quinolin-2-yl]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide, also referred to as PDS cPDS, is a large, linear polyamine compound centered on a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core, which links two substituted quinoline rings via amide bonds, with an additional substituted aminoethoxy group at the 4-position of the pyridine ring. The quinoline and pyridine moieties are nitrogen-containing heteroaromatics that contribute planarity and conjugation. The most significant feature is the presence of three 2-aminoethoxy substituents, introducing multiple primary amine groups that confer strong polycationic properties upon the molecule, making it highly protonated and positively charged at physiological pH. This multicationic structure and extended planar scaffold are common design elements for novel DNA/RNA binding agents, which interact with nucleic acids via electrostatic and intercalation mechanisms. Such compounds and their analogues are often explored for antiviral, antibacterial properties, and as novel non-viral gene transfection reagents or nucleic acid function inhibitors; additionally, PDS cPDS are potent and selective G-quadruplex-stabilizing small molecules that demonstrated excellent binding to the r(G4C2) G-quadruplex, however, they were ineffective at inhibiting RAN translation, providing key evidence that the G-quadruplex is not the therapeutically relevant structure for this disease mechanism.

RNA information: r(CUG)109 is a pathogenic RNA motif composed of 109 CUG repeats that folds into a stable, elongated hairpin structure containing periodic 1×1 UU internal loops. It functions as a toxic RNA by specifically binding to and sequestering muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, thereby disrupting its normal splicing regulation and contributing to the molecular pathology of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Small molecules that selectively bind r(CUG)109 can displace MBNL1, restore aberrant splicing patterns, and alleviate disease-associated phenotypes in cellular models .
Small_molecule_information: 6-nitro-2-[3-(6-nitro-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)propyl]-1H-benzimidazole, also referred to as Benzimidazole 1 (2015), is a symmetric or near-symmetric molecule composed of two benzimidazole units connected by a flexible three-carbon (propyl) chain, with each benzimidazole ring carrying a nitro group at the 6-position, and Benzimidazole 1 (2015) is a 2-phenyl-benzimidazole-4-carboxamide derivative optimized for selective recognition of the pathogenic r(CUG)109 RNA that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1, the benzimidazole scaffold, fused from a benzene ring and an imidazole ring, is a core structure in many pharmaceuticals (such as antifungals and anthelmintics), the two benzimidazole units are linked by a flexible alkyl chain, allowing the molecule to bind to biological targets in various conformations, while the nitro groups enhance the molecules electron-withdrawing capacity and potential redox activity, in real-world applications, nitro-containing benzimidazole derivatives are frequently investigated as antifungals, antiparasitics (e.g., antitrypanosomal or antimalarial agents), or anticancer drugs, with mechanisms possibly related to interfering with microbial or cancer cell DNA replication, or acting as substrates for bioreduction, by mimicking key hydrogen-bond and -stacking contacts normally supplied by the RNA-binding protein MBNL1, it binds the periodic 11 UU internal loops of the CUG hairpin with notable affinity in TR-FRET displacement, displaces MBNL1 from the toxic transcript, and restores correct alternative splicing of the cTNT mini-gene in DM1 cell models without altering the transcript level of DMPK mRNA or affecting off-target RNAs such as the human A-site rRNA or r(GGCC)4, its drug-like profilemoderate molecular weight (398 Da), cLogP 3.4, topological polar surface area 111 , and negligible cytotoxicity up to 100 Mmakes Benzimidazole 1 a validated chemical probe for demonstrating that non-charged, benzimidazole-based small molecules can selectively drug structured, repetitive RNA motifs implicated in human disease. RNA

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(5-amino-4-carbamoylimidazol-1-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methyl dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as 5-Aminoimidazole-4-carboxamide ribonucleotide (AICAR), is a non-azaaromatic purine nucleotide intermediate with an imidazole ring (bearing amino and carboxamide groups), a ribose moiety, and a phosphate group that lacks planar azaaromatic features, which binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; the molecule is a vital cellular metabolite and signaling molecule, a key intermediate in the de novo purine biosynthesis pathway (wherein its core role lies, unrelated to azaaromatic sensing), and its phosphorylated form ZMP is a potent activator of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), which is a crucial enzyme regulating cellular energy balance, making the molecule important in the study of metabolic diseases (such as type 2 diabetes) and energy metabolism.

RNA information: TCT8-4 has two conserved regions (5'-AUACCA-3' and 5'-CCUUGG(C/A)AG-3') . It binds theophylline  in a Mg-dependent way, showing 10,900-fold higher affinity for theophylline than caffeine. It aids in diagnosing diseases like asthma/bronchitis, where theophylline is used as a bronchodilator, by distinguishing theophylline from similar compounds.
Small_molecule_information: 1-methyl-3,7-dihydropurine-2,6-dione (also referred to as 1-methylxanthine) is a theophylline derivative that lacks the N3 methyl group of theophylline; it is a monomethylxanthine in which a methyl group is attached to N-1 and the imidazole ring is protonated at N-7, occurs naturally as a minor purine alkaloid and has been identified as a mouse metabolite. It has no mentioned specific biological function; its key relevance lies in binding theophylline-binding RNA. Compared to theophylline, the absence of the N-3 methyl and the presence of only a single N-1 methyl reduce its complementarity to the TCT8-4 RNA binding pocket, making it unable to fit into the hydrophobic pocket formed by RNAs C8, A28, and U24, though it still relies on core RNA bases (C22, U24) for weak binding, which explains its lower binding affinity, and both the N-3 methyl and the N-7 hydrogen-bond donor are critical for high-affinity recognition by the evolved RNA aptamer.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol;dihydrochloride, also referred to as Quinacrine Di HCl, is a quinoline-phenol heterocyclic compound presented as the dihydrochloride salt, which is an antimalarial and antiprotozoal drug with DNA intercalation properties and has been repurposed for cancer and prion diseases due to its ability to inhibit protein aggregation and nucleic acid interactions, its structure featuring a 4-aminoquinoline backbone with a 7-chloro substitution is chemically analogous to classic antimalarial drugs like chloroquine, the presence of both a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group on the phenyl ring provides multiple sites for interaction and protonation, quinolines are a broad class of compounds known for antimalarial, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory activities, due to its salt form the molecule is highly protonated enhancing its water solubility and the functional groups suggest potential for metal ion chelation, in reality this compound is likely explored as a novel antimalarial candidate to combat drug resistance or possibly as an anticancer agent due to its potential to interfere with metal homeostasis or kinase activity.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 7-(1,2,3,4,4a,5,7,7a-octahydropyrrolo[3,4-b]pyridin-1-ium-6-yl)-3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM51, is a non-planar coumarin derivative substituted at position 7 with an octahydropyrrolo[3,4-b]pyridin-1-ium quaternary ammonium salt, which has a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system and carries a 1 charge, this is a fused bicyclic system, comprising a five-membered pyrrole ring and a six-membered pyridine ring, saturated to an octahydro form, which provides a degree of conformational flexibility, the positive charge of the quaternary ammonium salt enhances water solubility and binding affinity to negatively charged biological targets, it recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, nitrogen-containing fused heterocycles of this type are often used in the design of drugs with central nervous system activity, as they can mimic the structure of endogenous neurotransmitters, this suggests a potential role as an antagonist or agonist for dopamine or serotonin receptors.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-amino-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridin-3-yl)-3-(trifluoromethyl)benzamide, also referred to as Compound 19, an isosteric derivative of the hit compound designed to systematically explore structural alternatives to the 3-amino/2-amide pharmacophore, was synthesized to evaluate the effect of bioisosteric replacement on binding affinity and selectivity, potentially improving metabolic stability or other drug-like properties, and this analog provides critical insights for optimizing the core scaffold in medicinal chemistry; the core structure is an N-(aminothienopyridinyl)benzamide, where the benzamide group is attached to the 3-amino group of the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine ring, with a free amino group at the 2-position, the molecule is substituted with a 3-(trifluoromethyl)benzoyl group, a highly lipophilic group, and this specific positional isomer of the amide bond is important in SAR studies, as it changes the molecules conformation and electronic properties compared to the 2-amide analogs, and this structural type is commonly investigated as a candidate for kinase inhibitors or antimicrobial agents, with its activity dictated by the precise interaction geometry with the target.

RNA information: No loop (loopless RNA), also known as 5'CAG/3'CUG and 5'CAG/3'GUC, is an RNA with a hairpin structure. Structurally, its stem has fully complementary base pairs (no mismatches/deletions) and no internal loops, showing high conformational stability without inter-strand "breathing effect" as a simplified dsRNA model. Functionally, it serves as a key experimental control to distinguish small molecules’ specific/non-specific binding to RNA, with no direct link to specific diseases.
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2R,3R,4R,5R)-4-amino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(1R,2S)-2-amino-6-(aminomethyl)cyclohexyl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-5-methyloxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as Sisomicin Sulphate, is a complex aminoglycoside compound featuring multiple sugar rings and an aminocyclohexitol structure linked by glycosidic bonds. The molecule contains numerous amine (NH2) groups, rendering it polycationic at physiological pH. Aminoglycosides are a critical class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that function by irreversibly binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, disrupting protein synthesis by binding to rRNA, inhibiting protein synthesis. Similar to clinical antibiotics like Streptomycin or Gentamicin, this molecule is primarily used to treat severe Gram-negative bacterial infections, though its therapeutic use must consider inherent ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity side effects.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 9-methoxy-5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 4, is a pyridocarbazole derivative structurally related to the powerful natural antitumor agent Ellipticine; it is an optimized, selective lead that potently binds the GG internal loop, selectively inhibits RAN translation without affecting canonical translation, prevents hnRNP H sequestration, and visualizes RNA foci in cells (establishing the hairpin as the therapeutically relevant target), while its large planar aromatic system facilitates DNA intercalation and topoisomerase inhibition, a characteristic that makes it a highly promising candidate in the development of new generation chemotherapeutic agents for various solid tumors.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroacridin-9-amine, also referred to as 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroacridine-9-amine, is a hydrogenated acridine derivative with the acridine core, where the benzene ring is fused with a dihydropyridine ring, and the 1,2,3,4 positions are hydrogenated, with an amino group at position 9, molecular formula C13H16N2; the compounds core is a partially saturated tricyclic acridine structure with some lipophilicity, and the amino group imparts basicity. In terms of real-world activity, such acridine derivatives, due to their unique planar tricyclic structure and amino side chain, are often studied as DNA intercalators, showing potential in anticancer and antibacterial fields; specifically, this compound shares structural similarities with the well-known Alzheimers drug tacrine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor), which makes it a derivative of tacrine with potential acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity, suggesting its potential for Central Nervous System activity as a candidate for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimers disease treatment, and it may also possess topoisomerase inhibitory activity, making it valuable in antitumor drug development.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-amino-4-(3-morpholin-4-ylpropoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one has a side chain terminal amine group that is morpholine. Morpholine is an oxygen-containing six-membered ring tertiary amine. The introduction of the oxygen atom reduces the basicity of the nitrogen and increases the molecules hydrophilicity. Functionally, the morpholine scaffold is common in many drugs that target kinases or receptors. In practical applications, the morpholine substituent is often used to improve a drugs solubility, and is considered to have a better safety profile due to its relatively lower cytotoxicity, frequently explored as a lead compound for novel kinase inhibitors or anti-inflammatory agents.

RNA information: RNA3 is a key model RNA that incorporates both the Drosha site (1x1 UU loop, target of 1) and the adjacent site (1x1 GG loop, target of 2). It binds the dimeric compound 3 with high avidity (Kd = 85 nM), demonstrating simultaneous engagement of both modules.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-azidoethyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2, is an azide-tagged bis-benzimidazole probe featuring a short two-carbon (ethyl) linker, which is a bis-benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module that Informa predicted and binding studies confirmed selectively targets a 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loop adjacent to the Drosha site in pri-miR-96, while it binds its target RNA motif, it also exhibits high, undesired affinity for an AT-rich DNA hairpin and does not effectively inhibit miR-96 biogenesis in cellular assays when used as a monomer; the shorter linker alters the spatial presentation of the azide group for the subsequent Click Chemistry reaction, and its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for DNA binding and labeling studies, used to investigate the impact of a close-to-core tag on the probes biological performance.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: N-cyclopentyl-4-[5-[4-(N-cyclopentylcarbamimidoyl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as DB244 (2,5-bis-[4-(N-cyclopentylamidino)phenyl]furan), is a bis-cyclopentyl amidine-substituted biphenyl furan structure, and DB244 is a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative that belongs to the same chemotype as DB60 and DB75 but is endowed with bulkier cyclopentylamidine end-groups, furan-phenyl scaffolds are often used to construct fluorescent probes or molecules with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, the bis-amidine substitution design of next-generation TAR-targeted diphenylfurans. The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol, also referred to as Amodiaquine Di HCl, is a quinoline derivative structured around a quinoline core, with a chloro substitution at the 7-position and an amino link at the 4-position to a benzene ring containing a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group (molecular formula C20H22ClN3O), and it is an antimalarial drug with immunomodulatory effects that shares mechanisms with chloroquine but has higher toxicity risks, limiting its use despite efficacy against resistant Plasmodium strains; this compound possesses a planar conjugated system and multiple functional groups (chloro, amine, phenol, quinoline), functionally, the quinoline core is famous as the scaffold for well-known antimalarial drugs (such as chloroquine), and derivatives are commonly investigated for broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antimalarial, and anti-cancer activities, the presence of the phenolic hydroxyl and amine groups suggests potential for metal complexation or binding to enzyme active sites.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.

Compounds with the propylamine backbone are often studied as Histamine H2 receptor antagonists or antidepressants. Its structure gives it the potential for strong electrostatic interactions with negatively charged cellular components (like DNA) or the phospholipid membrane, which may confer antimicrobial activity or utility as a gene transfection aid.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 1-(2-phenylethyl)quinolin-1-ium-8-ol, also referred to as CP19, is a permanently positively charged quaternary ammonium salt based on the quinolin-8-ol scaffold, and CP19 is a hydroxyquinoline N-oxide connected to a benzyl group via an ethyl linker whose N-oxide and hydroxy groups likely disrupted its binding profile, leading to extremely low affinity which disqualified it from further investigation, the quinoline nitrogen is substituted with a 2-phenylethyl group, forming a quaternary ammonium (quinolin-1-ium) structure with the positive charge located at the 1-position nitrogen, this structure integrates the excellent metal chelating ability of quinolin-8-ol with the altered surface activity and membrane penetration characteristics conferred by the quaternary ammonium salt, quaternary ammonium salts typically possess potent antimicrobial and antiseptic activity because the positive charge allows them to disrupt microbial cell membranes, therefore, 1-(2-phenylethyl)quinolin-1-ium-8-ol is likely explored as a novel antimicrobial agent or a biofilm inhibitor, furthermore, due to its metal-chelating function, it may also be utilized as a new fluorescent sensor or bioimaging probe. RNA information: This RNA forms a well-ordered binding pocket through interlocking structural motifs . In the presence of Mg²+, it can bind theophylline with high affinity  and exhibits molecular discrimination specificity towards caffeine.
Small_molecule_information: 1,3,7-trimethylpurine-2,6-dione, also referred to as Caffeine, structurally similar to theophylline but with an extra methyl group at the N7 position, lacks the bronchodilator function of theophylline and is structurally distinct from theophylline due to this N-7 methyl group; it functions as a competitor for theophylline-binding RNA aptamers, binds theophylline-binding RNA far weaker, its N7 methyl disrupts key H-bonds and causes steric repulsion, failing to form the RNAs core base triples and stacking motifs needed for stable binding, explaining RNAs molecular discrimination, and while no direct diseases are associated with it, its structural similarity to theophylline may interfere with diagnostic assays for theophylline-treated conditions like asthma and bronchitis.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)benzenecarbothioamide, also referred to as 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole, is a combination of benzimidazole and carbothioamide structures, and 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole is a heterocyclic small molecule with a benzimidazole core linked to a thiocarbamoyl-substituted phenyl group, the benzimidazole core is often associated with antiparasitic, antifungal, antiviral, and antineoplastic activities, the carbothioamide group may enable the molecule to act as a metal chelator or enzyme inhibitor, the structure suggests the molecule may serve as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting on cell division or specific enzyme systems, unlike myricetin, it lacks reported affinity for CAG-repeat RNAs in HD/SCAs but may interact with structured RNAs via benzimidazole intercalation and thiocarbamoyl H-bonding, it shows no validated activity against polyQ aggregation or mitochondrial dysfunction in HD models, though its benzimidazole motif could potentially target nucleic acid mismatches, making it a speculative scaffold for TRED-focused optimization with further biophysical validation needed. -

RNA information: It serves as an in vitro research tool to detect the binding properties of small molecules to composite RNA motifs: the monomeric Compound 1 has weak binding affinity , while the dimeric Compound 2 shows significantly enhanced affinity for it. It is used to detect the binding properties between small molecules and RNA. Such interaction studies provide key data support for screening selective small molecules that target specific miRNA precursors.

Small_molecule_information: 16-[3-(3-fluoropyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene is a fluorinated analog of 17-methoxy-16-[3-(3-methylpyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.02,10.04,8.015,20]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene, with a fluorine atom introduced at the 3-position of the pyridinium ring. The piperidine-ethylamine scaffold is the foundation for many local anesthetics and antiarrhythmic drugs (similar to structures like Lidocaine). In real-world pharmacology, this compound is highly likely to be studied for voltage-gated sodium channel blockade, which stabilizes the membrane potential in neural or myocardial cells. The shorter ethyl spacer brings the piperidine ring closer to the rigid core, which may influence the depth of binding and conformation at the channel receptor site.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6: This RNA contains six consecutive CAG repeats with AA mismatches, forming multiple internal loops. It is a key target for small molecules due to its structural similarity to pathogenic expanded repeats. It shows high affinity and selective binding to compounds such as pyridocoumarin derivatives, leading to conformational changes and thermal stabilization.; The (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6 RNA contains multiple consecutive A-A mismatch loops. This multi-loop structure enhances conformational stability and ligand binding avidity, more accurately mimicking the expanded toxic repeats found in patient-derived RNA and providing a robust platform for biophysical testing.
Small_molecule_information: methyl N-(4-chlorophenyl)-2H-quinoline-1-carboximidothioate, also referred to as CP2, is a quinoline-based molecule featuring a thione and imine functionality (which incorporates a quinoline core linked to a thiocarbamate group and a 4-chlorophenyl ring, with the combination of its planar aromatic system and halogen substituent contributing to its affinity for CAG repeat RNA and efficacy in reducing polyQ aggregation in cellular models, marking it as a promising candidate). Quinoline scaffolds are associated with various anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities, and this complex structure is a candidate for research as an enzyme inhibitor, possibly targeting microbial enzymes, due to its specialized reactive groups.

Small_molecule_information: (2S)-5-hydroxy-2-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxyphenyl)-7-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxy-2,3-dihydrochromen-4-one, also referred to as Hesperidin, is a flavonoid glycoside with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects commonly found in citrus fruits, and is an extremely complex natural product flavonoid O-diglycoside with a dihydroflavone (flavanone) core substituted at the 7-position with a diglycoside unit (likely involving glucose and rhamnose); the numerous phenolic hydroxyl groups and the bulky glycoside moiety are the primary drivers of its activity, and in practice, such compounds are key functional components in herbal medicines, demonstrating potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and free radical scavenging capabilities, while due to its glycosidic structure, it typically has high water solubility and specific oral bioavailability (via gut microbial hydrolysis), and it is widely studied for cardiovascular benefits as well as cardioprotective, antiviral, and hepatoprotective effects, making it a focal point in natural product research.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 1H-indol-6-ol, also referred to as 6-hydroxyindole, is a hydroxyl derivative of the indole scaffold. Structurally, it is an azaaromatic molecule with an indole core (fused benzene and pyrrole rings) and a hydroxyl group at the 6-position, exhibiting planar and hydrophobic characteristics typical of azaaromatics; the indole ring (fused from a benzene ring and a pyrrole ring) is an extremely important class of heterocycles in nature, being the core structure of tryptophan and many neurotransmitters (such as Serotonin), thus having great significance in neuroscience and drug development. 6-hydroxyindole is a metabolic precursor or related structure to the pineal gland hormone Melatonin, playing a role in in vivo metabolism, and while the document focuses on azaaromatics binding yjdF riboswitches, no info on this compounds riboswitch binding affinity or gene expression effects is given; in reality, such compounds are often used as synthetic building blocks for neuroactive drugs, for developing selective serotonin receptor agonists or antagonists, and for studying drugs for sleep regulation, mood disorders, and migraines.
