Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as the saturated analog of DAPI (whose full name is 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide), is a compound featuring a dihydroindole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, with the formula C16H16N4, while DAPI is a fluorescent stain binding AT-rich DNA regions, widely used in microscopy for nuclear labeling and is cell-impermeant unless membranes are compromised, making it useful for viability assays and chromosome visualization; this compound is the saturated analog of 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, the saturation of the indole ring removes aromaticity and significantly increases the flexibility and changes the 3D shape of the molecule, this structural change dramatically influences its binding mode and affinity for the DNA minor groove, and this compound is investigated to explore the impact of molecular rigidity/flexibility on the activity of DNA-binding drugs, showing potential for anticancer and anti-infective activity.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dimethoxyphenyl)-1H-benzimidazole, also referred to as Compound H8, is a simple benzimidazole scaffold substituted at the 2-position with a 3,4-dimethoxyphenyl group, and it is a bis-benzimidazole derivative of Hoechst 33258 rationally designed to enhance RNA-binding affinity and selectivity for the pathogenic r(CUG)exp RNA that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1); it retains the DNA-binding benzimidazole core of Hoechst 33258 but is elongated with a hydrophobic p-methoxyphenyl tail that improves its shape complementarity to the major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat, can dissociate MBNL1 from r(CUG)12 with potency that is approximately two-fold higher than Hoechst 33258 yet is less selective than H1, binding with similar affinity to RNAs containing 5CUG/3GCC or 5CGG/3GGC motifs and to the human A-site, and in DM1 cell models can partially shift a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal while showing cytotoxicity at higher doses, exemplifying how computational shape similarity can evolve a DNA-binding dye into a low-micromolar chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics; the benzimidazole core of this compound is a high-profile structural motif in drug chemistry, found in a wide array of vital pharmaceuticals including antiparasitic agents and proton pump inhibitors, the introduction of the 3,4-dimethoxyphenyl group, a common lignan pharmacophore, may confer tubulin inhibitory activity, similar to some antineoplastic drugs, thereby interfering with cell division, additionally, this class of compounds is often investigated for anti-inflammatory and antiviral effects, and it represents a lead structure with good drug-like properties and potential for high bioavailability.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-amino-1-[(2R,5S)-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-oxathiolan-5-yl]pyrimidin-2-one, also referred to as Lamivudine (3TC) or its enantiomer, is a synthetic cytidine nucleoside analog where the 3 oxygen atom of the ribose ring is replaced by a sulfur atom, forming a 1,3-oxathiolane sugar ring, molecular formula C8H11N3O3S; it is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) that incorporates into viral DNA, terminating its synthesis, and in terms of real-world activity, Lamivudine is an extremely important antiviral drug, classified as a Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitor (NRTI), which is widely used clinically for the treatment of Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) infection and Hepatitis B Virus (HBV) infection, with its mechanism of action involving being phosphorylated intracellularly to its active triphosphate form, which is then incorporated into the viral DNA chain, causing chain termination and effectively inhibiting the activity of the HIV reverse transcriptase and HBV polymerase.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 1-ethyl-2-methyl-3-phenylquinazolin-1-ium-4-one, also referred to as CP8 which is based on a quinazolinone core with N-ethyl, N-methyl, and phenyl substitutions, is a quinazolinone quaternary ammonium salt; the substituted quinazolinone scaffold of this compound showed measurable binding but was ultimately superseded by compounds with superior affinity and selectivity, and quaternary ammonium salts often possess broad-spectrum antibacterial activity by non-specifically disrupting bacterial membranes, making this a potential antimicrobial agent.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Luteolin (a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects that exhibits antioxidant activity and may influence gene expression), is a naturally occurring flavone compound (distinguished from flavonols like 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one by the lack of a hydroxyl group at C3), with the formula C15H10O6. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and a catechol moiety (3,4-dihydroxyphenyl) on the B ring. Luteolin is widely found in celery, parsley, broccoli, cabbage, and various medicinal herbs. In real-world biological activity, Luteolin demonstrates powerful antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, making it an important active component in traditional herbal medicine and modern supplements. It operates through multiple mechanisms, including free radical scavenging, inhibition of various inflammatory mediators, and modulation of immune cell function. Furthermore, it is extensively studied for its neuroprotective, anti-allergic, and potential anticancer properties, such as by inhibiting tumor cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 3, shares the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one core with 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-methoxybenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one but lacks the bromine atom, and Compound 3 emerged as a lead compound with high affinity and selectivity for r(CAG)exp RNA, its large aromatic and amino-substituted structure suggests high lipophilicity and the potential to interact strongly with biological targets, it is likely investigated as an antiproliferative agent targeting intracellular enzymes or signaling pathways, Compound 3 significantly reduced polyQ aggregation in cellular models, showed low cytotoxicity, and was cell-permeable, localizing primarily in the cytoplasm. RNA

RNA information: r(GGCC)4 is a short, double-stranded RNA motif composed of four tandem GGCC repeats that adopts a fully base-paired hairpin structure. It is commonly used in competition assays as a negative control because it lacks internal loops or single-stranded regions and therefore does not bind to RNA-targeted small molecules such as CB253, which selectively recognize the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp RNA .
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (DB75, furamidine), is a bis-imidamide compound where the two benzamidine groups are connected by a furan ring, with the formula C18H16N4O, and it is also a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative with a furamidine chromophore and smaller amidine end-groups; the introduction of the furan ring imparts some flexibility to the molecule, allowing for conformational adjustments during DNA binding, and this compound can selectively bind AU base pairs with positive cooperativity, targeting r(AUUCU) repeats and protecting AU sites from RNase cleavage, can bind HIV-1 TAR RNAs GCUCU motif via shape-driven recognition, displace Tat, block trans-activation, stabilize TAR, and has anti-PCP activity/DNA minor-groove binding, with no cytotoxicity below 30 M and serving as a TAR-targeting benchmark; these heterocyclic diamidines are a focus in the development of antiparasitic drugs, leveraging their strong DNA binding ability to effectively inhibit the growth of protozoa like Trypanosoma, and also show potential for antifungal and anticancer therapy.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: N-cyclopentyl-4-[5-[4-(N-cyclopentylcarbamimidoyl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as DB244 (2,5-bis-[4-(N-cyclopentylamidino)phenyl]furan), is a bis-cyclopentyl amidine-substituted biphenyl furan structure, and DB244 is a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative that belongs to the same chemotype as DB60 and DB75 but is endowed with bulkier cyclopentylamidine end-groups, furan-phenyl scaffolds are often used to construct fluorescent probes or molecules with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, the bis-amidine substitution suggests potential DNA binding or serine protease inhibitory activity, with the cyclopentyl substitution likely intended to enhance lipophilicity and cell membrane permeability, DB244 was originally optimized for anti-Pneumocystis carinii (PCP) therapy, exhibits excellent DNA minor-groove binding and ranks among the most potent agents against PCP in rat models (DB244 DB75 DB226 DB60), when redirected to the HIV-1 TAR RNA target, the added bulk reduces RNA affinity, in vitro gel-shift assays show notable IC50 for displacing Tat peptide from the 59-nt TAR hairpin, while the FIGS cellular assay shows essentially no suppression of Tat-driven trans-activation up to a high tested concentration, melting-temperature experiments record a modest Tm with DB244TAR complexes, and RNase A footprints reveal protection of the GCUCU site opposite the bulge only at high concentrations, confirming weaker, lower-occupancy binding, like DB60, DB244 recognizes the three-dimensional cavity created by the pyrimidine bulge rather than specific nucleotides: mutation of U23 or the GC26-39 pair has little influence on affinity, whereas deletion of the bulge abolishes the interaction, the compound is essentially non-toxic to reporter cells below a certain concentration and retains full anti-PCP potency, illustrating how increasing the steric demand of the terminal basic chains tips the selectivity balance toward DNA and away from RNA recognition, thus, although DB244 itself is not an effective anti-HIV lead, its profile delineates structureselectivity relationships that guide the design of next-generation TAR-targeted diphenylfurans. DNARNA

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 4, is a complex, linear peptidic macromolecule which is a lower molecular weight analog retaining the triazole core, designed to improve ligand efficiency while preserving HIV-1 FSS RNA recognition, binds the FSS with sub-micromolar affinity despite simplification of structure compared to earlier leads, reduces viral production in T-cell infection assays although with slightly reduced potency relative to compound 3 and shows notable metabolic effects in cytotoxicity assays suggesting some off-target influence, demonstrating that substantial structural streamlining is possible while maintaining RNA-targeting activity, and its structure contains multiple chiral amino acid residues (e.g., Pro, Phe derivatives), a triazole linking unit, and a quinoline-3-carboxamide group. In terms of biological function, compounds with such complex structures are usually inhibitors or ligands targeting specific biological targets, potentially exhibiting anticancer, antiviral, or signal pathway regulatory activities.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]butanedioic acidThis complex is a quinoline-peptide conjugate featuring a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core, which is known for its DNA intercalation and topoisomerase inhibition activities with potential antineoplastic activity. The core is linked to a dipeptide fragment containing Arginine and Aspartic acid. The Arginine residues guanidino group enhances binding to nucleic acids due to its strong basicity. This structure is designed as a targeted anticancer prodrug or biological probe to improve water solubility and selectively deliver the cytotoxic quinoline core to specific biological targets.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: [5-[3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-2-oxochromen-7-yl]-5-azaspiro[2.4]heptan-7-yl]azanium, also referred to as SMSM56, is a coumarin derivative whose position 7 is substituted with a highly rigid spirocyclic system, the 5-azaspiro[2.4]heptane group, which bears an additional azanium positive charge at the 7-carbon atom, and SMSM56 itself is a non-planar coumarin analog with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, carrying a 1 charge, this spirocycle merges a highly strained three-membered ring with a five-membered ring, creating a molecular geometry that is both highly unique and rigid, the presence of the quaternary ammonium positive charge is a key design feature for enhancing water solubility and affinity for biomembranes, the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group is attached at position 3 of the coumarin core, and this compound recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand, due to its unique spatial structure and high rigidity, this compound is likely designed as a highly selective ligand for biological targets with complex binding pockets, with the coumarin and imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine fragments suggesting potential anti-tumor or anti-inflammatory activities.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-5-hydroxy-2-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxyphenyl)-7-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxy-2,3-dihydrochromen-4-one, also referred to as Hesperidin, is a flavonoid glycoside with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects commonly found in citrus fruits, and is an extremely complex natural product flavonoid O-diglycoside with a dihydroflavone (flavanone) core substituted at the 7-position with a diglycoside unit (likely involving glucose and rhamnose); the numerous phenolic hydroxyl groups and the bulky glycoside moiety are the primary drivers of its activity, and in practice, such compounds are key functional components in herbal medicines, demonstrating potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and free radical scavenging capabilities, while due to its glycosidic structure, it typically has high water solubility and specific oral bioavailability (via gut microbial hydrolysis), and it is widely studied for cardiovascular benefits as well as cardioprotective, antiviral, and hepatoprotective effects, making it a focal point in natural product research.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[4-[[2,6-bis[[4-(2-aminoethoxy)quinolin-2-yl]carbamoyl]-4-pyridinyl]oxymethyl]triazol-1-yl]propanoic acid, also referred to as PDS cPDS, is a complex heterocyclic molecule often constructed via click chemistry, which is structurally similar to 4-(2-aminoethoxy)-2-N,6-N-bis[4-(2-aminoethoxy)quinolin-2-yl]pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide but modified at the pyridine 4-position, and it is a potent and selective G-quadruplex-stabilizing small molecule that demonstrated excellent binding to the r(G4C2) G-quadruplex, though it was ineffective at inhibiting RAN translation, providing key evidence that the G-quadruplex is not the therapeutically relevant structure for this disease mechanism; the molecule maintains the core features of the multicationic backbone with a 2,6-bis-amide substituted pyridine and two 2-aminoethoxy groups on the quinoline side chains, however, the modification at the pyridine 4-position introduces a side chain containing a stable and polar 1,2,3-triazole ring, terminated by a carboxylic acid group (propanoic acid), the carboxylic acid group introduces an ionizable negative charge, creating an amphoteric or zwitterionic-like molecule that combines polycationic properties (from the primary amines) with a terminal negative charge, and this complex charge distribution may enable unique functionalities, such as pH-sensitive molecular probes, ligands for specific cell receptors, or prodrugs designed to release or change activity in specific environments, like the acidic tumor microenvironment.

RNA information: MALAT1 is a well-studied long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) known to form a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure consisting of three G-tetrads. It functions as a key regulatory molecule in processes such as alternative splicing modulation. The rG4 structure within MALAT1 has been confirmed in vitro through techniques including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays and fluorescence-based methods using probes like NMM and ThT. Studies show that in potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, MALAT1's rG4 forms efficiently, causing significant reverse transcriptase stalling (~60%). Notably, ligands such as PhenDC3 and PDS can act as molecular chaperones, inducing and stabilizing MALAT1's rG4 structure even under unfavorable lithium (Li⁺) conditions, highlighting its dynamic structural plasticity and potential as a therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: N-[9-[4-(dimethylamino)anilino]-6-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropanoylamino)acridin-3-yl]-3-piperidin-1-ylpropanamide, also referred to as Braco-19, is an acridine analog of N-[9-[4-(dimethylamino)anilino]-6-(3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanoylamino)acridin-3-yl]-3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanamide, replacing pyrrolidine with a piperidine side chain, it is an acridine-derived G4 ligand with a tricyclic acridine core modified by cationic side chains, enabling selective binding to G-quadruplexes (G4s) via -stacking, can stabilize pre-formed RNA G4s (rG4s) with moderate affinity but lacks robust chaperone activity for rG4 folding especially for non-canonical 2-quartet rG4s (e.g., XIST, RPPH1), weakly induces tetramolecular rG4 assembly and does not significantly enhance reverse transcriptase stalling in Li buffers, making it suitable for stabilizing existing rG4s rather than promoting de novo folding, and is explored as a potential acridine-based anticancer drug with possible altered pharmacokinetics and selective cytotoxicity.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoyl]amino]-3-(1H-imidazol-5-yl)propanoic acidThis is a simpler Arginine-Histidine dipeptide conjugate with the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core. It combines the strong basicity of Arginine and the pH-responsive imidazole ring of Histidine with the cores cytotoxicity. Its potential activity is antineoplastic, focusing on targeting acidic microenvironments or enzyme active centers. Such molecules are often utilized to explore strategies for targeting acidic organelles like endosomes and lysosomes in cancer cells.

RNA information: 5'CNG/3'CNG refers to repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide), commonly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing.
Small_molecule_information: 6-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)-2-[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-1H-indol-3-amine, also referred to as D2, is a bis-imidazolinyl substituted indolamine, characterized by high basicity and hydrophilicity, which is a close congener of the DNA fluorophore DAPI identified via shape-screening the NCI library for compounds capable of recognizing the pathogenic 5CAG/3GAC 1 1 nucleotide internal loop of expanded r(CAG) repeat RNAs, structurally conserving DAPIs two amidine groups while being decorated with an extra phenyl-amidine substituent that markedly increases its -surface and positive charge, able to bind the 5CAG/3GAC hairpin with tighter affinity than DAPI and showing essentially no detectable affinity for fully-paired RNA or DNA, capable of disrupting the r(CAG)MBNL1 complex, restoring correct splicing of NCOR2 and ATP2A1 pre-mRNAs in Huntington-disease patient fibroblasts and neuroblastoma lines expressing expanded CAG repeats without altering cell morphology or nuclei staining and without affecting the splicing pattern of control cells, thus serving as a synthetically accessible, highly selective chemical probe that validates the druggability of r(CAG)exp RNAs and constitutes a lead for developing disease-modifying therapy in poly-glutamine expansion disorders; the imidazolinyl group of this molecule is a common pharmacophore for adrenergic receptor agonists or antagonists (e.g., clonidine), and concurrently, its indolamine core is related to the tryptamine structure, suggesting potential Central Nervous System (CNS) activity, while as a dicationic structure, it may also interact with nucleic acids or polysaccharides, holding potential research value in anticancer or antimicrobial fields.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[2-[8-(cyclopropylamino)-7-methyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl]ethyl]piperidine-4-carboxylic acid, also referred to as BRX1072, is a complex pteridine derivative containing a carboxylic acid and a piperidine ring, which is a synthetic analog of FMN (flavin mononucleotide) whose structural design involves introducing a heptanoic acid side chain at the end of FMNs ribityl chain and simultaneously adding both piperidine and cyclopropyl substituents at the N8 position, with these complex chemical modifications aiming to further optimize binding ability with the FMN riboswitch; its structure is based on the benzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione core, with a cyclopropylamino group at the 8-position, a methyl group at the 7-position, and an ethyl-piperidine-carboxylic acid side chain attached at the N10-position, the molecular formula is C23H27N5O4, and this compound combines the features of a flavin core, a cyclopropylamino group, and a piperidine ring (often associated with receptor binding and permeability); however, despite incorporating multiple functional groups, BRX1072s binding affinity and activity did not meet the expected outcomes: it exhibits a certain degree of binding ability but shows weak activity in regulating gene expression in transcription termination assays, which indicates that although various functional groups were introduced, the interactions between these groups may not be fully synergistic, resulting in overall activity inferior to compounds modified with a single functional group, such as BRX1151 and BRX1224, and research on this compound provides important insights into the role of functional groups in drug design, while also emphasizing the need to balance interactions between different chemical groups during the drug optimization process, and in terms of biological function, the combination of the piperidine and carboxylic acid groups may confer neuropharmacological activity or serve as a ligand for targeted delivery systems.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 7-methoxy-1-methyl-4,9-dihydro-3H-pyrido[3,4-b]indole, also referred to as harmaline, is a tetrahydro--carboline (THBC) derivative which is a planar azaaromatic -carboline alkaloid with a fused tricyclic core (indole and pyridine rings, containing multiple nitrogen atoms) and a methoxy group at the 7-position, plus a hydrogenated double bond in the pyridine ring (distinct from harmine), and THBCs are often involved in neurotransmitter modulation in the brain, with this compound binding to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) via non-specific interactions but with lower affinity than chelerythrine, failing to activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis while having monoamine oxidase inhibitory activity which aids in studying riboswitch ligand structure-activity relationships, and THBCs are explored for their neuroprotective and antioxidant properties, with this molecule being a candidate for research as a ligand for 5-HT (serotonin) or dopamine receptors in the context of neurological and psychiatric drug development.

RNA information: xpt-pbuX riboswitch is an guanine-sensing RNA regulatory element located in the 5' untranslated region of Bacillus subtilis mRNA, consisting of a conserved aptamer domain and an expression platform. Its aptamer folds into a three-way connection structure composed of three stems, P1, P2, and P3, and two rings, L2 and L3. The core area precisely recognizes and closely binds guanine through seven hydrogen bonds. After guanine binding, it stabilizes the P1 stem, promotes the formation of a terminator stem in the downstream expression platform, blocks the transcription of the downstream xpt-pbuX gene, and thereby inhibits the expression of purine transporters. In the absence of guanine, the aptamer conformation is loose, and the 3' end of P1 can form an antiterminator structure with the expression platform, allowing transcription to continue. This riboswitch dynamically regulates gene expression by sensing intracellular guanine concentration, maintains purine metabolic balance, and can be activated by artificially designed guanine analogues (such as G7) to achieve antibacterial effects.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-amino-7H-purin-6-yl)hydroxylamine, also referred to as G7 (6-n-hydroxyaminopine), is a purine nucleobase analog (and a guanine analogue) where the carbonyl group at position 6 of Guanine (2-amino-7H-purin-6-one) is replaced by a hydroxylamine group (-NH-OH) (equivalent to a hydroxyl group introduced at the C6 position of guanine), molecular formula C5H6N6O. In terms of real-world activity, this non-natural base analog has unique chemical reactivity and hydrogen bonding properties due to the introduction of the hydroxylamine group; it can bind to xpt-pbuX riboswitch aptamer and induce the formation of a compact structure similar to natural guanine, and in Bacillus subtilis, it can simultaneously and completely inhibit bacterial growth and the reporter gene expression controlled by xpt-pbuX riboswitch, with its antibacterial activity not affected by the pbuE pump mutation related to G6 resistance, indicating that it mainly exerts antibacterial effects by activating the guanine riboswitch and blocking the purine metabolic pathway. Such compounds are often studied as chemical mutagens, as they can alter the base-pairing properties of nucleic acids, leading to replication errors and gene mutations. Therefore, this compound may possess potential antiviral or anticancer activity by being incorporated into the DNA strand or acting as a potent inhibitor of certain metabolic enzymes, thereby interfering with the normal synthesis and function of nucleic acids to inhibit the proliferation of rapidly dividing cells.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 3,6-bis[(E)-2-(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]-9H-carbazole diiodide, also referred to as BMVC/BMVC2/BMVC4, is a dicationic compound built on a carbazole scaffold, where two 1-methylpyridinium groups are attached at positions 3 and 6 via trans-ethenyl linkers, with the formula C28H27I2N3; as a series of carbazole derivatives and well-known fluorescent probes with high affinity for DNA/RNA G-quadruplex structures, it served as a representative G-quadruplex ligand in a related study, and despite binding strongly to the G-quadruplex form of r(G4C2)exp in vitro, it was a poor inhibitor of RAN translation in cellular models, while its carbazole core provides optoelectronic properties, the long conjugated chain and dicationic nature contribute to its strong fluorescence and nucleobase affinity, it is typically employed as a high-performance fluorescent dye in biological systems, and biologically, its ability to electrostatically bind to and potentially intercalate with biomacromolecules suggests potential anticancer and antimicrobial activities by disrupting cell membrane function or DNA replication.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2-chlorophenyl)quinolin-3-ol, also referred to as CP7, is a quinoline derivative (and a hydroxyquinoline derivative with a 2-chlorophenyl substitution) that has a classic intercalator motif which provided RNA binding capability, though its cellular efficacy was not sufficiently highlighted for further progression; the quinoline scaffold of this compound is an important nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocyclic system, very common in natural products and drug molecules, possessing broad biological activity, and its structural features are the hydroxyl group (OH) at the 3-position and the 2-chlorophenyl substituent at the 2-position, with 3-hydroxyquinoline as the core structure of many fluorescent dyes or metal chelating agents because the hydroxyl group and the quinoline nitrogen can form strong intramolecular hydrogen bonds and are capable of forming stable complexes with metal ions (e.g., aluminum, zinc, magnesium), while the 2-(2-chlorophenyl) substituent increases the molecules steric hindrance and lipophilicity, and in real-world applications, such compounds are frequently studied as fluorescent probes, particularly for detecting and imaging metal ions within cells, additionally, its structure bears resemblance to some potential anticancer or anti-inflammatory drugs.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2,3-dihydro-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-6-ylmethyl)-3-(trifluoromethoxy)aniline, also referred to as Compound 4g, is a dihydropyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine derivative (Compound 4g is additionally categorized as a 2-aminomethylene-dihydropyrrolo[3,2]-pyridine derivative) synthesized via ruthenium-catalyzed [222] cycloaddition, amine substitution, and Boc deprotection, where the aniline is substituted with a trifluoromethoxy (OCF3) group at the meta position; the trifluoromethoxy group is a more polar and strongly electron-withdrawing substituent compared to trifluoromethyl, and this structural change is designed to optimize the molecules pharmacokinetic properties, such as improving water solubility and altering metabolic pathways, while the dihydropyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine core suggests potential binding to nervous system receptors, and the compound (Compound 4g) binds pre-miR-21 with moderate affinity and shows selectivity over duplex DNA/tRNA, though it lacks Dicer cleavage inhibition activity, likely due to its O-containing aniline substituent failing to form critical H-bonds or block pre-miR-21s G28-A29 cleavage site, limiting its ability to suppress oncogenic miR-21 biogenesis, and this compound is investigated as a potential drug acting on neural or signaling pathways, offering potential advantages in absorption and distribution.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: [1-[3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-2-oxochromen-7-yl]azepan-4-yl]azanium, also referred to as SMSM53, is a non-planar coumarin derivative where the substituent at position 7 is an azepane ring, which carries an azanium positive charge at the 4-carbon atom, and it has a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system with a 1 charge; azepane is a seven-membered ring, which is generally more conformationally flexible than the six-membered piperidine ring, allowing the molecule a certain capacity for induced fit upon target binding, and this compound recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand; the positive charge is a conventional means of enhancing water solubility and electrostatic binding, and the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine group and the coumarin core suggest potential as an inhibitor for specific enzymes or a ligand for receptors; structurally, it may be utilized for developing drugs against cardiovascular diseases or inflammation, as azepane scaffolds are found in active molecules within these therapeutic areas.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: 5,11-dimethyl-2-(2-piperidin-1-ylethyl)-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazol-2-ium-9-ol, also referred to as hydroxyellipticine 1a, is the first-generation lead molecule discovered to bind r(GC), which is a rationally designed small-molecule ligand that selectively targets the pathogenic RNA repeat r(GGGGCC) responsible for c9FTD/ALS, originally developed to bind 1 1 GG internal loops in r(CGG)exp and later found to recognize the closely related r(GGGGCC) hairpin, it binds the hairpin structure (showing selective recognition of the hairpin fold over the G-quadruplex conformer), inhibits RAN translation (dose-dependently reducing RAN-translated poly(GP) and poly(GA) proteins in (GGGGCC)-expressing cells and patient-derived iNeurons), reduces foci formation in neurons (decreasing the fraction of RNA-foci-positive nuclei by up to 50 without altering total C9ORF72 mRNA levels, confirming a post-transcriptional mechanism), and exhibits low cytotoxicity as well as minimal off-target effects on antisense r(CCCCGG)exp or canonical translation, establishing the proof-of-concept that small molecules can target this RNA and ameliorate toxicity.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 8-cycloheptyl-1,3,7-trimethylpurine-2,6-dione, also referred to as CP5 which contains a cycloheptane ring connected to a complex imidazolinone and dimethylurea heterocyclic system, is a xanthine derivative similar to caffeine; CP5 has a bulky, multi-heterocyclic structure that showed binding but was likely hampered by poor specificity, preventing its selection for detailed study, while this compound may have bronchodilator, myocardial stimulant, or central nervous system modulatory activity, and is often explored in drug development as a new phosphodiesterase (PDE) inhibitor.

RNA information: The bacterial riboflavin (FMN) riboswitch is located in the 5' untranslated region (5' UTR) of mRNA and consists of an aptamer domain and an expression platform. It can specifically bind to FMN and regulate the expression of the downstream key riboflavin synthesis gene ribB through conformational changes. When FMN is sufficient, the riboswitch forms a terminator/sequestering loop to inhibit gene expression; when FMN is deficient, it switches to an antiterminator/anti-sequestering loop, allowing gene transcription and translation to achieve negative feedback regulation. This riboswitch exists exclusively in bacteria and has no homologous structure in mammals, making it a highly selective target for new antibacterial drugs (such as ribocil).
Small_molecule_information: 2-[1-[[2-(methylamino)pyrimidin-5-yl]methyl]piperidin-3-yl]-4-thiophen-2-yl-1H-pyrimidin-6-one, also referred to as Ribocil, is a synthetic small molecule with an overall U-shaped rigid skeleton, this complex molecule has a substituted pyrimidin-6-one core, linked at the 2-position to a substituted piperidine ring, which is further connected to a 2-(methylamino)pyrimidinyl-methyl group, the 4-position is substituted with a thiophenyl group, its left 6-thienyl-pyrimidinone ring can intercalate into the stacking plane of the RNA double helix, while the right methylamino-piperidinyl group forms weak hydrogen bonds with key guanines, such multi-heterocyclic and complex structures are characteristic of many small-molecule drugs, this compound can highly specifically bind to the aptamer pocket of the bacterial FMN riboswitch, mimicking the conformation of the natural ligand FMN and blocking the binding of FMN to RNA, thereby relieving the negative feedback inhibition of the riboswitch on the riboflavin synthesis gene ribB, leading to impaired bacterial growth due to riboflavin depletion, and importantly, its activity can be completely reversed by exogenous riboflavin, meanwhile this compound is often designed to target specific protein kinases, by inhibiting key kinases, it may find applications in the treatment of cancer, inflammation, or autoimmune diseases.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₄ sequence is an intermediate-length repeat consisting of four triplets, forming a more stable stem–loop than the dimer but still shorter than pathogenic expansions. Structurally, it offers a limited number of binding sites for ligands, sometimes resulting in inconsistent stoichiometry. Its reduced size diminishes the cooperativity observed in longer repeats, and it can behave differently depending on ligand structure. Functionally, r(CUG)₄ serves as a bridge model between minimal repeats and pathological expansions, highlighting how the length of the repeat influences recognition dynamics. However, it cannot fully mimic the complex misfolded conformations that drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 5 (2012), is the E-isomer counterpart of compound 4, differing only in the configuration of the olefin bridge, and this geometric change alters the positioning of the benzo[g]quinoline group relative to the peptide scaffold, leading to slightly different recognition properties, while it retains overall high binding capability, though its cooperativity and selectivity are reduced compared to the Z-isomer, and structurally, it highlights how subtle stereochemical differences strongly affect RNA recognition, and biologically, it is still active in cellular models and displaces pathogenic complexes, demonstrating that both isomers contribute valuable insights into structureactivity relationships for RNA-binding molecules; it is a highly complex, multi-cationic peptidomimetic structure, its core is the (E)-oct-4-enediamide scaffold, with side chains derived from proline, 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl, and lysine, the molecule contains multiple primary amino groups, rendering it poly-cationic at physiological pH, the 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline is a bulky, highly hydrophobic aromatic group, this combination of poly-cationic character, multiple chiral centers, and large hydrophobic groups makes it suitable as an anti-tumor agent, a DNA/RNA binding agent, or a novel antibiotic, and the (E)-alkene provides a specific, rigid conformation.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-4-amino-2-[(1S,2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(2R,3R,5S,6R)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-5-hydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, is a member of the aminoglycoside antibiotic class, structurally composed of multiple aminosugar units, including an aminocyclohexyl core linked by glycosidic bonds to other aminopyranose units. Like its congeners, the compound is characterized by multiple primary and secondary amine groups that are protonated and polycationic at physiological pH, a feature essential for its biological function. Its mechanism involves binding to the A-site of the bacterial ribosome (which is equivalent to binding to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes) to disrupt protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and premature termination of protein synthesis, effectively killing the bacteria. Based on its specific arrangement of sugar units and stereochemistry, this compound corresponds to a specific aminoglycoside drug, possibly related to amikacin or netilmicin. These drugs are commonly used clinically as second-line or combination therapy for serious conditions like endocarditis, septicemia, and pulmonary infections caused by susceptible organisms, and are also used to treat Gram-negative infections, particularly Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-N-(2-piperidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine diiodide. This is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium derivative with diiodide ions, featuring a 4-methylmorpholin-4-ium quaternary ammonium side chain and a 2-piperidin-1-ylethyl substituted chain. The molecule structurally integrates two different saturated heterocycles (morpholine and piperidine). The potent cytotoxicity is a key feature of this class of compounds. In practical applications, it is regarded as a potential broad-spectrum germicide and antitumor agent. Its mechanism involves complex interactions with biological membranes, DNA, and RNA, with the quaternary ammonium structures being particularly important for its antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

RNA information: A designed Watson–Crick stem-loop RNA with a 7-bp stem and 4-nt loop (AGUG). It serves as a non-G4 control structure. Ligands 2a and 2b bind to it with lower affinity , confirming their specificity for G4 structures.
Small_molecule_information: 4,11-bis(2-aminoethylamino)naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzofuran-5,10-dione, also referred to as a furan-fused anthraquinone derivative with bis(2-aminoethylamino) side chains, is a compound based on a Naphthoquinone core fused with a Benzofuran ring, resulting in a Naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzofuran-5,10-dione structure, substituted with two 2-aminoethylamino groups at positions C-4 and C-11. Anthraquinone/Naphthoquinone compounds are a well-known class of DNA topoisomerase inhibitors and DNA intercalators, forming the basis of many chemotherapy drugs (e.g., Mitoxantrone). Additionally, these quinone structures readily undergo redox cycling, generating Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) that cause DNA damage and cell apoptosis, and this compound also exhibits high cellular uptake, strong stabilization of KRAS RG4s and specific binding affinity, which enables it to effectively inhibit translation and induce apoptosis in pancreatic cancer cells, meaning it is therefore primarily researched as a novel anti-tumor agent with a mechanism involving dual DNA intercalation and ROS-mediated cytotoxicity.

RNA information: CG2a is a highly conserved, guanine-rich RNA sequence located in the core gene of hepatitis C virus (HCV) genotype 2a. It folds into a stable parallel G-quadruplex (G4) structure, which serves as an innate structural marker within the viral RNA genome. This non-canonical configuration is critical for its role as a specific molecular target, allowing for selective recognition without the need for genetic labels or modifications. Its conservation across clinical isolates makes it an ideal target for diagnostic and research applications.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(3,6-dimethyl-1,3-benzothiazol-3-ium-2-yl)-N,N-diethylaniline, also referred to as ThT-NE, is a quaternary ammonium salt dye containing benzothiazole and diethylaniline moieties, which is a rationally designed small-molecule fluorogenic probe based on a benzothiazole scaffold. Its structure features a benzothiazolium ring as the core, linked at the 2-position to a diethylaniline group, with specific structural modifications that enhance its binding affinity and selectivity for RNA G-quadruplex structures like CG2a, and has the molecular formula C19H23N2S (typically associated with a counterion like iodide). This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and a permanent positive charge, lending it strong fluorescent properties; in its free state, it remains non-fluorescent due to intramolecular rotation, but upon binding to CG2a, it undergoes restriction of molecular motion, resulting in strong fluorescence enhancement, and has high membrane permeability and low cytotoxicity. In biological function, such compounds are commonly employed as fluorescent probes or cell stains to label live cells, mitochondria, or specific intracellular structures for bioimaging studies, and it enables rapid, specific, and real-time visualization of native HCV RNA in live cells, supporting dynamic studies of viral infection and replication.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 7-hydroxy-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Genistein, a naturally occurring isoflavone (a type of phytoestrogen, with Daidzein being another soy-derived isoflavone with estrogenic and antioxidant properties that is studied for its role in hormone-related conditions), has the formula C15H10O4; its core structure is the chromen-4-one backbone, substituted with a 4-hydroxyphenyl group at C3 and a hydroxyl group at C7, and it is abundant in soybeans and soy-derived products as a principal component of soy isoflavones, in terms of real-world biological activity, Genistein is known for its ability to mimic or antagonize estrogen due to its structural similarity to 17-estradiol thus earning the classification of phytoestrogen often used to alleviate menopausal symptoms, importantly, Genistein is a potent tyrosine kinase inhibitor particularly of the Src family kinases which has led to intense interest in cancer research for its potential to inhibit tumor growth in models of breast and prostate cancer, and it also possesses significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, the xanthene moiety bears 3,6-dihydroxy groups, which facilitate the formation of the emissive quinoid structure, while the 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro substituents on the benzofuranone ring can significantly modify its fluorescence properties, such as increasing quantum yield or causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)benzenecarbothioamide, also referred to as 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole, is a combination of benzimidazole and carbothioamide structures, and 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole is a heterocyclic small molecule with a benzimidazole core linked to a thiocarbamoyl-substituted phenyl group, the benzimidazole core is often associated with antiparasitic, antifungal, antiviral, and antineoplastic activities, the carbothioamide group may enable the molecule to act as a metal chelator or enzyme inhibitor, the structure suggests the molecule may serve as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting on cell division or specific enzyme systems, unlike myricetin, it lacks reported affinity for CAG-repeat RNAs in HD/SCAs but may interact with structured RNAs via benzimidazole intercalation and thiocarbamoyl H-bonding, it shows no validated activity against polyQ aggregation or mitochondrial dysfunction in HD models, though its benzimidazole motif could potentially target nucleic acid mismatches, making it a speculative scaffold for TRED-focused optimization with further biophysical validation needed. -

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 7 (the tetra-N-methylated triazole analog), is a complex, linear peptidic analog that has the highest affinity for HIV-1 FSS RNA among its class, binds to the target RNA with stronger affinity than Compound 3, shows reduced selectivity in the presence of tRNA, potently inhibits HIVIIIB replication, significantly suppresses a multidrug-resistant HIV strain while protecting infected cells from cytopathic effects, has increased toxicity above a certain concentration in infected MT-2 cells and possesses the strongest antiviral efficacy in its series which confirms the impact of full N-methylation on potency, its structure features two units containing (2S)-phenylalaninyl-3-aminopropylamine and (2S)-proline fragments linked by a 1,4-disubstituted 1,2,3-triazole ring, with both termini acylated by a 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl group, similar to N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide and N-[(2S)-1-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide, this compound upgrades the quinoline core to benzo[g]quinoline, and the phenylalanine amine and the two benzo[g]quinoline amide bonds are substituted with N-methyl groups, it incorporates the benzo[g]quinoline aromatic heterocycle and contains multiple chiral centers and amide bonds, this compound adopts a non-planar, flexible conformation, the benzo[g]quinoline core confers stronger aromatic stacking and hydrophobic interaction capabilities, and the 3-aminopropylamine group enhances hydrophilicity and cationic properties, in terms of biological function, peptides and their analogs generally have the potential to act as receptor ligands, enzyme inhibitors, or signaling molecules, and benzo[g]quinoline compounds typically possess diverse biological activities.

RNA information: xpt-pbuX riboswitch is an guanine-sensing RNA regulatory element located in the 5' untranslated region of Bacillus subtilis mRNA, consisting of a conserved aptamer domain and an expression platform. Its aptamer folds into a three-way connection structure composed of three stems, P1, P2, and P3, and two rings, L2 and L3. The core area precisely recognizes and closely binds guanine through seven hydrogen bonds. After guanine binding, it stabilizes the P1 stem, promotes the formation of a terminator stem in the downstream expression platform, blocks the transcription of the downstream xpt-pbuX gene, and thereby inhibits the expression of purine transporters. In the absence of guanine, the aptamer conformation is loose, and the 3' end of P1 can form an antiterminator structure with the expression platform, allowing transcription to continue. This riboswitch dynamically regulates gene expression by sensing intracellular guanine concentration, maintains purine metabolic balance, and can be activated by artificially designed guanine analogues (such as G7) to achieve antibacterial effects.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(phenylmethoxyamino)-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one, also referred to as G3 (a derivative obtained by substituting the C2 position of guanine with an n-phenylformamide group (-N-CO-NH-CH2-PH)), is characterized by a phenylmethoxyamino (benzyloxyamino, C6H5CH2ONH) substituent at the 2-position of the purine ring, a relatively unique structural unit involving an ether-amine linkage, while G3 can bind to xpt-pbuX riboswitch aptamer and induce RNA to undergo structural changes similar to natural guanine, yet it only retains good in vitro binding activity but lacks significant antibacterial function as its inhibition of Bacillus subtilis growth is weak at relevant concentrations; the benzyloxy group often serves as a protecting group or a structural modification to increase lipophilicity, which can impact the molecules ability to cross cellular membranes, biological activity studies of such purine derivatives typically focus on their capacity to disrupt purine metabolism due to their structural similarity to natural purine bases, given its amino structure, 2-(phenylmethoxyamino)-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one may interact with the active sites of certain enzymes as a competitive or non-competitive inhibitor, and in real-world applications, similar molecules might be used as molecular probes in drug discovery to gain insights into the catalytic mechanisms or binding sites of specific target proteins, such as nucleoside reductases or guanylate cyclases.

RNA information: 1x1 UU has one 5’CUG/3’GUC motif . It binds compound 2 with 1:1 stoichiometry , 4-fold lower affinity vs r(CUG)₁₂. Used to study 2’s binding cooperativity to longer RNAs, aiding analysis of 2’s specificity for r(CUG)exp in DM1.
Small_molecule_information: 1,8-diamino-3,6-dipyrrolidin-1-yl-2,7-naphthyridine-4-carbonitrile, also referred to as Compound 1 (2) (N-(3-(1H-imidazol-1-yl)propyl)-2-(pyrrolidin-1-yl)quinolin-4-amine), is a compound featuring a highly substituted 2,7-naphthyridine scaffold with multiple amino and pyrrolidine substituents, and a cyano group at position 4, with the formula C19H23N7, and Compound 1 (2), identified via Inforna, selectively binds miR-544 precursors Dicer site and r(CUG)exp U loops, naphthyridine cores are often investigated as fluorescent dyes and DNA binders, the high polarity and multiple hydrogen bond sites suggest good water solubility and potential biological activity, it is likely designed as a DNA minor groove binder, aiming to interfere with replication and transcription processes through non-covalent binding, thus having prospects in antimicrobial and anticancer research, it inhibits miR-544 biogenesis, derepresses mTOR, disrupts ATMmTOR axis (induces TNBC apoptosis to a notable extent, with significant efficacy vs antagomirs), displaces MBNL1, improves DM1 splicing/translation, and disperses foci, a single i.p. injection of a low dose abolishes MDA-MB-231 tumor growth (low toxicity), acting as an RNA-directed probe for TNBC and DM1. RNADNA

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: N-[9-[4-(dimethylamino)anilino]-6-(3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanoylamino)acridin-3-yl]-3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanamide, also referred to as Braco-19, is an acridine derivative with multiple amine and pyrrolidine side chains; Braco-19 is a classic, high-affinity G-quadruplex ligand and telomerase inhibitor which, like other quadruplex binders in this study, effectively bound the r(G4C2)exp quadruplex in vitro but failed to significantly inhibit RAN translation in cells, underscoring that quadruplex binding alone is insufficient to block the toxic pathway, while acridines are a known class of anti-tumor drugs that work by DNA intercalation and are studied as high-affinity nucleic acid binders to overcome multidrug resistance (MDR).
