Small_molecule_information: 6,7-dimethoxy-2-piperazin-1-ylquinazolin-4-amine, also referred to as DPQ, is the unsubstituted piperazine amine, serving as the common intermediate or active metabolite for the 1-[4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-2-methylpropan-1-one to 6,7-dimethoxy-2-(4-methylsulfonylpiperazin-1-yl)quinazolin-4-amine series; discovered through NMR screening, this compound is a novel small molecule that specifically binds to the (A-A)-U internal loop within the influenza A virus RNA promoter, whose dimethoxy group mediates key interactions with nucleobases in the internal loop such as G13, C22, A11, and A12 thereby widening the major groove, functionally its binding straightens the overall promoter conformation and disrupts adjacent base pairs, this conformational change competitively inhibits the viral RdRp from binding to its promoter site, consequently it effectively inhibits the replication of multiple influenza strains including H1N1, H3N2, and type B virus demonstrating moderate antiviral activity and low cellular cytotoxicity and thus represents a promising lead compound targeting a highly conserved viral RNA element, while in real-world pharmacology, due to its free secondary amine, it is itself an 1-adrenergic receptor antagonist, the secondary amine can be highly protonated at physiological pH, increasing its polarity and potentially leading to higher concentrations in peripheral tissues, and as a core structure, its activity is fundamental to understanding the activity of its acylated derivatives.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-fluorobenzoyl)-9-methyl-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 7, incorporates a fluorine atom on the benzoyl ring which improved its binding properties, showed good affinity for CAG RNA and was used to study the effect of halogen substituents; this is a complex pentacyclic fused heterocycle based on the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold, a fused chromone and pyridine system, featuring an amino group at the 3-position, a phenyl group at the 4-position, a methyl group at the 9-position, and a 4-fluorobenzoyl group at the 2-position, and chromone structures are found in natural products and various anti-inflammatory and antiallergic drugs, while this rigid, multi-ring, highly substituted structure suggests potential as a kinase or phosphodiesterase (PDE) inhibitor candidate, potentially for anti-cancer or anti-inflammatory applications, and the 4-fluoro substitution on the benzoyl group is a common modification to improve metabolic stability and lipophilicity.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(2E,6E,10E,14E,18E,22E,26E,30E,34E)-3,7,11,15,19,23,27,31,35,39-decamethyltetraconta-2,6,10,14,18,22,26,30,34,38-decaenyl]-5,6-dimethoxy-3-methylcyclohexa-2,5-diene-1,4-dione, also referred to as Coenzyme Q10 (ubiquinone), is a non-azaaromatic lipid-soluble compound and a large structure consisting of an extremely long polyisoprenoid chain (ten isoprene units) attached to a methoxy-substituted benzoquinone core that lacks planar azaaromatic heterocycles, this structure is characteristic of Ubiquinone (Coenzyme Q) or its higher homologs, it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, and its core real-world activity is its role as a vital electron carrier in mitochondrial respiration and an antioxidant unrelated to azaaromatic sensing, specifically serving as a key electron carrier in the mitochondrial electron transport chain, the highly lipophilic polyisoprenoid tail anchors it within the lipid bilayer of the inner mitochondrial membrane, consequently, this compound is known to enhance cellular energy production, exhibit antioxidant properties, and protect against oxidative damage, and it finds critical practical use as a nutritional supplement or co-treatment for conditions like heart failure, Parkinsons disease, and age-related disorders. Coenzyme Q10Erythromycin

RNA information: BS-xpt(A73G) is a mutant of Bacillus subtilis BS-xpt guanine riboswitch, with A at position 73 (aptamer core, near ligand-binding site) mutated to G. Structurally, this mutation disrupts the wild-type core folding. Functionally, it abolishes 2AP binding (unlike natural G73-containing aptamers) and impairs guanine binding, potentially reducing its in vivo transcriptional termination regulation efficiency compared to wild-type BS-xpt.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-2,7-bis[[(2S)-6-amino-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]hexanoyl]amino]-N-[(2S)-1-(2-aminoethylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N-[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N,N-dipropyloct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 9 (2012), is a highly complex asymmetric peptidomimetic bearing the benzo[g]quinoline moiety; while benzo[g]quinoline is a necessary aromatic element for relevant interactions, DCC 9 (2012) has an altered peptide framework that disrupts the orientation required for strong and selective recognition, resulting in a weaker interaction profile, limited ability to discriminate among target sequences, and minimal biological effects, which confirms that benzo[g]quinoline is necessary but not sufficient and that correct positioning within the molecular scaffold is equally critical, highlighting its role as a negative control and supporting evidence for the structureactivity relationship developed across the compound series. The molecules core eight-carbon chain contains a trans (E) double bond, and its most significant feature is the unequal length of the polyamine chains attached to the two termini: one is a 3-aminopropylamine (three carbons) and the other is a 2-aminoethylamine (two carbons); this asymmetric polyamine structure is designed to potentially cause differences in binding affinity or mode with biological targets, and the molecule is symmetrically linked to benzo[g]quinoline-lysine derivatives on both sides, integrating DNA intercalation capability with polycationic electrostatic binding, with this design combining asymmetry and dual-targeting groups aiming to investigate how regulating asymmetric conformation can enhance the selectivity and multi-action efficacy against specific biological targets, holding potential application in multi-target anti-cancer therapy.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: (2S)N[(2R)1[[(2S)1[[(2R)1[[(2S)1[(2amino2oxoethyl)[3[4[(9hydroxy5,11dimethyl6Hpyrido[4,3b]carbazol2ium2yl)methyl]triazol1yl]propyl]amino]1oxopropan2yl]methylamino]1oxopropan2yl]methylamino]1oxopropan2yl]methylamino]1oxopropan2yl]2[[2[3[4[(9hydroxy5,11dimethyl6Hpyrido[4,3b]carbazol2ium2yl)methyl]triazol1yl]propylamino]acetyl]methylamino]Nmethylpropanamideis a dimeric, also referred to as 2HE-5NMe modularly assembled small molecule designed to bind specifically to expanded r(CGG) repeats (r(CGG)exp) that cause fragile X-associated tremor ataxia syndrome (FXTAS). It consists of two 9-hydroxy-N-propargylellipticine (HE-alkyne) modules conjugated to an N-methyl peptide backbone, optimized to span the repeating 5CGG/3GGC motifs in the RNA hairpin. In vitro, 2HE-5NMe binds r(CGG)12 with high affinity (Kd 5065 nM), increases RNA thermal stability, and selectively disrupts r(CGG)protein interactions. In cellular models, it displaces sequestered splicing factors from nuclear foci, corrects pre-mRNA splicing defects, and inhibits repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation without affecting canonical translation. Notably, 2HE-5NMe dissolves preformed RNA foci within 4 h and reduces toxic polyglycine protein aggregates, demonstrating superior potency and faster kinetics than its monomeric precursor 1a. These findings establish 2HE-5NMe as a promising chemical probe and potential therapeutic lead for FXTAS and related repeat-expansion disorders.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(6,8-dimethylimidazo[1,2-a]pyrazin-2-yl)-7-(4-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl)chromen-2-one, also referred to as C41, is a non-planar coumarin derivative featuring a 6,8-dimethylimidazo[1,2-a]pyrazine group at position 3 and the 4-methylpiperazin-4-ium cation at position 7, carrying a 1 charge, which recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand; the replacement of the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine scaffold with the imidazo[1,2-a]pyrazine scaffold is a common scaffold-hopping strategy in medicinal chemistry, employed to optimize activity, selectivity, and pharmacokinetic properties, the two methyl groups increase hydrophobicity, while the imidazo-pyrazine structure itself may possess different electronic properties and biological activity profiles, and the quaternary ammonium positive charge is key for enhancing water solubility and electrostatic binding, the overall structure suggests a ligand targeting specific biological targets, such as kinases or receptors, with the potential for developing new targeted therapeutics.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,3-N-bis[3-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,3-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound H7, is a dicationic molecule based on a meta-phthalic acid diamide (benzene-1,3-dicarboxamide) core and a bis-benzimidazole derivative of Hoechst 33258 that was rationally optimized through 3-D shape-based similarity screening to enhance RNA-binding affinity and selectivity for the pathogenic r(CUG)exp RNA that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Its central scaffold is a meta-substituted benzene ring, with both ends similarly attached to two 3-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl substituents, while it retains the DNA-binding benzimidazole core of Hoechst 33258 but is elongated with a hydrophobic alkynyl-phenyl tail that improves its shape complementarity to the major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat. Compared to N-[3-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]-4-[4-[[3-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]carbamoyl]phenyl]benzamide (a para-connected core), the meta arrangement of the two amide bonds on the central phenyl ring causes the entire molecule to adopt a V-shape or bent conformation rather than a linearly extended one, and in a TR-FRET displacement assay, it dissociates MBNL1 from r(CUG)12 with potency about two-fold more than Hoechst 33258 yet is less selective than H1, binding with similar affinity to RNAs containing 5CUG/3GCC or 5CGG/3GGC motifs and to the human A-site. This alteration in molecular geometry is crucial for DNA minor groove binders, as the DNA minor groove itself possesses curvature, and in DM1 cell models, a certain dose of this compound partially shifts a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal, but cytotoxicity becomes evident at higher doses. Dicationic molecules with a bent shape may be better suited to bind specific curved regions of the DNA minor groove, potentially leading to increased sequence selectivity, and this compound exemplifies how computational shape similarity can evolve a DNA-binding dye into a low-micromolar chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics, consequently, it is studied in the search for highly specific antitumor or antiviral lead compounds.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroacridin-9-amine, also referred to as 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroacridine-9-amine, is a hydrogenated acridine derivative with the acridine core, where the benzene ring is fused with a dihydropyridine ring, and the 1,2,3,4 positions are hydrogenated, with an amino group at position 9, molecular formula C13H16N2; the compounds core is a partially saturated tricyclic acridine structure with some lipophilicity, and the amino group imparts basicity. In terms of real-world activity, such acridine derivatives, due to their unique planar tricyclic structure and amino side chain, are often studied as DNA intercalators, showing potential in anticancer and antibacterial fields; specifically, this compound shares structural similarities with the well-known Alzheimers drug tacrine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor), which makes it a derivative of tacrine with potential acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity, suggesting its potential for Central Nervous System activity as a candidate for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimers disease treatment, and it may also possess topoisomerase inhibitory activity, making it valuable in antitumor drug development.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxy-3-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Rutin, is a naturally occurring flavonoid glycoside found in plants, known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and vasoprotective properties; Rutin is widely used as a dietary supplement and medicinal agent due to its potent antioxidant and free-radical scavenging activities, it stabilizes blood vessels, reduces capillary fragility, may protect against oxidative stress-related diseases, clinically it is known for its Vitamin P activity, primarily used to strengthen capillary walls, reduce capillary permeability, and treat conditions like varicose veins, hemorrhoids, and capillary fragility, it also exhibits anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties, and its poor water solubility limits bioavailability, but derivatives are explored for enhanced delivery.

RNA information: RNA4 contains the UU internal loop for compound 1 binding, but the GG loop targeted by compound 2 has been mutated to a GC base pair. This mutation significantly reduces the binding affinity of dimeric compound 3, confirming the necessity of the GG loop for high-affinity bivalent binding.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoyl-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Targaprimir-96, is the parent, non-covalent small molecule designed to bind selectively to the Drosha processing site of pri-miR-96, specifically targeting a UU internal loop within its secondary structure, and by binding to this site, it inhibits the biogenesis of oncogenic mature miR-96, which leads to the upregulation of FOXO1 protein expression and subsequently induces apoptosis in triple-negative breast cancer cells, while showing no significant effect in healthy breast epithelial cells, demonstrating its selective, phenotype-modulating efficacy; this compound is a complex benzimidazole, triazole, and amide derivative, whose structure contains multiple benzimidazole rings, piperazine groups, tert-butyl groups, and a lengthy peptidic mimicry chain, resulting in an extremely high molecular weight and high polar surface area, it presents as a large molecule with extreme complexity and multiple potential hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, and its structure is indicative of a potential Anti-cancer Agent or Antiparasitic Agent (Macromolecular Ligand), often designed to interact with DNA or essential biomacromolecular targets (such as protein kinases or enzymes), leveraging its benzimidazole core and polyamine linkers.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: N,N-diethyl-N-[5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]ethane-1,2-diamine diiodide is a dicationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline analog where the piperidinium is replaced by a morpholinium quaternary ammonium salt. This fused quinoline core is a common scaffold in medicinal chemistry, primarily screened for cytotoxic activity against various cancer cell lines.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-[5-[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]phenyl]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole, also referred to as DB60 (furimidazoline, 2,5-bis-[4-(2-imidazolin-2-yl)phenyl]furan), is a symmetric or near-symmetric structure centered around a biphenyl-linked furan ring, with a 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) group attached to each end. The entire molecule forms a conjugated system consisting of two imidazolinium groups, two phenyl groups, and a furan ring, where the imidazolinium groups are connected to the 2- and 5-positions of the furan ring via the phenyl linkers. Imidazolinium groups are typically weakly basic and can bind to biomacromolecules like DNA or RNA, and such polyaromatic heterocyclic systems often possess fluorescent properties. This compound was originally optimized for anti-Pneumocystis carinii activity but has been repurposed as a selective HIV-1 TAR RNA antagonist, binding the pyrimidine bulge of the TAR hairpin, displacing the Tat protein, blocking Tat-mediated trans-activation, stabilizing TAR structure, and suppressing HIV-1 LTR-driven gene expression without cytotoxicity, illustrating a clear RNA-versus-DNA selectivity switch compared with amidine congeners, and identifying it as a lead structure for developing small-molecule inhibitors targeting the TARTat axis of HIV-1 replication.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-amino-4-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one, also referred to as Phenylquinolone 1, is a quinolinone core derivative (and a 2-phenylquinolone derivative with N1-methyl (RMe), 5-methoxy (ROMe), 7-methoxy (ROMe) substituents on the quinolone nucleus) connected via a propoxy chain (n3, XO, one of the shortest flexible linkers) to a piperidinyl head (piperidine ring), which is synthesized via Suzuki couplings and nucleophilic displacement, can inhibit HIV-1 TatTAR interaction but has low cellular uptake, and this propoxy chain potentially offers the most compact binding conformation; functionally, the shortening of the side chain may affect the molecules binding specificity to targets, and in practical applications, amine compounds with three-carbon side chains are often used in the development of novel anti-inflammatory agents or antimalarial drugs, as this length has been shown to be optimal in many bioactive molecules.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 4-amino-1-[3-[(17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-yl)oxy]propyl]pyrimidin-2-one is a complex structure featuring a giant cationic polycyclic scaffold connected via a propoxy linker to a cytosine derivative (4-aminopyrimidin-2-one). The core pentacyclic framework (henicosa-octaene) has multiple fused aromatic rings, ether linkages, and a quaternary ammonium center (13-azonium), giving it high lipophilicity and a positive charge. The pyrimidinone moiety, a nucleobase analog, is introduced to facilitate its interaction with biomacromolecules like DNA or RNA. Functionally, compounds with this large cationic polycyclic structure are often studied as novel antimicrobial agents or cytotoxic agents. Their activity is presumed to be based on strong binding to cell membranes and nucleic acids due to their cationic nature. Potential applications include screening for broad-spectrum antibacterial drugs or novel anti-tumor lead compounds.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-benzoyl-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 1, is one of the parent derivatives of the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold, relatively simple with only amino at 3, benzoyl at 2, and phenyl at 4, lacking the halogen atom at position 9 of the core, and it is a pyridocoumarin derivative that showed moderate binding affinity to the disease-related r(CAG)exp RNA, with a higher preference for the AA-mismatched RNA motif over the canonical AU-paired RNA and demonstrated potential in reducing polyglutamine aggregation in cellular models, though it was not among the top leads; this unsubstituted structure is typically used to establish the intrinsic biological activity of the scaffold, due to its large planarity and conjugated system, this compound is considered a chromophoric molecule, in medicinal chemistry, its structure suggests it could be a DNA intercalator or a broad-spectrum bioactive small molecule, while in photophysics and photochemistry, it may be investigated as a fluorescent probe or photosensitizer for bioimaging or photodynamic therapy.

RNA information: BH-guaA refers to the guanine riboswitch from Bacillus halodurans (a type of salt-tolerant bacillus). The structural features of this ribose switch include a secondary structure composed of three stem ring regions (P1, P2 and P3), which are organized around a tridirectional junction point, forming a ligand binding site capable of specifically recognizing and binding to guanine. The structure of BH-guaA is highly conserved in the core region. Except for the specific positions mentioned in the article (such as 24, 48 and 73) where variations may exist, these variations may affect its ligand binding affinity. Functionally, BH-guaA regulates gene expression through its binding to guanine in the aptamer region. When guanine combines with aptamer, it causes a rearrangement of RNA structure. This structural change can lead to the termination or continuation of transcription, thereby controlling the expression of downstream genes. This regulatory mechanism of the BH-guaA ribose switch enables it to respond rapidly when the intracellular guanine concentration changes, thereby playing a key role in the metabolic regulation and gene expression of bacteria.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Luteolin (a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects that exhibits antioxidant activity and may influence gene expression), is a naturally occurring flavone compound (distinguished from flavonols like 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one by the lack of a hydroxyl group at C3), with the formula C15H10O6. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and a catechol moiety (3,4-dihydroxyphenyl) on the B ring. Luteolin is widely found in celery, parsley, broccoli, cabbage, and various medicinal herbs. In real-world biological activity, Luteolin demonstrates powerful antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, making it an important active component in traditional herbal medicine and modern supplements. It operates through multiple mechanisms, including free radical scavenging, inhibition of various inflammatory mediators, and modulation of immune cell function. Furthermore, it is extensively studied for its neuroprotective, anti-allergic, and potential anticancer properties, such as by inhibiting tumor cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]-N-[6-[4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]hexyl]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-HD, is a highly complex bis-benzimidazole dimer (double-headed molecule) where two bis-benzimidazole units are connected by a long, flexible hexamethylene (hexyl) linker, and 2H-HD is based on a hexanediamine spacing motif, making it more extended than ED or BD analogues, dimerization is a strategy used to increase binding affinity or achieve bivalent targeting, this dimer is designed to be an extremely potent DNA minor groove binder, potentially spanning several base pairs, it showed comparable moderate potency to 2H-BD, again suggesting that small changes in linker length influence the ability to bind structured RNA repeats, its potency was lower than the most potent oligoglycine-based derivatives, its potential activity is as a highly effective antineoplastic or antiparasitic agent, cellular assays and further optimization were not reported for this compound, and the molecule remains part of the initial comparative analysis.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxy-3-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Rutin, is a naturally occurring flavonoid glycoside found in plants, known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and vasoprotective properties; Rutin is widely used as a dietary supplement and medicinal agent due to its potent antioxidant and free-radical scavenging activities, it stabilizes blood vessels, reduces capillary fragility, may protect against oxidative stress-related diseases, clinically it is known for its Vitamin P activity, primarily used to strengthen capillary walls, reduce capillary permeability, and treat conditions like varicose veins, hemorrhoids, and capillary fragility, it also exhibits anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties, and its poor water solubility limits bioavailability, but derivatives are explored for enhanced delivery.

RNA information: The bacterial riboflavin (FMN) riboswitch is located in the 5' untranslated region (5' UTR) of mRNA and consists of an aptamer domain and an expression platform. It can specifically bind to FMN and regulate the expression of the downstream key riboflavin synthesis gene ribB through conformational changes. When FMN is sufficient, the riboswitch forms a terminator/sequestering loop to inhibit gene expression; when FMN is deficient, it switches to an antiterminator/anti-sequestering loop, allowing gene transcription and translation to achieve negative feedback regulation. This riboswitch exists exclusively in bacteria and has no homologous structure in mammals, making it a highly selective target for new antibacterial drugs (such as ribocil).
Small_molecule_information: 2-[1-[[2-(methylamino)pyrimidin-5-yl]methyl]piperidin-3-yl]-4-thiophen-2-yl-1H-pyrimidin-6-one, also referred to as Ribocil, is a synthetic small molecule with an overall U-shaped rigid skeleton, this complex molecule has a substituted pyrimidin-6-one core, linked at the 2-position to a substituted piperidine ring, which is further connected to a 2-(methylamino)pyrimidinyl-methyl group, the 4-position is substituted with a thiophenyl group, its left 6-thienyl-pyrimidinone ring can intercalate into the stacking plane of the RNA double helix, while the right methylamino-piperidinyl group forms weak hydrogen bonds with key guanines, such multi-heterocyclic and complex structures are characteristic of many small-molecule drugs, this compound can highly specifically bind to the aptamer pocket of the bacterial FMN riboswitch, mimicking the conformation of the natural ligand FMN and blocking the binding of FMN to RNA, thereby relieving the negative feedback inhibition of the riboswitch on the riboflavin synthesis gene ribB, leading to impaired bacterial growth due to riboflavin depletion, and importantly, its activity can be completely reversed by exogenous riboflavin, meanwhile this compound is often designed to target specific protein kinases, by inhibiting key kinases, it may find applications in the treatment of cancer, inflammation, or autoimmune diseases.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 8-cycloheptyl-1,3,7-trimethylpurine-2,6-dione, also referred to as CP5 which contains a cycloheptane ring connected to a complex imidazolinone and dimethylurea heterocyclic system, is a xanthine derivative similar to caffeine; CP5 has a bulky, multi-heterocyclic structure that showed binding but was likely hampered by poor specificity, preventing its selection for detailed study, while this compound may have bronchodilator, myocardial stimulant, or central nervous system modulatory activity, and is often explored in drug development as a new phosphodiesterase (PDE) inhibitor.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: [5-[8-(dimethylamino)-7-methyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl]-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as RoFMN (Roseoflavin Mononucleotide), is a phosphate-containing pteridine derivative and a phosphorylated derivative of roseoflavin (RoF), a natural antibacterial compound; its structure features the 8-(dimethylamino)-7-methylbenzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione core, with the terminal of the tetrahydroxypentyl side chain attached at N10-position being phosphorylated, which is similar to that of flavin mononucleotide (FMN) but features a dimethylamino substituent at the N10 position of the flavin ring, and the molecular formula is C18H24N5O9P; this compound is a substituted analog of Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN), the phosphate group increases its water solubility and gives it a negative charge under physiological conditions, and this structural difference enables it to specifically bind to and regulate the FMN riboswitch, thereby inhibiting bacterial growth, by binding to the FMN riboswitch, it downregulates the expression of genes related to riboflavin biosynthesis and transport, exerting its antibacterial effects, and studies have shown that its antibacterial activity is partially attributed to its binding to the FMN riboswitch, which can effectively suppress bacterial growth and reproduction; in terms of biological function, as a flavin coenzyme analog, it may act as an inhibitor or alternative cofactor for oxidoreductases, thereby affecting cellular metabolic processes.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N-[8-[2-(dimethylamino)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

RNA information: RNA4 contains the UU internal loop for compound 1 binding, but the GG loop targeted by compound 2 has been mutated to a GC base pair. This mutation significantly reduces the binding affinity of dimeric compound 3, confirming the necessity of the GG loop for high-affinity bivalent binding.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 1, is a mono-benzimidazole Click Chemistry probe with tert-butyl and azide groups, which is a benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module previously identified by the Informa platform to avidly bind a 1x1 nucleotide UU internal loop within the Drosha processing site of the pri-miR-96 hairpin precursor, inhibits miR-96 biogenesis and induces apoptosis in breast cancer cells with activity that limits its in vivo utility, shows no detectable binding to DNA, and its real-world activity is dedicated to biological orthogonal labeling experiments for target validation and mechanism elucidation.

RNA information: r(AAUU): This RNA serves as a model hairpin construct designed with a specific AU-rich sequence in its stem region, featuring a contiguous 5'-AAUU/3'-UUAA stretch. Its primary function is to act as a tool for probing the binding specificity and affinity of small molecules towards defined AU base pair orientations. The RNA was used in fluorescence-based binding assays, where it helped establish that certain benzamidine-based compounds, like molecule 2, display a significant binding preference for AU base pairs over GC-rich counterparts. Its thermodynamic stability is lower than that of GC-rich hairpins, which is a characteristic feature influencing its interaction with small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-[4-[N-(3-azidopropyl)carbamimidoyl]phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as AU-azide, is a highly functionalized probe molecule with the molecular formula C24H23N7O, and AU-azide is a derivative of Compound 2 with an azide group for conjugation; it exhibits even higher AU selectivity and is used to build multivalent compounds like dimers, its core is a planar diaryl-furan (biphenyl-furan) system, which provides hydrophobicity and - stacking ability, the molecule contains two key functional groups: carboximidamide and azido, the carboximidamide group (-C(NH)NH2) is typically positively charged at physiological pH, allowing it to interact strongly with negatively charged biomolecules like DNA; thus, amidine derivatives are often used as DNA minor groove binders with anti-tumor or antimicrobial activity, the azido group (-N3) is a prominent bio-orthogonal chemistry functional group, specifically a core component of Click Chemistry, which permits the molecule to be covalently linked to alkyne-containing partners under specific conditions, therefore, this compound is designed as a bifunctional molecular probe for target identification and bio-orthogonal chemical labeling.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: methyl N-(4-chlorophenyl)-2H-quinoline-1-carboximidothioate, also referred to as CP2, is a quinoline-based molecule featuring a thione and imine functionality (which incorporates a quinoline core linked to a thiocarbamate group and a 4-chlorophenyl ring, with the combination of its planar aromatic system and halogen substituent contributing to its affinity for CAG repeat RNA and efficacy in reducing polyQ aggregation in cellular models, marking it as a promising candidate). Quinoline scaffolds are associated with various anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities, and this complex structure is a candidate for research as an enzyme inhibitor, possibly targeting microbial enzymes, due to its specialized reactive groups.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₁₂ RNA is a repetitive hairpin-forming sequence that adopts a stable secondary structure characterized by regularly spaced U–U mismatches within a C–G base-paired stem. These noncanonical interactions create specific binding pockets that sequester the splicing regulator MBNL1, thereby disrupting its normal function and driving disease phenotypes such as mis-splicing in myotonic dystrophy type 1. The structured repeats also provide discrete molecular recognition elements that can be selectively targeted by small molecules, enabling competitive displacement of MBNL1 and partial restoration of RNA processing functions.
Small_molecule_information: 1,8-diamino-3,6-dipyrrolidin-1-yl-2,7-naphthyridine-4-carbonitrile, also referred to as Compound 1 (2) (N-(3-(1H-imidazol-1-yl)propyl)-2-(pyrrolidin-1-yl)quinolin-4-amine), is a compound featuring a highly substituted 2,7-naphthyridine scaffold with multiple amino and pyrrolidine substituents, and a cyano group at position 4, with the formula C19H23N7, and Compound 1 (2), identified via Inforna, selectively binds miR-544 precursors Dicer site and r(CUG)exp U loops, naphthyridine cores are often investigated as fluorescent dyes and DNA binders, the high polarity and multiple hydrogen bond sites suggest good water solubility and potential biological activity, it is likely designed as a DNA minor groove binder, aiming to interfere with replication and transcription processes through non-covalent binding, thus having prospects in antimicrobial and anticancer research, it inhibits miR-544 biogenesis, derepresses mTOR, disrupts ATMmTOR axis (induces TNBC apoptosis to a notable extent, with significant efficacy vs antagomirs), displaces MBNL1, improves DM1 splicing/translation, and disperses foci, a single i.p. injection of a low dose abolishes MDA-MB-231 tumor growth (low toxicity), acting as an RNA-directed probe for TNBC and DM1. RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-2,7-dihydroxy-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]-1,2-diaminopurin-6-one, also referred to as N1-aminoguanosine-3,5-cyclic monophosphate, is another modified guanine nucleotide derivative, but its purine ring on the base is uniquely modified (1,2-diaminopurin-6-one) and the ribose part contains a cyclic phosphonate structure, similar to the one in 9-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-2,7-dihydroxy-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]-2-amino-8-bromo-1H-purin-6-one, and N1-aminoguanosine-3,5-cyclic monophosphate is a modified cyclic nucleotide, featuring a guanosine core with an amino group at the N1 position of its purine ring and a 3,5-cyclic phosphate linkage, this cyclic phosphate structure is characteristic of natural cyclic nucleotides (like cAMP or cGMP), which act as crucial intracellular signaling molecules, structurally, it retains the cyclic nucleotide scaffold but has an additional amine moiety that enhances hydrogen bonding and alters charge distribution, the real-world activity of this non-natural cyclic nucleotide analog is likely as a modulator or inhibitor of phosphodiesterases (PDEs) or as an agonist/antagonist of adenylate cyclases (ACs), thereby interfering with the cAMP or cGMP signaling pathways, functionally, it acts as a cGMP signaling modulator, targeting cyclic nucleotide-dependent pathways, competing with endogenous cGMP for binding to effector proteins, and serving as a tool to study cyclic nucleotide-mediated signal transduction, such signal interference is of significant interest in research for treating cardiovascular diseases, inflammation, and neurological disorders, with potential applications in investigating cardiovascular and neuronal signaling cascades due to its modified interaction with cGMP-dependent enzymes.

Small_molecule_information: (3S,4S)-3-methoxy-2-methyl-4-(methylamino)-29-oxa-1,7,17-triazaoctacyclo[12.12.2.12,6.07,28.08,13.015,19.020,27.021,26]nonacosa-8,10,12,14,19,21,23,25,27-nonaen-16-one, also referred to as Staurosporine, is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound and a giant molecule with eight fused rings, multiple heteroatoms (N, O), and numerous unsaturations, this highly complex chemical name reflects its intricate molecular structure, which is almost certainly derived from a microbial secondary metabolite, belonging to a family of highly complex natural antibiotics or cytotoxic molecules; in addition, this compound can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant structural changes in the RNA, has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA and can activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, these characteristics indicate that it is an important tool for studying the functional mechanism of 108yjdF RNA, while in terms of real activity, such complex structures typically possess potent cytotoxicity and antitumor activity, potentially acting through mechanisms like DNA cleavage, topoisomerase inhibition, or microtubule disruption.

RNA information: G-quadruplex TERRA is a well-characterized, stable RNA G-quadruplex structure formed by the human telomeric repeat sequence. It serves as a canonical model for studying RNA G-quadruplex interactions. In this study, it was used as a control to demonstrate that although known G-quadruplex ligands bind TERRA with high affinity (low nM Kd), they are poor inhibitors of C9orf72 RAN translation. This key finding confirms that the toxic pathology in c9ALS/FTD is driven primarily by the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp rather than the G-quadruplex form, highlighting the therapeutic importance of targeting the specific hairpin structure.
Small_molecule_information: N-[9-[4-(dimethylamino)anilino]-6-(3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanoylamino)acridin-3-yl]-3-pyrrolidin-1-ylpropanamide, also referred to as Braco-19, is an acridine derivative with multiple amine and pyrrolidine side chains; Braco-19 is a classic, high-affinity G-quadruplex ligand and telomerase inhibitor which, like other quadruplex binders in this study, effectively bound the r(G4C2)exp quadruplex in vitro but failed to significantly inhibit RAN translation in cells, underscoring that quadruplex binding alone is insufficient to block the toxic pathway, while acridines are a known class of anti-tumor drugs that work by DNA intercalation and are studied as high-affinity nucleic acid binders to overcome multidrug resistance (MDR).

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: 2-ethyl-5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazol-2-ium-9-ol, also referred to as Compound 1c (a bis-benzimidazole analog that retains the 9-hydroxy-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazolium core of 1a but carries a shortened 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl tail in place of the piperidinium side chain), is a pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole quaternary ammonium salt analog; this compound preserves high-affinity recognition of the 5CGG/3GGC internal loops within the r(CGG)exp hairpin and maintains relevant potency in disrupting the pathogenic r(CGG)12DGCR8 complex, can restore a large proportion of wild-type SMN2 and Bcl-x pre-mRNA splicing in FXTAS-model COS-7 cells expressing relevant CGG repeats while halving both the number and nuclear volume of r(CGG)exp-containing foci without altering Sam68-independent transcripts or gross cellular viability, and has favorable aqueous solubility, low cytotoxicity and efficient synthesis which make it a validated chemical probe for dissecting r(CGG)exp-mediated toxicity and an attractive alternate lead scaffold for developing therapies against FXTAS and related CGG-repeat disorders, while due to its known DNA intercalation properties, it is also researched as a potential anticancer drug and Topoisomerase II inhibitor.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-7-(pyrrolidin-1-ium-3-ylamino)chromen-2-one, also referred to as SMSM28, is a non-planar coumarin analog bearing a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and an imidazopyridine DE-fused system, carrying a 1 charge, which has the coumarin core, with the 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl group at C3, the C7 position is substituted with the pyrrolidin-1-ium-3-ylamino group, connected by an amine linker, pyrrolidine is a five-membered saturated nitrogen heterocycle that, when quaternized, forms a compact cation, compared to six-membered rings, pyrrolidine offers a smaller, more rigid cationic structure, this small cationic side chain modification is frequently used to fine-tune the molecules membrane permeability and binding specificity, it selectively recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1, with the binding mediated by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking interactions, and electrostatic attraction, and its selectivity has been corroborated through structureactivity relationship (SAR) comparisons, since pyrrolidine derivatives are common in neuropsychiatric drugs, it might be a potential drug candidate targeting CNS receptors, leveraging its fluorescence for high-throughput screening.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[11-[3-(dimethylamino)propylamino]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]ethyl-trimethylazanium diiodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine and quaternary ammonium salt side chains. The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[4-[(2-amino-4-oxo-7,8-dihydro-3H-pteridin-6-yl)methylamino]benzoyl]amino]pentanedioic acid, also referred to as Dihydrofolic acid (DHF), is commonly known as Folic acid, or Vitamin B9, with the molecular formula C19H19N7O6, and Dihydrofolic acid (DHF) is a folate derivative with a structure comprising a pteridine ring, p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), and glutamate moiety, lacking one reduced hydrogen pair vs. tetrahydrofolate (THF), its structure comprises a pteridine ring, a p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) residue, and a glutamic acid residue, its pteridine core enables one-carbon transfer reactions, in terms of real-world activity, Folic acid is an essential water-soluble vitamin for humans and many organisms, it is reduced in vivo to the active form, Tetrahydrofolate (THF), which serves as a one-carbon unit carrier, participating in vital biochemical reactions, including the synthesis of nucleic acid precursors like purines (A and G) and thymidine (T), as well as amino acid metabolism, functionally, DHF acts as a key intermediate in folate metabolism, converted to THF by dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), it supports nucleotide biosynthesis and amino acid metabolism, clinically, Folic acid is used for the treatment of megaloblastic anemia and is crucially administered during early pregnancy to prevent fetal neural tube defects, the folic acid metabolic pathway is also a key target for many anticancer drugs (e.g., Methotrexate) and antimicrobial agents, and its analogs are used as anticancer/antibacterial agents by inhibiting DHFR to disrupt cellular proliferation.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(2R,3S,4R,5S)-5-[(1R,2R,3S,5R,6S)-3,5-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3-amino-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]oxy-6-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol;sulfuric acid, also referred to as Paromomycin sulphate, is an aminoglycoside antibiotic existing as a sulfate salt, and Paromomycin sulphate is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, causing misreading of mRNA and inhibiting protein synthesis and is used to treat parasitic and bacterial infections, its core structure, (2S,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(2R,3S,4R,5S)-5-[(1R,2R,3S,5R,6S)-3,5-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3-amino-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]oxy-6-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, is similar to (2R,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(1R,2R,3S,4R,6S)-4,6-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,5R)-4-[(2R,3R,4R,5S,6S)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-4,5-dihydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxy-5-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, consisting of multiple aminosugar units linked via glycosidic bonds, featuring numerous amino and hydroxyl groups, the presence of multiple amino groups makes it strongly polycationic, which is the structural basis for its biological activity, allowing it to specifically bind to the bacterial ribosome and disrupt protein synthesis, its formulation as a sulfate salt is typical for this class of drugs, enhancing its water solubility and stability for clinical applications such as injection or solution preparation, this molecule is highly likely to be a clinically relevant antibiotic, such as a derivative of streptomycin, kanamycin, or tobramycin, employed as a broad-spectrum bactericidal agent primarily against aerobic Gram-negative infections, with caution required due to the risk of ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.

Small_molecule_information: 1-[10-(4-amino-2-methylquinolin-1-ium-1-yl)decyl]-2-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-amine, also referred to as dequalinium, is a bis-quaternary ammonium salt compound where two quinoline units are linked by a long carbon chain, with 4-amino-2-methylquinoline as the repeating unit: the two quinoline units are bridged at their quaternary N1 atoms by a decyl (decyl) carbon chain, making the molecule carry two positive charges, with a calculated molecular formula of CHN, and this nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound exhibits significant structural and functional properties when interacting with Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, as it can specifically bind to the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant changes in RNA structure that are highly similar to those when binding to other nitrogen-containing aromatic compounds such as proflavine and chelerythrine, indicating that the binding pocket of 108yjdF RNA has a broad recognition ability for such compounds, and it has a relatively high binding affinity with 108yjdF RNA and can activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, which further confirms its functional role in gene regulation, while although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, dequalinium, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch; such dicationic compounds, especially long-chain linked aromatic cations, are well-known disinfectants and antimicrobial agents, and also show high affinity for DNA/RNA.

RNA information: miR-1587 is a G-rich microRNA that forms a stable parallel G-quadruplex structure, confirmed by biophysical methods. It directly targets and downregulates TAGLN, an important tumor suppressor and marker of smooth muscle differentiation, at both mRNA and protein levels. Its function is structurally modulated: KCl-induced G-quadruplex formation inhibits its activity by blocking seed region access. Ligands further regulate its function—pseudopalmatine stabilizes the quadruplex and enhances suppression, while TMPyP4 disrupts the structure and restores miRNA-mediated repression.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,10,11-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium, also referred to as Pseudopalmatine, is another protoberberine-class alkaloid, sharing the tetramethoxy and quaternary ammonium structure with 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, and Pseudopalmatine is a natural alkaloid ligand identified for its specific interaction with the G-quadruplex structure of miR-1587, it features a planar conjugated structure that enables it to bind to and significantly stabilize the pre-formed RNA G-quadruplex, increasing its thermal melting temperature to a notable extent in a low ionic strength environment, its activity profile in reality is multi-targeted, including DNA intercalation (leading to anticancer activity) and modulation of metabolic enzymes, the modified position of the methoxy groups is a critical factor in structure-activity relationship studies to investigate the spatial fit between the molecule and its targets (like DNA, Topoisomerase), giving it unique potential for developing targeted antineoplastic or antimicrobial agents, this binding and stabilization event locks miR-1587 into its folded quadruplex conformation, which renders the miRNAs seed sequence inaccessible for base-pairing with its target mRNA, consequently, pseudopalmatine treatment functionally inhibits miR-1587 activity, disrupting its interaction with the TAGLN 3UTR and leading to a derepression of the target gene, which is observed as a notable increase in luciferase reporter activity, it exhibits no significant effect on its own when the target site is mutated, confirming its action is specifically through modulating the miRNAs secondary structure rather than non-specific effects.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Kaempferol is found in a variety of plant foods, such as tea, broccoli, grapefruit, and herbs. Functionally, as a dietary flavonol, Kaempferol possesses significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and potential anticancer properties, it modulates cell signaling pathways (e.g., PI3K/Akt) and is studied for cardiovascular and neurodegenerative disease prevention, research also suggests it can regulate apoptosis, inhibit angiogenesis, and suppress cell proliferation, thereby potentially inhibiting tumor development, its role in the prevention of chronic diseases and the maintenance of cardiovascular health is particularly studied, although its bioactivity may differ from other flavonols due to the single hydroxyl group on its B ring.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(Z,2S,7S)-7-amino-1,8-bis[(2S)-2-[[(E)-3-(3-aminopropylamino)-3-oxo-1-phenylprop-1-en-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-1,8-dioxooct-4-en-2-yl]-2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 7Z (a one-armed benzo[g]quinoline analog in which one heteroaromatic intercalating group is removed), is a peptidomimetic molecule characterized by a cis (Z) double bond in its core eight-carbon chain; the Z-configuration enforces a rigid bent conformation on the main chain, which is crucial for the molecules ability to achieve precise spatial arrangement and relative distance between its functional groups (like benzo[g]quinoline and polyamine side chains) when binding to a biological target (such as a receptor pocket or DNA minor groove), and this molecule combines a benzo[g]quinoline group (for DNA intercalation potential) with a dehydro-phenylalanine derivative (for enzymatic stability), while Compound 7Z retains moderate affinity for the HIV-1 FSS RNA (showing about a 200-fold decrease compared to fully dimeric analogs), this loss of potency highlights the structural necessity of both intercalating arms for strong and selective RNA binding, and though it does interact with the FSS, its affinity is insufficient to significantly alter frameshifting or inhibit viral infectivity, serving instead as a structural probe that validated the importance of full dimer symmetry in the active series, and this integrated design, combining a rigid bent conformation with enzymatic stability, aims to discover a ligand with high conformational specificity for its target, potentially applicable as a novel anti-inflammatory or anti-cancer drug that acts by precisely matching the geometry of the target site.

RNA information: This RNA serves as a defined model for the pathogenic expanded repeat in FXTAS. It folds into a stable intramolecular hairpin structure characterized by repeating 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. These loops adopt a specific syn-anti conformation that forms three hydrogen bonds, contributing to the hairpin's significant thermodynamic stability (ΔG°₃₇ ≈ -5.6 kcal/mol). This structured hairpin acts as a high-affinity binding platform, effectively sequestering proteins like DGCR8 and Sam68. The primary function of this RNA in the study is to demonstrate that its stable self-structure poses a major energetic barrier for duplex formation with complementary oligonucleotides, a key finding that highlights the challenge of targeting it with antisense approaches.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-3, is an ultra-long bis-head molecule based on the bis-benzimidazole-DNA binder, characterized by the inclusion of four repeating polyamide units as a flexible spacer arm between the two active units, while 2H-3 itself incorporates three propylamine spacers between Ht-N3 modules to increase flexibility and the potential to span larger RNA repeat distances, its added length enhances its ability to engage multiple binding sites resulting in improved activity in cell-based assays and it shows slightly better selectivity than earlier analogs, functionally it can restore FXTAS-associated splicing defects in model systems more robustly than Ht-N3 likely due to a closer alignment with RNA repeat spacing, though its scaffold flexibility still prevents maximal preorganization restricting affinity compared with optimized derivatives like 2H-5; this compounds further increased spacer length is intended for the systematic study of the influence of linker length on the spatial adaptability and binding affinity of multivalent DNA binders, a longer spacer allows the two benzimidazole heads to bind to more distantly separated DNA sites or maintain flexible mobility in more complex biological environments, therefore it is a key molecule for structure-activity relationship studies, potentially applicable in long-range DNA cross-linking or multi-target anti-cancer drugs.
