Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Luteolin (a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects that exhibits antioxidant activity and may influence gene expression), is a naturally occurring flavone compound (distinguished from flavonols like 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one by the lack of a hydroxyl group at C3), with the formula C15H10O6. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and a catechol moiety (3,4-dihydroxyphenyl) on the B ring. Luteolin is widely found in celery, parsley, broccoli, cabbage, and various medicinal herbs. In real-world biological activity, Luteolin demonstrates powerful antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, making it an important active component in traditional herbal medicine and modern supplements. It operates through multiple mechanisms, including free radical scavenging, inhibition of various inflammatory mediators, and modulation of immune cell function. Furthermore, it is extensively studied for its neuroprotective, anti-allergic, and potential anticancer properties, such as by inhibiting tumor cell proliferation and inducing apoptosis.

RNA information: MALAT1 is a well-studied long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) known to form a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure consisting of three G-tetrads. It functions as a key regulatory molecule in processes such as alternative splicing modulation. The rG4 structure within MALAT1 has been confirmed in vitro through techniques including reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays and fluorescence-based methods using probes like NMM and ThT. Studies show that in potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, MALAT1's rG4 forms efficiently, causing significant reverse transcriptase stalling (~60%). Notably, ligands such as PhenDC3 and PDS can act as molecular chaperones, inducing and stabilizing MALAT1's rG4 structure even under unfavorable lithium (Li⁺) conditions, highlighting its dynamic structural plasticity and potential as a therapeutic target.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₄ sequence is an intermediate-length repeat consisting of four triplets, forming a more stable stem–loop than the dimer but still shorter than pathogenic expansions. Structurally, it offers a limited number of binding sites for ligands, sometimes resulting in inconsistent stoichiometry. Its reduced size diminishes the cooperativity observed in longer repeats, and it can behave differently depending on ligand structure. Functionally, r(CUG)₄ serves as a bridge model between minimal repeats and pathological expansions, highlighting how the length of the repeat influences recognition dynamics. However, it cannot fully mimic the complex misfolded conformations that drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,11S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,11-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]dodec-6-enediamide is a complex, symmetrical dimeric peptidic analog. Its structure is based on a C12 diene-diamide (dodec-6-enediamide, E-configured double bond) core, with the 2 and 11 positions linked to two N-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)proline units, and the termini connected to (2S)-2-(3-aminopropylamino)-6-aminohexanoyl (a lysine derivative) groups. It incorporates the benzo[g]quinoline aromatic heterocycle and contains multiple chiral centers and amide bonds. This compound has an amphiphilic structure. The benzo[g]quinoline core confers stronger aromatic stacking and hydrophobic interaction capabilities, and the two free amino groups (on the side chain and terminus of the lysine derivative) likely make it cationic at physiological pH. In terms of biological function, peptidic analogs generally have the potential to act as receptor ligands or enzyme inhibitors; its cationic and hydrophobic nature may confer membrane permeability or the ability to bind negatively charged biomolecules (such as DNA/RNA), and benzo[g]quinoline compounds, like their quinoline isomers, typically possess diverse biological activities.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR(WT) RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription. Small_molecule_information: N-pentan-3-yl-4-[5-[4-(N-pentan-3-ylcarbamimidoyl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as DB226 (2,5-bis-[4-(N-isopentylamidino)phenyl]furan), is a bis-amidine compound containing biphenyl and furan structures, which is a bis-cationic diphenylfuran congener in which the terminal amidines carry branched isopentyl side-chains; its structure is based on the benzene and furan parent skeletons: two benzene rings are linked by a furan ring, and the para-position (position 4) of each benzene ring is substituted with an N-pentan-3-ylcarboximidamide group; in terms of biological function, amidine compounds are often used as antiparasitic agents (such as for Trypanosoma and Plasmodium), antibacterial agents, or as DNA binding agents, and this compound was originally optimized for anti-Pneumocystis carinii (PCP) therapy, with its lipophilic modification strengthening minor-groove binding to AT-rich DNA and preserving high anti-PCP potency in rat models, while when tested against the HIV-1 TAR RNA target, the extra bulk reduces RNA affinity, showing no measurable suppression of Tat-driven trans-activation in the relevant cellular assay, and melting-temperature experiments record a modest Tsubm/sub for the DB226TAR complex, with RNase A footprints requiring a certain concentration for detectable protection of the GCUCU site opposite the bulge, confirming low-occupancy binding; like its analogues, DB226 recognizes the three-dimensional cavity created by the pyrimidine bulge rather than specific nucleotidesmutation of certain sites has little influence, whereas deletion of the bulge abolishes interaction; the compound is non-toxic to reporter cells below a specific concentration and retains full anti-PCP efficacy, illustrating that enlarging the alkyl-amidine substituent tips selectivity toward DNA while diminishing RNA recognition, thus, although DB226 itself is not an effective anti-HIV lead, its profile further defines structureselectivity relationships guiding the design of next-generation TAR-targeted diphenylfurans.

RNA information: 5′GUG/3′C_C: This asymmetric loop motif was found both in HCV 3′ UTR stem–loop I and stem–loop III. It represents a recurrent viral structural element and was highlighted in Table 1 as a key binding site. Small molecules engaging this motif displayed antiviral activity, linking it to functional inhibition of HCV replication.; 5′UUA/3′A_U: This internal loop motif contains a three-nucleotide bulge opposite two nucleotides with a mismatch. It was identified as a preferred binding site for several small molecules, including Compounds 7 and 8, with submicromolar affinity. Functional analysis demonstrated that the motif occurs in HCV 3′ UTR stem–loops, where small molecule binding interferes with viral replication. Its asymmetric structure provides a selective recognition element for ligands designed to target RNA.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[3-[[2,5-bis[[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]carbamoyl]phenyl]carbamoylamino]phenyl]carbamoylamino]-1-N,4-N-bis[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 7, is an extremely large, highly branched polycationic compound, and it can selectively bind internal loop motifs such as 5UUA/3A_U and 5UGU/3G_A with submicromolar affinity, with structural studies revealing that its binding is driven by recognition of asymmetric bulges within RNA stems, it can directly interact with HCV 3 UTR stemloops I and II by targeting motifs present in viral RNA, functional assays have demonstrated that it inhibits HCV replication in cell culture without affecting host ribosomal RNAs, and RNase H/ASO mapping has confirmed its intracellular engagement of the predicted binding sites; its intricate scaffold contains multiple phenyl rings, amide bonds, and urea groups, terminated by several 4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl substituents, structural analysis indicates that this molecule bears at least six imidazolinyl groups, making it a potent hexacationic system, this structural design is an extreme example used to construct supramolecular nucleic acid binders, where the molecular length, rigidity, and multiple charges enable high-affinity interactions with multiple sites on the DNA helix, including the minor groove, major groove, or phosphate backbone, and such compounds are often investigated as non-viral gene delivery vectors (by charge-neutralizing DNA), highly effective anticancer agents, or for use as DNA structural probes, aiming for extremely high binding stability and cytotoxicity.

RNA information: The bacterial riboflavin (FMN) riboswitch is located in the 5' untranslated region (5' UTR) of mRNA and consists of an aptamer domain and an expression platform. It can specifically bind to FMN and regulate the expression of the downstream key riboflavin synthesis gene ribB through conformational changes. When FMN is sufficient, the riboswitch forms a terminator/sequestering loop to inhibit gene expression; when FMN is deficient, it switches to an antiterminator/anti-sequestering loop, allowing gene transcription and translation to achieve negative feedback regulation. This riboswitch exists exclusively in bacteria and has no homologous structure in mammals, making it a highly selective target for new antibacterial drugs (such as ribocil).
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-2,6-diamino-N-[12,17-bis[[(2S)-2,6-diaminohexanoyl]amino]-4-pentacyclo[6.6.6.0{2,7}.0{9,14}.0{15,20}]icosa-2(7),3,5,9(14),10,12,15(20),16,18-nonaenyl]hexanamide is a vast and immensely complex molecule consisting of a polycyclic cage-like core (a pentacyclo-nonaenyl structure) and multiple attached Lysine (2,6-diaminohexanoic acid) residues. The cage core suggests applications in supramolecular chemistry, while the multiple lysine residues provide numerous positive charge centers. This sophisticated structure is most likely researched in supramolecular chemistry or biomaterials science. It may be designed as a highly effective metal ion chelator (for heavy metal removal or as a radiotracer ligand) or as a peptidomimetic with a unique 3D structure, potentially for targeting specific protein-protein interactions or as an antiviral agent by interfering with viral envelope proteins.

Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-[5-[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]furan-2-yl]phenyl]-4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole, also referred to as DB60 (furimidazoline, 2,5-bis-[4-(2-imidazolin-2-yl)phenyl]furan), is a symmetric or near-symmetric structure centered around a biphenyl-linked furan ring, with a 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazole (imidazolinium) group attached to each end. The entire molecule forms a conjugated system consisting of two imidazolinium groups, two phenyl groups, and a furan ring, where the imidazolinium groups are connected to the 2- and 5-positions of the furan ring via the phenyl linkers. Imidazolinium groups are typically weakly basic and can bind to biomacromolecules like DNA or RNA, and such polyaromatic heterocyclic systems often possess fluorescent properties. This compound was originally optimized for anti-Pneumocystis carinii activity but has been repurposed as a selective HIV-1 TAR RNA antagonist, binding the pyrimidine bulge of the TAR hairpin, displacing the Tat protein, blocking Tat-mediated trans-activation, stabilizing TAR structure, and suppressing HIV-1 LTR-driven gene expression without cytotoxicity, illustrating a clear RNA-versus-DNA selectivity switch compared with amidine congeners, and identifying it as a lead structure for developing small-molecule inhibitors targeting the TARTat axis of HIV-1 replication.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 3,4-dimethyl-N-[2-(pyridin-1-ium-1-ylmethyl)benzoyl]-2,5-diazabicyclo[5.2.0]nona-1,3,5-triene-8-carbohydrazide;bromide, also referred to as NaphDH5 which is similar to NaphDH3 but with 1-benzylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups and exhibited good G4 stabilization but only moderate FID affinity and no biological effect, with poor drug-likeness, possesses a unique, highly strained 2,5-diazabicyclo[5.2.0]nona-1,3,5-triene core, which is a fused system of an eight-membered ring and a four-membered ring, along with a carbohydrazide functionality. The molecule contains a quaternized pyridinium ring, specifically the pyridin-1-ium-1-ylmethyl group, linked via a benzoyl moiety, with bromide as the counterion. The combination of this complex, rigid bicyclic system, the carbohydrazide group, and the cationic pyridinium suggests that 3,4-dimethyl-N-[2-(pyridin-1-ium-1-ylmethyl)benzoyl]-2,5-diazabicyclo[5.2.0]nona-1,3,5-triene-8-carbohydrazide;bromide may be explored as a non-classical -lactam analog antibiotic or as an active site inhibitor for bacterial enzymes, leveraging its rigid structure and cationic charge for target binding. RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(3R,4R,5S,6R)-2,4,5-trihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-3-yl]acetamide, also referred to as N-acetyl-D-glucosamine (GlcNAc), is a typical amino sugar derivative, with the oxane ring as its core and the stereoconfiguration (3R,4R,5S,6R), containing multiple hydroxyl groups and a hydroxymethyl group, and the amino group at position 3 substituted with an acetamide group; it functions as an essential monomer for constructing biological macromolecules such as chitin, peptidoglycan, and glycoproteins, playing key roles in cell recognition, immune response, and biosynthesis, while lacking planar hydrophobic azaaromatic features and therefore not binding tightly to the yjdF motif RNA nor triggering the corresponding riboswitch-mediated gene regulation in Bacillus subtilis

RNA information: r(GGGGCC)₈ is an RNA repeat sequence that forms a structured RNA motif capable of binding small molecules. It adopts both hairpin and G-quadruplex conformations and plays a pathogenic role in c9FTD/ALS by undergoing repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation and forming nuclear RNA foci. Small molecules targeting r(GGGGCC)₈ can inhibit these toxic events, suggesting its potential as a therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[4-(4-carbamimidoylanilino)phenyl]-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide is an indole bis-amidine derivative, containing an indole core, an aniline bridge, and two amidine groups. Indole compounds are widely found in natural products and possess various biological activities. The amidine groups impart a potential for nucleic acid binding, and the molecule is often developed as an antiprotozoal and antiviral agent. Bis-amidine-indole compounds with similar structures have been studied for the treatment of tropical diseases like African trypanosomiasis or as DNA minor groove binding probes.

RNA information: NRQ mutant is a G-base mutant of NRQ that prevents the formation of the G4 structure.

Small_molecule_information: [[(2R,3R,4R,5R)-5-(6-aminopurin-9-yl)-3-hydroxy-4-phosphonooxyoxolan-2-yl]methoxy-hydroxyphosphoryl] [(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(3-carbamoylpyridin-1-ium-1-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methyl phosphate, also referred to as -nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP), is Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide Phosphate (NADP), or its reduced form, NADPH, and -nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP) is a phosphorylated nucleotide coenzyme with a structure comprising a nicotinamide ring, adenine base, two ribose sugars, and three phosphate groups (with an extra phosphate on adenines ribose vs. NAD), its nicotinamide moiety acts as a redox active site, accepting/donating electrons, NADPH is the source of reducing power essential for biosynthesis and protecting cells from oxidative stress, the molecule is a key component in biochemical and pharmacological research, involved in a wide range of metabolic regulation, functionally, it mediates anabolic reactions (e.g., photosynthesis, lipid/nucleotide synthesis), serving as a key electron carrier in cellular redox pathways and supporting biosynthetic processes by providing reducing power in the form of NADPH. RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6: This RNA contains six consecutive CAG repeats with AA mismatches, forming multiple internal loops. It is a key target for small molecules due to its structural similarity to pathogenic expanded repeats. It shows high affinity and selective binding to compounds such as pyridocoumarin derivatives, leading to conformational changes and thermal stabilization.; The (5'CAG/3'GAC)x6 RNA contains multiple consecutive A-A mismatch loops. This multi-loop structure enhances conformational stability and ligand binding avidity, more accurately mimicking the expanded toxic repeats found in patient-derived RNA and providing a robust platform for biophysical testing.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-benzoyl-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 1, is one of the parent derivatives of the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold, relatively simple with only amino at 3, benzoyl at 2, and phenyl at 4, lacking the halogen atom at position 9 of the core, and it is a pyridocoumarin derivative that showed moderate binding affinity to the disease-related r(CAG)exp RNA, with a higher preference for the AA-mismatched RNA motif over the canonical AU-paired RNA and demonstrated potential in reducing polyglutamine aggregation in cellular models, though it was not among the top leads; this unsubstituted structure is typically used to establish the intrinsic biological activity of the scaffold, due to its large planarity and conjugated system, this compound is considered a chromophoric molecule, in medicinal chemistry, its structure suggests it could be a DNA intercalator or a broad-spectrum bioactive small molecule, while in photophysics and photochemistry, it may be investigated as a fluorescent probe or photosensitizer for bioimaging or photodynamic therapy.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 2,6-diamino-N-[2-(2,6-diaminohexanoylamino)-3-[3-[2-(methylamino)-1,3-thiazol-4-yl]anilino]-3-oxopropyl]hexanamide, also referred to as [N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-2,3-diaminopropanamide]-dilysinamide (13b), is a large, highly complex peptide/amino acid derivative, containing multiple lysine (2,6-diaminohexanoyl) residues and several amide bonds, along with a thiazole-aniline unit, and [N-(2-N-Acetylamino-4-(3-aminophenyl)thiazole)-2,3-diaminopropanamide]-dilysinamide (13b) is a synthetic nucleobaseamino acid conjugate engineered to occupy the HIV-1 TAR RNA stembulgeloop in place of the viral Tat protein, the molecule contains a total of four amine groups, resulting in multiple positive charges at physiological pH, exhibiting potent polycationic properties, it replaces the natural ribose-phosphate backbone with a metabolically stable thiazolearyl core and uses a 2,3-diaminopropanamide bridge to present two lysine side-chains for bidentate electrostatic contact with the TAR bulge, this structural design is commonly employed to mimic natural Antimicrobial Peptides (AMPs) or oligoamines, leveraging the multiple positive charges for high-affinity binding to negatively charged phospholipids in bacterial cell membranes or DNA in the nucleus, furthermore, the presence of amide bonds suggests its potential role as a substrate or inhibitor for proteases, based on its highly complex and multi-charged structure, this compound likely possesses strong antimicrobial activity by disrupting bacterial membrane potential or permeability, and it may also function as a gene transfection agent or nucleic acid binder, in fluorescence-binding assays 13b displays ultra-high affinity, adopts a 1:1 RNA-ligand stoichiometry, and retains excellent specificity against excess tRNA or dsDNA, in HIV-infected MAGIC-5B reporter cells 13b blocks Tat-mediated trans-activation with notable potency, suppresses viral replication comparably to a reference concentration of AZT, and shows no cytotoxicity up to a high tested concentration, these properties establish 13b as a metabolically stable, high-affinity artificial riboswitch ligand that selectively silences TAR-controlled transcription and constitutes a promising lead for next-generation RNA-targeted antivirals. RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[2-methoxy-4-[2-(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]phenyl]morpholine, also referred to as Compound 13, is a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium quaternary salt derivative, with a 3-methoxy-4-morpholinostyryl group at the 2-position of the quinoline ring and no substitution at the 4-position, where the 2-position is connected via a vinyl bridge to a phenyl ring substituted with methoxy and a morpholinyl group; the morpholine moiety modifies the lipophilicity and polarity of the molecule, SPR assay shows it has weak affinity for A10-RNA-Wt (ADAM10 mRNAs G-quadruplex sequence), dual-luciferase reporter assay reveals it has little effect on ADAM10 translation, it exhibits cytotoxicity toward related cell lines, and in real activity, due to its large conjugated system and positive charge, this compound is investigated as a fluorescent probe and a cytotoxic agent, the morpholine and quaternary salt combination may enhance cell membrane permeability and targeting to specific organelles (like mitochondria), thus boosting its potential anticancer or antibacterial activity.

RNA information: Prokaryotic 16S rRNA has 5', central, and 3' domains; its helix 34  is critical for spectinomycin binding. It assembles into the 30S ribosomal subunit, facilitates mRNA-tRNA interaction in translation, and is a major antibiotic target. Mutations cause bacterial resistance to spectinomycin, affecting antibiotic efficacy against pathogens.
Small_molecule_information: (1R,3S,5R,8R,10R,11S,12S,13R,14S)-8,12,14-trihydroxy-5-methyl-11,13-bis(methylamino)-2,4,9-trioxatricyclo[8.4.0.03,8]tetradecan-7-one, also referred to as Spectinomycin, mainly acts on Gram-negative bacteria like Neisseria gonorrhoeae, clinically treats gonorrhea, and bacterial resistance to it occurs via 16S rRNA mutations (e.g., G1064/C1193) which weakens its therapeutic efficacy; this compound has an extremely complex polycyclic structure with multiple hydroxyl, methylamino, and ether functional groups, its structure strongly suggests it is a natural product or a semi-synthetic analog, often associated with the macrolide antibiotic or related complex polyketide families, and given its complexity and functional groups, this compound is expected to possess potent antibacterial activity, likely by inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis or cell wall formation, positioning it as a potential anti-infective drug.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(6-methoxyimidazo[1,2-a]pyrazin-2-yl)-7-piperazin-4-ium-1-ylchromen-2-one, also referred to as C28, is a complex coumarin (chromen-2-one) derivative and a non-planar coumarin analog with a piperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, substituted at the 7-position with a quaternary ammonium piperazinium group and at the 3-position with an imidazo[1,2-a]pyrazine heterocycle, carrying a 1 charge. The quaternary piperazinium group at the 7-position grants the molecule a permanent positive charge, predisposing it to bind to negatively charged biomolecules, and it can recognize the single-G bulge of RNA1, with this binding driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics as confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand. Coumarin-class molecules are well-known for their fluorescent properties and broad bioactivities, and this molecule has the characteristics of a cationic fluorescent dye for bio-imaging; in terms of drug activity, it may be designed as a dual-mechanism anticancer or antimicrobial agent, combining the potential cytotoxicity of coumarin with the targeting capabilities of the quaternary ammonium salt.

RNA information: The bacterial riboflavin (FMN) riboswitch is located in the 5' untranslated region (5' UTR) of mRNA and consists of an aptamer domain and an expression platform. It can specifically bind to FMN and regulate the expression of the downstream key riboflavin synthesis gene ribB through conformational changes. When FMN is sufficient, the riboswitch forms a terminator/sequestering loop to inhibit gene expression; when FMN is deficient, it switches to an antiterminator/anti-sequestering loop, allowing gene transcription and translation to achieve negative feedback regulation. This riboswitch exists exclusively in bacteria and has no homologous structure in mammals, making it a highly selective target for new antibacterial drugs (such as ribocil).
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(3S)-1-[[2-(methylamino)pyrimidin-5-yl]methyl]piperidin-3-yl]-4-thiophen-2-yl-1H-pyrimidin-6-one, also referred to as Ribocil-B which is the active S-configured form of ribocil, is a compound with the defined stereochemistry at the piperidine rings 3-position as (3S); Ribocil-B has a U-shaped skeleton where the 6-thienyl-pyrimidinone ring and methylamino-piperidinyl group respectively embed into the aptamer pocket of the FMN riboswitch, forming stable stacking and hydrogen bond networks with key guanines and adenines, thereby mimicking the conformation of the natural ligand FMN, and by competitively occupying the binding site with high affinity, it blocks the negative feedback inhibition of FMN on ribB gene expression, leading to bacterial growth arrest due to riboflavin depletion with its bacteriostatic activity being completely reversible by exogenous riboflavin, while as an enantiomer, this compound will interact with chiral biological systems (enzymes, receptors) differently and consequently its pharmacological profile, including potency, efficacy, metabolism, and toxicity, is expected to differ significantly from its counter-enantiomer, which requires specific biological screening to determine its real activity.

RNA information: (CUG)₁₆ is a synthetic RNA oligonucleotide mimicking pathogenic expanded (CUG)ₙ repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it folds into a stable A-form hairpin with repeating 5'CUG/3'GUC units, featuring U-U mismatches flanked by G-C base pairs. Functionally, it high-affinity binds Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1), sequestering it to disrupt alternative splicing (DM1’s core gain-of-function mechanism) and serving in vitro for cleavage assays to screen catalytic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboximidamide, also referred to as Bisamidinium 1a, combines bis-amidine and triazine structures, which is a monomeric ligand designed with a bisamidinium groove binder and two triaminotriazine recognition units; the diamino substituent on the triazine ring gives it structural characteristics of a folic acid antagonist, similar to inhibitors of dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR); it is a weak inhibitor of the MBNL1-rCUGexp complex, shows low cytotoxicity and can partially disrupt nuclear foci and correct the IR splicing defect at high concentration, improves disease phenotypes in a dose-dependent manner in the DM1 Drosophila model (though requiring a much higher concentration to achieve an effect comparable to dimer 2a) and is well-tolerated in mice at high doses, and the molecule might be developed as an antineoplastic or antimicrobial agent by interfering with the folate metabolic pathway to inhibit the growth of rapidly proliferating cells or pathogens.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 9-methoxy-5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 4, is a pyridocarbazole derivative structurally related to the powerful natural antitumor agent Ellipticine; it is an optimized, selective lead that potently binds the GG internal loop, selectively inhibits RAN translation without affecting canonical translation, prevents hnRNP H sequestration, and visualizes RNA foci in cells (establishing the hairpin as the therapeutically relevant target), while its large planar aromatic system facilitates DNA intercalation and topoisomerase inhibition, a characteristic that makes it a highly promising candidate in the development of new generation chemotherapeutic agents for various solid tumors.

RNA information: 5′CGG/3′GGC is a 1×1 GG internal-loop RNA motif of the 5'CNG/3'GNC family, featuring CGG/GCC pairing, no U·U mismatches, high stability, and low "breathing effects". Linked to FXTAS (mutant FMR1 mRNA’s r(CGG) repeats), it acts as a disease-specific RNA switch, recruiting ligands like D2, D6, and 2H-5 . It also serves as a minimal binding unit for affinity characterization and an experimental control for RNA-small molecule binding specificity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as 2H-5, is an ultra-long flexible bis-head benzimidazole molecule with seven repeating polyamide units, 2H-5 employs five propylamine spacers which produce a distance between Ht-N3 units closely mimicking the natural periodicity of 5CGG/3GGC motifs in r(CGG)exp, this structural optimization endows it with the highest potency and selectivity within the series with minimal loss of activity in the presence of tRNA, it also has strong binding affinity to r(CGG)12 and is the most effective compound at restoring splicing defects in FXTAS cellular models without interfering with downstream ORF translation, making it the optimal candidate, and this compound represents a further increase in length from the six-unit analogue (N-[3-[4-[[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[1-[3-[4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanoylamino]propyl]triazol-4-yl]methylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]-propylamino]acetyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[3-[5-[5-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide), in drug development, this minor length increase is vital for determining the optimal physiological length of the linker arm, the design with this maximum length is intended to simulate or achieve the farthest effective binding distance between proteins or nucleic acids, and this molecules potential real-world activity is focused on highly specific and low-toxicity targeted anti-cancer drugs, acting by long-range cross-linking or chelation of biomacromolecules to disrupt cellular function.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. Small_molecule_information: 3-[[11-[3-(diethylamino)propylamino]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]propyl-trimethylazanium diiodide is a benzofuroquinoline bis-quaternary ammonium salt derivative, similar to N-[7-[(1,1-dimethylpiperidin-1-ium-4-yl)methoxy]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]-N,N-diethylpropane-1,3-diamine diiodide. Its structure is based on the benzofuroquinoline scaffold, with the 5-position nitrogen quaternized, and the propane-amine group on the 7-position side chain also quaternized (trimethylammonium), with diiodide as the counterion (molecular formula C31H43N4O22I). This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and two positive charges. The structural difference from N-[7-[(1,1-dimethylpiperidin-1-ium-4-yl)methoxy]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]-N,N-diethylpropane-1,3-diamine diiodide may influence its spatial orientation and DNA binding mode, making it a powerful potential DNA-targeting anti-cancer agent.

RNA information: NRQ mutant is a G-base mutant of NRQ that prevents the formation of the G4 structure.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated quaternary salt analogue of 4a-10, is a quaternary ammonium salt built upon a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core, and this compound is the N5-methylated quaternary salt analogue of 4a-10, similar to other large planar cationic molecules, it is expected to exhibit strong DNA binding/intercalation and potential topoisomerase inhibition, making it a target for anticancer drug development, the N5-methylated analogue of 4a-10 showed notable affinity for the NRQ, consistent with other methylated derivatives, it showed poor selectivity, it exhibited notable activity in A375 cells.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: methyl 7-hydroxy-6-(hydroxymethylidene)-4,9-dioxo-10H-phenazine-1-carboxylate, also referred to as lomofungin, is a derivative of phenazine, a core structure found in natural pigments with anthraquinone-like features, substituted with hydroxyl, keto, and methyl carboxylate groups; it is a natural antimicrobial agent produced by Streptomyces lomondensis that undergoes spontaneous dimerization in DMSO to form dilomofungin, and in real activity, phenazine compounds are well-known for their redox activity, enabling them to generate reactive oxygen species, this compound has potential antimicrobial activity as certain phenazine natural products are potent antibiotics, while it has also been identified as a potent inhibitor of MBNL1-CUG RNA binding, binds selectively to RNA hairpins containing CUG repeats particularly those with UU mismatches and disrupts the interaction between MBNL1 protein and pathogenic CUGexp RNA, in cellular models of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) it partially restores MBNL1-dependent alternative splicing and increases the diffuse nucleoplasmic localization of MBNL1 indicating functional reversal of RNA toxicity, though its dimeric form dilomofungin, while more potent in vitro, unexpectedly stabilizes CUGexp RNA and leads to its accumulation in nuclear foci highlighting a potential limitation in the therapeutic use of high-affinity RNA ligands, and these findings suggest that it represents a promising scaffold for developing small-molecule therapies against DM1 provided that off-target effects on RNA turnover are carefully managed.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[(5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]pentanoic acid is the benzofuroquinoline core conjugated via the glycine-glycine-arginine (Gly-Gly-Arg) tripeptide. It combines the enzyme-cleavable Gly-Gly linker with the cell-penetrating properties of arginine. This structure is used in the development of highly targeted and cytotoxic prodrugs, leveraging enzyme-mediated activation for specific release and arginine to enhance cellular uptake and nuclear localization.

RNA information: TERRA (Telomeric Repeat-containing RNA) is a long non-coding RNA essential for telomere maintenance. It forms a canonical RNA G-quadruplex (rG4) structure characterized by three G-tetrads, which is more stable than non-canonical two-tetrad rG4s. In vitro studies using reverse transcriptase stalling (RTS) assays demonstrate that TERRA's rG4 causes significant stalling (~40%) under potassium-rich (K⁺) conditions, confirming its stable formation. This structural integrity is crucial for its role in telomere length regulation and heterochromatin formation. Notably, ligands like PhenDC3 and PDS act as effective molecular chaperones, significantly inducing and stabilizing the TERRA rG4 structure even under unfavorable ionic conditions (Li⁺), highlighting its dynamic nature and functional importance in genome stability.; TERRA (telomeric repeat-containing RNA) is a well-characterized RNA that forms stable G-quadruplex structures, with its sequence featuring repeated G-tracts . It binds selectively to RGB-1, showing a 1:1.2 binding stoichiometry and inducing a Tₘ increase of the RNA G-quadruplex upon binding. As a 5'UTR insert in reporter mRNAs, it mediates translation repression by RGB-1 in vitro and HEK293 cells, serving as a model for studying RNA G-quadruplex-related translational regulation.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,6-N-bis(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-3-yl)pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide, also referred to as 360A, is a pyridodicarboxamide (PDC) derivative and an early prototype of G4 ligands initially identified by Sanofi-Aventis, its chemistry is well-developed, with conjugates like PDC-biotin (for SELEX), PDC-TO (fluorophore), BrdU-linked (visualization), and PDC-XL (cross-linking), and functionally, tritiated forms of this compound aid in demonstrating telomeric G4 existence and impact telomere integrity, while it shows some renaturing activity on rG4s but has limited molecular chaperone ability on unusual rG4s compared to PhenDC3 and PDS; this is a dicationic compound with a pyridine-2,6-dicarboxamide core, linking two 1-methylquinolin-1-ium cationic groups via two amide bonds, this rigid, highly conjugated molecule is itself a well-known G-quadruplex (G4) stabilizer ligand, its two positive charges and planar structure allow it to interact efficiently with nucleic acids, the real-world application of this compound is mainly focused on anticancer drug development and biomolecular fluorescent sensing, as a G4 stabilizer, it selectively binds to and stabilizes G4 structures found in the promoter regions of oncogenes (like cMYC) or telomeric DNA, thereby inhibiting oncogene expression or interfering with telomerase activity, achieving an anti-tumor effect.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[4-[4-[2-(diethylamino)ethoxy]anilino]quinazolin-2-yl]phenyl]-3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propanamide is a quinazoline derivative incorporating a complex N-methylpiperazine substituted propanamide side chain. This structure aims to improve solubility and bioavailability and is viewed as a precursor to a highly active kinase inhibitor for treating cancer or autoimmune diseases.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: (4R)-4-[(3R,5R,8R,9S,10S,12S,13R,14S,17R)-3,12-dihydroxy-10,13-dimethyl-2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9,11,12,14,15,16,17-tetradecahydro-1H-cyclopenta[a]phenanthren-17-yl]pentanoic acid is a secondary bile acid known as Deoxycholic acid (DCA), also referred to as its stereoisomer, which is a non-azaaromatic bile acid with a rigid steroid nucleus (four fused cycloalkane rings) and a carboxyl side chain, featuring two hydroxyl groups and lacking planar azaaromatic heterocycles; it binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, while physiologically it is involved in lipid digestion by emulsifying dietary fats in the intestine (a function unrelated to azaaromatic sensing), and in practical application, DCA is FDA-approved for the local dissolution of fat cells, particularly for treating submental fullness (double chin), where it acts by disrupting adipocyte membranes, and it is also involved in intestinal signaling and lipid metabolism regulation.

This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-[5-methyl-7-[2-(1-methylpiperidin-1-ium-1-yl)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]propane-1,3-diamine diiodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine and quaternary ammonium salt side chains. The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(1,2,4-triazol-1-ylmethyl)-1H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl]pyridine is a poly-nitrogen heterocyclic compound featuring two 1,2,4-triazole rings and one pyridine ring. One 1,2,4-triazole ring (connected to the pyridine ring) serves as the core, while the other 1,2,4-triazole ring is linked to the 5-position of the core triazole ring via a methylene bridge. The triazole ring is an important pharmacophore, widely present in antifungal drugs and antivirals. This molecule is rich in nitrogen atoms, granting it strong hydrogen bonding and coordination capabilities, suggesting potential biological activities such as antifungal, antiviral, or anticancer effects, or applications as a metal coordinating agent.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: 2-[2-(diethylamino)ethyl]-5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazol-2-ium-9-olalso referred to as Compound 1b, which is also designated Compound 2is a pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole quaternary ammonium salt (a fused PAH) known for its strong DNA intercalating ability, while Compound 1b is a positively charged bis-benzimidazole analog and 1a-derivative featuring a truncated 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl side chain instead of 1as piperidinium appendage, retaining high in vitro binding affinity to displace 1a-TO and recognize the 5CGG/3GGC internal loops of r(CGG)exp hairpins, possessing the potency to disrupt the pathogenic r(CGG)DGCR8 complex, being able to restore near wild-type SMN2/Bcl-x pre-mRNA splicing and reduce r(CGG)exp-containing foci in FXTAS-model COS-7 cells without affecting Sam68-independent transcripts or cell viability (yet failing to inhibit RAN translation in cells likely due to poor bioavailability), and having favorable aqueous solubility, low cytotoxicity and efficient synthesis to serve as a validated chemical probe for r(CGG)exp-mediated toxicity and a promising lead scaffold for FXTAS and related CGG-repeat disorders; this class of pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole quaternary ammonium salt compounds are major candidates for anti-tumor drugs, acting as Topoisomerase II inhibitors.

RNA information: ATP-54-1 is an active RNA molecule with a length of 54 nucleotides, obtained by analyzing the 5' and 3' deletions of the ATP-binding RNA clone C8-ATP-3. Structurally, its core feature is an 11-nucleotide consensus sequence flanked by base-paired stems (the deletion of the left-hand stem directly abolishes its ATP-binding activity), and all clones containing this structure have an invariant unpaired G opposite the 11-nucleotide consensus sequence, which can fold into a specific secondary structure as a whole. Functionally, it binds ATP with high affinity, with a dissociation constant (Kd) of less than 50 μM, and a conformational change of RNA occurs when binding to ATP (confirmed by kethoxal modification experiments). It exhibits specificity for ATP binding—modification or removal of groups such as the 2' hydroxyl group on ATP and the 6-amino group of adenine significantly reduces binding affinity, and it also serves as an important basis for the subsequent design of the shorter 40-nucleotide ATP-binding RNA (ATP-40-1)
Small_molecule_information: [[(2S,3R,4R,5R)-5-(6-aminopurin-9-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methoxy-hydroxyphosphoryl] phosphono hydrogen phosphate, also referred to as ADP (with ATP being its closely related triphosphate counterpart), is the standard nucleoside diphosphate, Adenosine 5-Diphosphate (ADP), where the Adenosine is linked to a pyrophosphate group at the C5 position, and structurally, ATP is a nucleotide composed of 1 molecule of adenine (a nitrogenous base), 1 molecule of ribose (a pentose sugar), and 3 phosphate groups linked by phosphodiester bonds, the latter two phosphate groups of ATP are connected via high-energy phosphate bonds, which are crucial for its energy storage, ADP is a key molecule in cellular energy metabolism, converting to ATP through oxidative or substrate-level phosphorylation, functionally, ATP is the energy currency of cells, primarily responsible for storing and transferring energy required for cellular metabolism, cells synthesize ATP through processes such as cellular respiration and photosynthesis; when the high-energy phosphate bonds of ATP hydrolyze, a large amount of energy is released, which can be used for life activities like active transport, substance synthesis, and muscle contraction, additionally, ATP acts as a coenzyme in nucleic acid synthesis and signal transduction, in real-world medicine, the compound is critically important in hematology as a major signaling molecule for platelet activation and aggregation, consequently, drugs like P2Y12 receptor antagonists (e.g., clopidogrel) function by blocking ADPs action to exert anti-thrombotic effects, the compound is widely used in laboratories to study platelet function and energy metabolism, as mentioned in the document, ATP can bind specifically to certain RNAs (e.g., ATP-54-1, ATP-40-1), with its adenosine moiety (adenine ribose) being the key for binding; the triphosphate groups tend to stay away from RNA due to negative charge repulsion, moreover, both ATP and AMP can effectively elute specific RNAs bound to ATP-agarose columns.
